NRSV.copyright: New Revised Standard Version Bible, copyright © 1989 National Council of the Churches of Christ in the United States of America. Used by permission. All rights reserved worldwide.

text.NRSV: There was once a man in the land of Uz whose name was Job. That man was blameless and upright, one who feared God and turned away from evil.
text.Rotherham: A man, there was—in the land of Uz, Job, his name,—and that man was blameless and upright, and one who revered God, and avoided evil.
text.KJV: There was a man in the land of Uz, whose name was Job; and that man was perfect and upright, and one that feared God, and eschewed evil.
text.NABRE: In the land of Uz there was a blameless and upright man named Job, who feared God and avoided evil.
text.DRB: There was a man in the Land of Hus, named Job, and that man was simple, and right, and fearing God, and departing from evil.

text.NRSV: There were born to him seven sons and three daughters.
text.Rotherham: And there were born unto him seven sons and three daughters.
text.KJV: And there were born unto him seven sons and three daughters.
text.NABRE: Seven sons and three daughters were born to him;
text.DRB: And there were born to him seven sons and three daughters.

text.NRSV: He had seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels, five hundred yoke of oxen, five hundred donkeys, and very many servants; so that this man was the greatest of all the people of the east.
text.Rotherham: And his substance was—seven thousand sheep, and three thousand camels, and five hundred yoke of oxen, and five hundred she-asses, and a body of servants exceeding large,—thus was that man the greatest of all the sons of the East.
text.KJV: His substance also was seven thousand sheep, and three thousand camels, and five hundred yoke of oxen, and five hundred she asses, and a very great household; so that this man was the greatest of all the men of the east.
text.NABRE: and he had seven thousand sheep, three thousand camels, five hundred yoke of oxen, five hundred she-donkeys, and a very large household, so that he was greater than anyone in the East.
text.DRB: And his possession was seven thousand sheep, & three thousand camels, also five hundred yoke of oxen, and five hundred she-asses, and a family exceeding great: and that man was great among all them of the East.

text.NRSV: His sons used to go and hold a feast in one another’s houses in turn; and they would send and invite their three sisters to eat and drink with them.
text.Rotherham: Now his sons were wont to go, and make a banquet, at the house of each one upon his day,—and to send and call their three sisters, to eat and to drink with them.
text.KJV: And his sons went and feasted in their houses, every one his day; and sent and called for their three sisters to eat and to drink with them.
text.NABRE: His sons used to take turns giving feasts, sending invitations to their three sisters to eat and drink with them.
text.DRB: And his sons went, and made a feast by houses, every one in his day. And sending they called their three sisters to eat & drink with them.

text.NRSV: And when the feast days had run their course, Job would send and sanctify them, and he would rise early in the morning and offer burnt offerings according to the number of them all; for Job said, “It may be that my children have sinned, and cursed God in their hearts.” This is what Job always did.
text.Rotherham: And so it was, when the days of the banquet came round, that Job sent and hallowed them, and rising early in the morning offered ascending-sacrifices according to the number of them all; for Job said, Peradventure my sons have sinned, and have cursed God in their hearts. Thus and thus, was Job wont to do all the days.
text.KJV: And it was so, when the days of their feasting were gone about, that Job sent and sanctified them, and rose up early in the morning, and offered burnt offerings according to the number of them all: for Job said, It may be that my sons have sinned, and cursed God in their hearts. Thus did Job continually.
text.NABRE: And when each feast had run its course, Job would send for them and sanctify them, rising early and offering sacrifices for every one of them. For Job said, “It may be that my children have sinned and cursed God in their hearts.” Job did this habitually.
text.DRB: And when the days of feasting had passed about in course, Job sent to them, and sanctified them: and rising up early offered holocausts for every one. For he said: Lest perhaps my sons have sinned, and have blessed God in their hearts. So did Job all the days.

text.NRSV: One day the heavenly beings came to present themselves before the Lord, and Satan also came among them.
text.Rotherham: Now there came a certain day, when the sons of God entered in to present themselves unto Yahweh,—so the accuser also entered, in their midst.
text.KJV: Now there was a day when the sons of God came to present themselves before the Lord, and Satan came also among them.
text.NABRE: The Interview Between the Lord and the Satan. One day, when the sons of God came to present themselves before the Lord, the satan also came among them.
text.DRB: But on a certain day when the sons of God were come to assist before our Lord, Satan also was present among them.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Satan, “Where have you come from?” Satan answered the Lord, “From going to and fro on the earth, and from walking up and down on it.”
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto the accuser, Whence comest thou? And the accuser answered Yahweh, and said, From going to and fro in the earth, and wandering about therein.
text.KJV: And the Lord said unto Satan, Whence comest thou? Then Satan answered the Lord, and said, From going to and fro in the earth, and from walking up and down in it.
text.NABRE: The Lord said to the satan, “Where have you been?” Then the satan answered the Lord and said, “Roaming the earth and patrolling it.”
text.DRB: To whom our Lord said: From whence comest thou? Who answering, said: I have gone round about the earth, & walked through it.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Satan, “Have you considered my servant Job? There is no one like him on the earth, a blameless and upright man who fears God and turns away from evil.”
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto the accuser, Hast thou applied thy heart unto my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth, a man blameless and upright, one revering God and avoiding evil?
text.KJV: And the Lord said unto Satan, Hast thou considered my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth, a perfect and an upright man, one that feareth God, and escheweth evil?
text.NABRE: The Lord said to the satan, “Have you noticed my servant Job? There is no one on earth like him, blameless and upright, fearing God and avoiding evil.”
text.DRB: And our Lord said to him: Hast thou considered my servant Job, that there is not the like to him in the earth, a man simple, and right, and fearing God, and departing from evil?

text.NRSV: Then Satan answered the Lord, “Does Job fear God for nothing?
text.Rotherham: And the accuser answered Yahweh, and said, Is it, for nought, that Job revereth God?
text.KJV: Then Satan answered the Lord, and said, Doth Job fear God for nought?
text.NABRE: The satan answered the Lord and said, “Is it for nothing that Job is God-fearing?
text.DRB: To whom Satan answering, said: Why, doth Job fear God in vain?

text.NRSV: Have you not put a fence around him and his house and all that he has, on every side? You have blessed the work of his hands, and his possessions have increased in the land.
text.Rotherham: Hast not, thou thyself, made a hedge about him, and about his house, and about all that he hath, on every side? The work of his hands, thou hast blessed, and, his substance, hath broken forth in the land.
text.KJV: Hast not thou made an hedge about him, and about his house, and about all that he hath on every side? thou hast blessed the work of his hands, and his substance is increased in the land.
text.NABRE: Have you not surrounded him and his family and all that he has with your protection? You have blessed the work of his hands, and his livestock are spread over the land.
text.DRB: hast not thou fenced him, and his house, and all his substance round about, blessed the works of his hands, and his possession hath increased on the earth?

text.NRSV: But stretch out your hand now, and touch all that he has, and he will curse you to your face.”
text.Rotherham: But, in very deed, put forth, I pray thee, thy hand, and smite all that he hath,—verily, unto thy face, will he curse thee.
text.KJV: But put forth thine hand now, and touch all that he hath, and he will curse thee to thy face.
text.NABRE: But now put forth your hand and touch all that he has, and surely he will curse you to your face.”
text.DRB: But stretch forth thy hand a little, and touch all things that he possesseth, unless he bless thee in the face.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Satan, “Very well, all that he has is in your power; only do not stretch out your hand against him!” So Satan went out from the presence of the Lord.
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto the accuser, Lo! all that he hath, is in thy hand, only, against himself, do not put forth thy hand. So the accuser went forth from the presence of Yahweh.
text.KJV: And the Lord said unto Satan, Behold, all that he hath is in thy power; only upon himself put not forth thine hand. So Satan went forth from the presence of the Lord.
text.NABRE: The Lord said to the satan, “Very well, all that he has is in your power; only do not lay a hand on him.” So the satan went forth from the presence of the Lord.
text.DRB: Our Lord therefore said to Satan: behold, all things that he hath, are in thy hand, only upon him extend not thy hand. And Satan went forth from the face of our Lord.

text.NRSV: One day when his sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in the eldest brother’s house,
text.Rotherham: And there came a certain day,—when his sons and his daughters were eating, and drinking wine, in the house of their eldest brother.
text.KJV: And there was a day when his sons and his daughters were eating and drinking wine in their eldest brother’s house:
text.NABRE: The First Trial. One day, while his sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in the house of their eldest brother,
text.DRB: And when upon a certain day his sons and daughters did eat, and drink wine in the house of their eldest brother,

text.NRSV: a messenger came to Job and said, “The oxen were plowing and the donkeys were feeding beside them,
text.Rotherham: And, a messenger, came in unto Job, and said,—The oxen, were plowing, and, the asses, feeding beside them;
text.KJV: And there came a messenger unto Job, and said, The oxen were plowing, and the asses feeding beside them:
text.NABRE: a messenger came to Job and said, “The oxen were plowing and the donkeys grazing beside them,
text.DRB: there came a messenger to Job, which said: The oxen ploughed, and the she-asses fed beside them,

text.NRSV: and the Sabeans fell on them and carried them off, and killed the servants with the edge of the sword; I alone have escaped to tell you.”
text.Rotherham: when the Sabeans swooped down, and took them, and, the young men, smote they with the edge of the sword,—and escaped am, only I alone, too tell thee.
text.KJV: And the Sabeans fell upon them, and took them away; yea, they have slain the servants with the edge of the sword; and I only am escaped alone to tell thee.
text.NABRE: and the Sabeans carried them off in a raid. They put the servants to the sword, and I alone have escaped to tell you.”
text.DRB: and the Sabeians came in violently, and have taken all things, and have strucken the servants with the sword, and I only have escaped to tell thee.

text.NRSV: While he was still speaking, another came and said, “The fire of God fell from heaven and burned up the sheep and the servants, and consumed them; I alone have escaped to tell you.”
text.Rotherham: Yet was this one speaking, when, another, came in and said,—A fire of God, fell out of the heavens, and burned up the sheep and the young men, and consumed them; and escaped am, only I alone, to tell thee.
text.KJV: While he was yet speaking, there came also another, and said, The fire of God is fallen from heaven, and hath burned up the sheep, and the servants, and consumed them; and I only am escaped alone to tell thee.
text.NABRE: He was still speaking when another came and said, “God’s fire has fallen from heaven and struck the sheep and the servants and consumed them; I alone have escaped to tell you.”
text.DRB: And when he yet spake, another came, and said: The fire of God fell from heaven, and striking the sheep and the servants hath consumed them, and I only have escaped to tell thee.

text.NRSV: While he was still speaking, another came and said, “The Chaldeans formed three columns, made a raid on the camels and carried them off, and killed the servants with the edge of the sword; I alone have escaped to tell you.”
text.Rotherham: Yet was this one speaking, when, another, came in and said, The Chaldeans, appointed three chiefs, and spread out against the camels, and took them, and, the young men, smote they with the edge of the sword; and escaped am, only I alone, to tell thee.
text.KJV: While he was yet speaking, there came also another, and said, The Chaldeans made out three bands, and fell upon the camels, and have carried them away, yea, and slain the servants with the edge of the sword; and I only am escaped alone to tell thee.
text.NABRE: He was still speaking when another came and said, “The Chaldeans formed three columns, seized the camels, carried them off, and put the servants to the sword; I alone have escaped to tell you.”
text.DRB: But whilst he also was yet speaking, there came another and said: The Chaldees made three troops, and have invaded the camels, and taken them, moreover the servants also they have strucken with the sword, and I alone am fled to tell thee.

text.NRSV: While he was still speaking, another came and said, “Your sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in their eldest brother’s house,
text.Rotherham: Yet was this one speaking, when, another, came in and said,—Thy sons and thy daughters, were eating, and drinking wine, in the house of their eldest brother;
text.KJV: While he was yet speaking, there came also another, and said, Thy sons and thy daughters were eating and drinking wine in their eldest brother’s house:
text.NABRE: He was still speaking when another came and said, “Your sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in the house of their eldest brother,
text.DRB: He yet spake, and behold another came in, and said: Thy sons and daughters eating and drinking wine in the house of their eldest brother,

text.NRSV: and suddenly a great wind came across the desert, struck the four corners of the house, and it fell on the young people, and they are dead; I alone have escaped to tell you.”
text.Rotherham: when lo! a great wind, came from over the desert, and smote the four corners of the house, and it fell upon the young men, and they died,—and escaped am, only I alone, to tell thee.
text.KJV: And, behold, there came a great wind from the wilderness, and smote the four corners of the house, and it fell upon the young men, and they are dead; and I only am escaped alone to tell thee.
text.NABRE: and suddenly a great wind came from across the desert and smashed the four corners of the house. It fell upon the young people and they are dead; I alone have escaped to tell you.”
text.DRB: suddenly a vehement wind came violently from the country of the desert, and shook the four corners of the house, which falling oppressed thy children and they are dead, and I alone have escaped to tell thee.

text.NRSV: Then Job arose, tore his robe, shaved his head, and fell on the ground and worshiped.
text.Rotherham: Then Job arose, and rent his robe, and shaved his head, and fell to the earth and worshipped;
text.KJV: Then Job arose, and rent his mantle, and shaved his head, and fell down upon the ground, and worshipped,
text.NABRE: Job’s Reaction. Then Job arose and tore his cloak and cut off his hair. He fell to the ground and worshiped.
text.DRB: Then Job rose up, and rent his garments, & with polled head falling on the ground, adored,

text.NRSV: He said, “Naked I came from my mother’s womb, and naked shall I return; the Lord gave, and the Lord has taken away; blessed be the name of the Lord.”
text.Rotherham: and said—Naked came I forth from the womb of my mother, and naked must I return thither, Yahweh, gave, and, Yahweh, hath taken away,—The name of Yahweh be blessed!
text.KJV: And said, Naked came I out of my mother’s womb, and naked shall I return thither: the Lord gave, and the Lord hath taken away; blessed be the name of the Lord.
text.NABRE: He said, “Naked I came forth from my mother’s womb, and naked shall I go back there. The Lord gave and the Lord has taken away; blessed be the name of the Lord!”
text.DRB: and said: Naked came I out of my mother's womb, and naked shall I return thither: Our Lord gave, and our Lord hath taken away: as it hath pleased our Lord, so is it done: the name of our Lord be blessed.

text.NRSV: In all this Job did not sin or charge God with wrongdoing.
text.Rotherham: In all this, Job sinned not, nor imputed folly unto God.
text.KJV: In all this Job sinned not, nor charged God foolishly.
text.NABRE: In all this Job did not sin, nor did he charge God with wrong.
text.DRB: In all these things Job sinned not with his lips, neither spake he any foolish thing against God.

text.NRSV: One day the heavenly beings came to present themselves before the Lord, and Satan also came among them to present himself before the Lord.
text.Rotherham: And there came a certain day when the sons of God entered in, to present themselves unto Yahweh—so the accuser also entered in their midst, to present himself unto Yahweh.
text.KJV: Again there was a day when the sons of God came to present themselves before the Lord, and Satan came also among them to present himself before the Lord.
text.NABRE: One day, when the sons of God came to present themselves before the Lord, the satan also came with them.
text.DRB: And it came to pass when on a certain day the sons of God were come, and stood before our Lord, and Satan came among them, and stood in his sight,

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Satan, “Where have you come from?” Satan answered the Lord, “From going to and fro on the earth, and from walking up and down on it.”
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto the accuser, Whence comest thou? And the accuser answered Yahweh, and said, From going to and fro in the earth, and from wandering about therein.
text.KJV: And the Lord said unto Satan, From whence comest thou? And Satan answered the Lord, and said, From going to and fro in the earth, and from walking up and down in it.
text.NABRE: The Lord said to the satan, “Where have you been?” Then the satan answered the Lord and said, “Roaming the earth and patrolling it.”
text.DRB: that our Lord said to Satan: From whence comest thou? Who answering said: I have gone round about the earth, and walked through it.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Satan, “Have you considered my servant Job? There is no one like him on the earth, a blameless and upright man who fears God and turns away from evil. He still persists in his integrity, although you incited me against him, to destroy him for no reason.”
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto the accuser, Hast thou applied thy heart unto my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth, a man blameless and upright, one who revereth God, and avoideth evil; and still he is holding fast his integrity, although thou movedst me against him, to swallow him up without cause.
text.KJV: And the Lord said unto Satan, Hast thou considered my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth, a perfect and an upright man, one that feareth God, and escheweth evil? and still he holdeth fast his integrity, although thou movedst me against him, to destroy him without cause.
text.NABRE: The Lord said to the satan, “Have you noticed my servant Job? There is no one on earth like him, blameless and upright, fearing God and avoiding evil. He still holds fast to his innocence although you incited me against him to ruin him for nothing.”
text.DRB: And our Lord said to Satan: Hast thou considered my servant Job, that there is not the like to him in the earth, a man simple, and right, and fearing God, and departing from evil, and yet retaining innocency? But thou hast moved me against him, that I should afflict him in vain.

text.NRSV: Then Satan answered the Lord, “Skin for skin! All that people have they will give to save their lives.
text.Rotherham: Then the accuser answered Yahweh, and said, Skin for skin, and so, all that a man hath, will he give for his life.
text.KJV: And Satan answered the Lord, and said, Skin for skin, yea, all that a man hath will he give for his life.
text.NABRE: The satan answered the Lord and said, “Skin for skin! All that a man has he will give for his life.
text.DRB: To whom Satan answering, said: Skin for skin, and all things that a man hath, he will give for his life:

text.NRSV: But stretch out your hand now and touch his bone and his flesh, and he will curse you to your face.”
text.Rotherham: In very deed, put forth, I pray thee thy hand, and smite unto his bone, and unto his flesh,—verily, unto thy face, will he curse thee.
text.KJV: But put forth thine hand now, and touch his bone and his flesh, and he will curse thee to thy face.
text.NABRE: But put forth your hand and touch his bone and his flesh. Then surely he will curse you to your face.”
text.DRB: otherwise put thy hand, and touch his bone and flesh, and then shalt thou see that he will bless thee in the face.

text.NRSV: The Lord said to Satan, “Very well, he is in your power; only spare his life.”
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh said unto the accuser, Behold him! in thy hand,—only, his life, preserve thou!
text.KJV: And the Lord said unto Satan, Behold, he is in thine hand; but save his life.
text.NABRE: And the Lord said to the satan, “He is in your power; only spare his life.”
text.DRB: Our Lord therefore said to Satan: Behold he is in thy hand, but yet save his life.

text.NRSV: So Satan went out from the presence of the Lord, and inflicted loathsome sores on Job from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head.
text.Rotherham: So the accuser went forth from the presence of Yahweh,—and smote Job with a sore boil, from the sole of his foot, unto his crown.
text.KJV: So went Satan forth from the presence of the Lord, and smote Job with sore boils from the sole of his foot unto his crown.
text.NABRE: The Second Trial. So the satan went forth from the presence of the Lord and struck Job with severe boils from the soles of his feet to the crown of his head.
text.DRB: Satan therefore going out from the face of our Lord, struck Job with a very sore boil, from the sole of the foot even to the top of his head:

text.NRSV: Job took a potsherd with which to scrape himself, and sat among the ashes.
text.Rotherham: And he took him a potsherd, to scrape himself therewith; he being seated in the midst of ashes.
text.KJV: And he took him a potsherd to scrape himself withal; and he sat down among the ashes.
text.NABRE: Job’s Reaction. He took a potsherd to scrape himself, as he sat among the ashes.
text.DRB: who with a shell scraped the corruption, sitting on a dunghill.

text.NRSV: Then his wife said to him, “Do you still persist in your integrity? Curse God, and die.”
text.Rotherham: Then said his wife unto him, Art thou still holding fast thine integrity? Curse God, and die!
text.KJV: Then said his wife unto him, Dost thou still retain thine integrity? curse God, and die.
text.NABRE: Then his wife said to him, “Are you still holding to your innocence? Curse God and die!”
text.DRB: And his wife said to him: Doest thou yet continue in thy simplicity? Bless God and die.

text.NRSV: But he said to her, “You speak as any foolish woman would speak. Shall we receive the good at the hand of God, and not receive the bad?” In all this Job did not sin with his lips.
text.Rotherham: And he said unto her, As one of the base women speaketh, speakest thou? Blessing, shall we accept from God, and, misfortune, shall we not accept? In all this, Job sinned not with his lips.
text.KJV: But he said unto her, Thou speakest as one of the foolish women speaketh. What? shall we receive good at the hand of God, and shall we not receive evil? In all this did not Job sin with his lips.
text.NABRE: But he said to her, “You speak as foolish women do. We accept good things from God; should we not accept evil?” Through all this, Job did not sin in what he said.
text.DRB: Who said to her: Thou hast spoken like one of the foolish women: if we have received good things of the hand of God, evil things why should we not receive? In all these things Job sinned not with his lips.

text.NRSV: Now when Job’s three friends heard of all these troubles that had come upon him, each of them set out from his home—Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite. They met together to go and console and comfort him.
text.Rotherham: Now when the three friends of Job heard of all this misfortune which had befallen him,—they came, every man from his own place, Eliphaz the Temanite, and Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite,—for they had by appointment met together to come to shew sympathy with him, and to comfort him.
text.KJV: Now when Job’s three friends heard of all this evil that was come upon him, they came every one from his own place; Eliphaz the Temanite, and Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite: for they had made an appointment together to come to mourn with him and to comfort him.
text.NABRE: Job’s Three Friends. Now when three of Job’s friends heard of all the misfortune that had come upon him, they set out each one from his own place: Eliphaz from Teman, Bildad from Shuh, and Zophar from Naamath. They met and journeyed together to give him sympathy and comfort.
text.DRB: Therefore Job's three friends hearing all the evil, that had chanced to him, came every one out of their place, Eliphaz a Themanite, and Baldad a Suhite, and Sophar a Naamathite. For they had appointed, that coming together they would visit him, and comfort him.

text.NRSV: When they saw him from a distance, they did not recognize him; and they raised their voices and wept aloud; they tore their robes and threw dust in the air upon their heads.
text.Rotherham: And, when they lifted up their eyes afar off, and knew him not, they lifted up their voice, and wept—and rent, every one his robe, and sprinkled dust upon their heads, toward the heavens.
text.KJV: And when they lifted up their eyes afar off, and knew him not, they lifted up their voice, and wept; and they rent every one his mantle, and sprinkled dust upon their heads toward heaven.
text.NABRE: But when, at a distance, they lifted up their eyes and did not recognize him, they began to weep aloud; they tore their cloaks and threw dust into the air over their heads.
text.DRB: And when they had lifted up their eyes a far off, they knew him not, and crying out they wept, and renting their cloths sprinkled dust over their head toward heaven.

text.NRSV: They sat with him on the ground seven days and seven nights, and no one spoke a word to him, for they saw that his suffering was very great.
text.Rotherham: And they sat with him upon the ground, seven days and seven nights,—and none was speaking unto him a word, for they saw that, exceeding great, was the stinging pain.
text.KJV: So they sat down with him upon the ground seven days and seven nights, and none spake a word unto him: for they saw that his grief was very great.
text.NABRE: Then they sat down upon the ground with him seven days and seven nights, but none of them spoke a word to him; for they saw how great was his suffering.
text.DRB: And they sat with him on the ground seven days and seven nights, and no man spake to him a word: for they saw the pain to be vehement.

text.NRSV: After this Job opened his mouth and cursed the day of his birth.
text.Rotherham: After this, opened Job his mouth, and cursed his day.
text.KJV: After this opened Job his mouth, and cursed his day.
text.NABRE: After this, Job opened his mouth and cursed his day.
text.DRB: After these things Job opened his mouth, and cursed his day,

text.NRSV: Job said:
text.Rotherham: So then Job began, and said:
text.KJV: And Job spake, and said,
text.NABRE: Job spoke out and said:
text.DRB: and spake.

text.NRSV: “Let the day perish in which I was born, and the night that said, ‘A man-child is conceived.’
text.Rotherham: Perish, the day wherein I was born, and the night it was said, Lo! a manchild!
text.KJV: Let the day perish wherein I was born, and the night in which it was said, There is a man child conceived.
text.NABRE: Perish the day on which I was born, the night when they said, “The child is a boy!”
text.DRB: Perish may the day wherein I was born, & the night wherein it was said: A man is conceived.

text.NRSV: Let that day be darkness! May God above not seek it, or light shine on it.
text.Rotherham: That day, be it darkness,—Let not God enquire after it from above, May there shine upon it no clear beam:
text.KJV: Let that day be darkness; let not God regard it from above, neither let the light shine upon it.
text.NABRE: May that day be darkness: may God above not care for it, may light not shine upon it!
text.DRB: Be that day turned into darkness, God require it not from above, and let it not be lightened with light.

text.NRSV: Let gloom and deep darkness claim it. Let clouds settle upon it; let the blackness of the day terrify it.
text.Rotherham: Let darkness and death-shade buy it back, May there settle down upon it a cloud, Let a day’s dark eclipse cause it terror:
text.KJV: Let darkness and the shadow of death stain it; let a cloud dwell upon it; let the blackness of the day terrify it.
text.NABRE: May darkness and gloom claim it, clouds settle upon it, blackness of day affright it!
text.DRB: Let darkness, and the shadow of death obscure it, let a mist possess it, and be it wrapped in bitterness.

text.NRSV: That night—let thick darkness seize it! Let it not rejoice among the days of the year; let it not come into the number of the months.
text.Rotherham: That night, darkness take it,—May it not rejoice among the days of the year, Into the number of months, let it not enter.
text.KJV: As for that night, let darkness seize upon it; let it not be joined unto the days of the year, let it not come into the number of the months.
text.NABRE: May obscurity seize that night; may it not be counted among the days of the year, nor enter into the number of the months!
text.DRB: Adarksome hurl wind possess that night, be it not counted in the days of the year, nor numbered in the months.

text.NRSV: Yes, let that night be barren; let no joyful voice be heard in it.
text.Rotherham: Lo! that night, be it barren, Let no joyous shouting enter therein:
text.KJV: Lo, let that night be solitary, let no joyful voice come therein.
text.NABRE: May that night be barren; let no joyful outcry greet it!
text.DRB: Be that night solitary, nor praise-worthy.

text.NRSV: Let those curse it who curse the Sea, those who are skilled to rouse up Leviathan.
text.Rotherham: Let day-cursers denounce it, Those skilled in rousing the dragon of the sky:
text.KJV: Let them curse it that curse the day, who are ready to raise up their mourning.
text.NABRE: Let them curse it who curse the Sea, those skilled at disturbing Leviathan!
text.DRB: Let them curse it which curse the day, which are ready to raise up Leviathan.

text.NRSV: Let the stars of its dawn be dark; let it hope for light, but have none; may it not see the eyelids of the morning—
text.Rotherham: Darkened be the stars of its twilight,—Let it wait for light, and there be none, neither let it see the eyelashes of the dawn:
text.KJV: Let the stars of the twilight thereof be dark; let it look for light, but have none; neither let it see the dawning of the day:
text.NABRE: May the stars of its twilight be darkened; may it look for daylight, but have none, nor gaze on the eyes of the dawn,
text.DRB: Let the stars be darkened with the mist thereof: let it expect light and let it not see, neither the rising of the appearing morning.

text.NRSV: because it did not shut the doors of my mother’s womb, and hide trouble from my eyes.
text.Rotherham: Because it closed not the doors of the womb wherein I was, and so hid trouble from mine eyes.
text.KJV: Because it shut not up the doors of my mother’s womb, nor hid sorrow from mine eyes.
text.NABRE: Because it did not keep shut the doors of the womb to shield my eyes from trouble!
text.DRB: Because it shut not up the doors of the womb that bare me, nor took away evils from mine eyes.

text.NRSV: “Why did I not die at birth, come forth from the womb and expire?
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, in the womb, did I not die? From the womb, come forth and cease to breathe?
text.KJV: Why died I not from the womb? why did I not give up the ghost when I came out of the belly?
text.NABRE: Why did I not die at birth, come forth from the womb and expire?
text.DRB: Why died I not in the matrice, perished not forthwith being come forth of the womb?

text.NRSV: Why were there knees to receive me, or breasts for me to suck?
text.Rotherham: For what reason, were there prepared for me—knees? and why—breasts, that I might suck?
text.KJV: Why did the knees prevent me? or why the breasts that I should suck?
text.NABRE: Why did knees receive me, or breasts nurse me?
text.DRB: Why received upon the knees: why nursed with the breasts?

text.NRSV: Now I would be lying down and quiet; I would be asleep; then I would be at rest
text.Rotherham: Surely, at once, had I lain down, and been quiet, I had fallen asleep, then, had I been at rest:
text.KJV: For now should I have lain still and been quiet, I should have slept: then had I been at rest,
text.NABRE: For then I should have lain down and been tranquil; had I slept, I should then have been at rest
text.DRB: For now sleeping I should be quiet, and should rest in my sleep:

text.NRSV: with kings and counselors of the earth who rebuild ruins for themselves,
text.Rotherham: With kings, and counselors of the earth, who had built them pyramids:
text.KJV: With kings and counsellors of the earth, which built desolate places for themselves;
text.NABRE: With kings and counselors of the earth who rebuilt what were ruins
text.DRB: With kings and consuls of the earth, which build themselves solitary places:

text.NRSV: or with princes who have gold, who fill their houses with silver.
text.Rotherham: Or with rulers possessing, gold,—Who had filled their houses with silver:
text.KJV: Or with princes that had gold, who filled their houses with silver:
text.NABRE: Or with princes who had gold and filled their houses with silver.
text.DRB: Or with Princes, that posses gold, and replenish their houses with silver:

text.NRSV: Or why was I not buried like a stillborn child, like an infant that never sees the light?
text.Rotherham: Or that, like an untimely birth hidden away, I had not come into being, like infants that never saw light:
text.KJV: Or as an hidden untimely birth I had not been; as infants which never saw light.
text.NABRE: Or why was I not buried away like a stillborn child, like babies that have never seen the light?
text.DRB: Or as a thing untimely born that is hid I should not be, or as they that being conceived have not seen the light.

text.NRSV: There the wicked cease from troubling, and there the weary are at rest.
text.Rotherham: There, the lawless, cease from raging, and there the toil-worn are at rest:
text.KJV: There the wicked cease from troubling; and there the weary be at rest.
text.NABRE: There the wicked cease from troubling, there the weary are at rest.
text.DRB: There the impious have ceased from tumult, & there the wearied with strength have rested.

text.NRSV: There the prisoners are at ease together; they do not hear the voice of the taskmaster.
text.Rotherham: At once are prisoners at peace, they hear not the voice of a driver:
text.KJV: There the prisoners rest together; they hear not the voice of the oppressor.
text.NABRE: The captives are at ease together, and hear no overseer’s voice.
text.DRB: And they sometime bound together without grief, have not heard the voice of the exactor.

text.NRSV: The small and the great are there, and the slaves are free from their masters.
text.Rotherham: Small and great, there, they are, and, the slave, is free from his master.
text.KJV: The small and great are there; and the servant is free from his master.
text.NABRE: Small and great are there; the servant is free from the master.
text.DRB: Little and great are there, and the servant free from his master.

text.NRSV: “Why is light given to one in misery, and life to the bitter in soul,
text.Rotherham: Wherefore give, to the wretched, light? Or, life, to the embittered in soul?—
text.KJV: Wherefore is light given to him that is in misery, and life unto the bitter in soul;
text.NABRE: Why is light given to the toilers, life to the bitter in spirit?
text.DRB: Why is there light given to a miserable man, and life to them, that are in bitterness of soul,

text.NRSV: who long for death, but it does not come, and dig for it more than for hidden treasures;
text.Rotherham: Who long for death, and it is not, And have digged for it, beyond hid treasures:
text.KJV: Which long for death, but it cometh not; and dig for it more than for hid treasures;
text.NABRE: They wait for death and it does not come; they search for it more than for hidden treasures.
text.DRB: that expect death, and it cometh not, as they that dig up treasure,

text.NRSV: who rejoice exceedingly, and are glad when they find the grave?
text.Rotherham: Who rejoice unto exultation, Are glad, when they can find the grave:
text.KJV: Which rejoice exceedingly, and are glad, when they can find the grave?
text.NABRE: They rejoice in it exultingly, and are glad when they find the grave:
text.DRB: and they rejoice exceedingly when they have found the grave?

text.NRSV: Why is light given to one who cannot see the way, whom God has fenced in?
text.Rotherham: To a man, whose way is concealed, And GOD hath straitly enclosed him?
text.KJV: Why is light given to a man whose way is hid, and whom God hath hedged in?
text.NABRE: A man whose path is hidden from him, one whom God has hemmed in!
text.DRB: To a man whose life is hid, and God hath compassed him with darkness?

text.NRSV: For my sighing comes like my food, and my groanings are poured out like water.
text.Rotherham: For, in the face of my food, my sighing, cometh in, and, poured out like the water, are my groans:
text.KJV: For my sighing cometh before I eat, and my roarings are poured out like the waters.
text.NABRE: For to me sighing comes more readily than food; my groans well forth like water.
text.DRB: Before I eat I sigh: and as it were overflowing waters, so my roaring:

text.NRSV: Truly the thing that I fear comes upon me, and what I dread befalls me.
text.Rotherham: For, a dread, I dreaded, and it hath come upon me, and, that from which I shrank, hath overtaken me.
text.KJV: For the thing which I greatly feared is come upon me, and that which I was afraid of is come unto me.
text.NABRE: For what I feared overtakes me; what I dreaded comes upon me.
text.DRB: Because the fear which I feared, hath chanced to me: and that which I was afraid of, hath happened.

text.NRSV: I am not at ease, nor am I quiet; I have no rest; but trouble comes.”
text.Rotherham: I was not careless, nor was I secure, nor had I settled down,—when there came—consternation!
text.KJV: I was not in safety, neither had I rest, neither was I quiet; yet trouble came.
text.NABRE: I have no peace nor ease; I have no rest, for trouble has come!
text.DRB: Have I not dissembled? Have I not kept silence? Have I not been at ease? And indignation is come upon me.

text.NRSV: Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Eliphaz the Temanite, and said:—
text.KJV: Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered and said,
text.NABRE: Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered and said:
text.DRB: But Eliphaz the Themanite answering, said:

text.NRSV: “If one ventures a word with you, will you be offended? But who can keep from speaking?
text.Rotherham: If one attempt a word unto thee, wilt thou be impatient? But, to restrain speech, who, can endure?
text.KJV: If we assay to commune with thee, wilt thou be grieved? but who can withhold himself from speaking?
text.NABRE: If someone attempts a word with you, would you mind? How can anyone refrain from speaking?
text.DRB: If we shall begin to speak to thee, perhaps thou wilt take it grievously, but the word conceived who can hold?

text.NRSV: See, you have instructed many; you have strengthened the weak hands.
text.Rotherham: Lo! thou hast admonished many, and, slack hands, hast thou been wont to uphold:
text.KJV: Behold, thou hast instructed many, and thou hast strengthened the weak hands.
text.NABRE: Look, you have instructed many, and made firm their feeble hands.
text.DRB: Behold thou hast taught many, and weary hands thou hast strengthened.

text.NRSV: Your words have supported those who were stumbling, and you have made firm the feeble knees.
text.Rotherham: Him that was stumbling, have thy words raised up, and, sinking knees, hast thou strengthened.
text.KJV: Thy words have upholden him that was falling, and thou hast strengthened the feeble knees.
text.NABRE: Your words have upheld the stumbler; you have strengthened faltering knees.
text.DRB: Them that wavered thy words have confirmed, and trembling knees thou hast strengthened:

text.NRSV: But now it has come to you, and you are impatient; it touches you, and you are dismayed.
text.Rotherham: But, now, it cometh upon thee, and thou despairest, It smiteth even thee, and thou art dismayed.
text.KJV: But now it is come upon thee, and thou faintest; it toucheth thee, and thou art troubled.
text.NABRE: But now that it comes to you, you are impatient; when it touches you, you are dismayed.
text.DRB: But now a plague is come upon thee, & thou hast fainted: hath touched thee, and thou art troubled.

text.NRSV: Is not your fear of God your confidence, and the integrity of your ways your hope?
text.Rotherham: Is not, thy reverence, thy confidence? And is not, thy hope, the very integrity of thy ways?
text.KJV: Is not this thy fear, thy confidence, thy hope, and the uprightness of thy ways?
text.NABRE: Is not your piety a source of confidence, and your integrity of life your hope?
text.DRB: Where is thy fear, thy strength, thy patience, and the perfection of thy ways?

text.NRSV: “Think now, who that was innocent ever perished? Or where were the upright cut off?
text.Rotherham: Remember, I pray thee, who, being innocent, hath perished, or when, the upright, have been cut off.
text.KJV: Remember, I pray thee, who ever perished, being innocent? or where were the righteous cut off?
text.NABRE: Reflect now, what innocent person perishes? Where are the upright destroyed?
text.DRB: Remember I beseech thee, who ever being innocent hath perished? Or when have the just been destroyed?

text.NRSV: As I have seen, those who plow iniquity and sow trouble reap the same.
text.Rotherham: So far as I have seen, They who plow for iniquity and sow misery, reap the same:
text.KJV: Even as I have seen, they that plow iniquity, and sow wickedness, reap the same.
text.NABRE: As I see it, those who plow mischief and sow trouble will reap them.
text.DRB: Yea rather I have seen them that work iniquity, and sow sorrows, and reap them,

text.NRSV: By the breath of God they perish, and by the blast of his anger they are consumed.
text.Rotherham: By the blast of GOD, they perish, And, by the breath of his nostrils, are they consumed:
text.KJV: By the blast of God they perish, and by the breath of his nostrils are they consumed.
text.NABRE: By the breath of God they perish, and by the blast of his wrath they are consumed.
text.DRB: to have perished by the blast of God, and with the spirit of his wrath to have been consumed.

text.NRSV: The roar of the lion, the voice of the fierce lion, and the teeth of the young lions are broken.
text.Rotherham: [Notwithstanding] the roaring of the lion, and the noise of the howling lion, yet, the teeth of the fierce lions, are broken:
text.KJV: The roaring of the lion, and the voice of the fierce lion, and the teeth of the young lions, are broken.
text.NABRE: Though the lion roars, though the king of beasts cries out, yet the teeth of the young lions are broken;
text.DRB: The roaring of the lion, and the voice of the lioness, and the teeth of the whelps of lions are bruised:

text.NRSV: The strong lion perishes for lack of prey, and the whelps of the lioness are scattered.
text.Rotherham: The strong lion perishing for lack of prey, Even the whelps of the lioness, are scattered.
text.KJV: The old lion perisheth for lack of prey, and the stout lion’s whelps are scattered abroad.
text.NABRE: The old lion perishes for lack of prey, and the cubs of the lioness are scattered.
text.DRB: The tiger hath perished, because he had no prey, and the lions' whelps are destroyed.

text.NRSV: “Now a word came stealing to me, my ear received the whisper of it.
text.Rotherham: But, unto me, something was brought by stealth,—and mine ear caught a whispering of the same:
text.KJV: Now a thing was secretly brought to me, and mine ear received a little thereof.
text.NABRE: A word was stealthily brought to me, my ear caught a whisper of it.
text.DRB: Moreover to me there was spoken a secret word, and as it were by stealth hath mine ear received the veins of the whispering thereof.

text.NRSV: Amid thoughts from visions of the night, when deep sleep falls on mortals,
text.Rotherham: When there were thoughts, from visions of the night,—When deep sleep falleth upon men,
text.KJV: In thoughts from the visions of the night, when deep sleep falleth on men,
text.NABRE: In my thoughts during visions of the night, when deep sleep falls on mortals,
text.DRB: In the horror of a vision by night, when deep sleep is wont to hold men,

text.NRSV: dread came upon me, and trembling, which made all my bones shake.
text.Rotherham: Dread, came upon me, and trembling, The multitude of my bones, it put in dread:
text.KJV: Fear came upon me, and trembling, which made all my bones to shake.
text.NABRE: Fear came upon me, and shuddering, that terrified me to the bone.
text.DRB: fear held me, and trembling, and all my bones were made sore afraid:

text.NRSV: A spirit passed before my face; the hair of my flesh bristled.
text.Rotherham: Then, a spirit, over my face, floated along, The hair of my flesh bristled-up:
text.KJV: Then a spirit passed before my face; the hair of my flesh stood up:
text.NABRE: Then a spirit passed before me, and the hair of my body stood on end.
text.DRB: And when the spirit passed in my presence, the hairs of my flesh stood upright.

text.NRSV: It stood still, but I could not discern its appearance. A form was before my eyes; there was silence, then I heard a voice:
text.Rotherham: It stood still, but I could not distinguish its appearance, I looked, but there was no form before mine eyes,—A whispering voice, I heard:—
text.KJV: It stood still, but I could not discern the form thereof: an image was before mine eyes, there was silence, and I heard a voice, saying,
text.NABRE: It paused, but its likeness I could not recognize; a figure was before my eyes, in silence I heard a voice:
text.DRB: There stood one, whose countenance I knew not, an image before mine eyes, and I heard the voice as it were of a gentle wind:

text.NRSV: ‘Can mortals be righteous before God? Can human beings be pure before their Maker?
text.Rotherham: Shall, mortal man, be more just than GOD? Or a man be more pure than, his Maker?
text.KJV: Shall mortal man be more just than God? shall a man be more pure than his maker?
text.NABRE: “Can anyone be more in the right than God? Can mortals be more blameless than their Maker?
text.DRB: What, shall man be justified in comparison of God, or shall a man be more pure than his maker?

text.NRSV: Even in his servants he puts no trust, and his angels he charges with error;
text.Rotherham: Lo! in his own servants, he trusteth not, and, his own messengers, he chargeth with error:
text.KJV: Behold, he put no trust in his servants; and his angels he charged with folly:
text.NABRE: Look, he puts no trust in his servants, and even with his messengers he finds fault.
text.DRB: Behold they that serve him are not stable, and in his Angels he found wickedness:

text.NRSV: how much more those who live in houses of clay, whose foundation is in the dust, who are crushed like a moth.
text.Rotherham: How much more the dwellers in houses of clay, which, in the dust, have their foundation, which are crushed sooner than a moth:
text.KJV: How much less in them that dwell in houses of clay, whose foundation is in the dust, which are crushed before the moth?
text.NABRE: How much more with those who dwell in houses of clay, whose foundation is in the dust, who are crushed more easily than a moth!
text.DRB: How much more they that inhabit houses of clay, which have an earthly foundation, shall be consumed as it were of the moth?

text.NRSV: Between morning and evening they are destroyed; they perish forever without any regarding it.
text.Rotherham: Betwixt morning and evening, are they broken in pieces, With none to save, they utterly perish:
text.KJV: They are destroyed from morning to evening: they perish for ever without any regarding it.
text.NABRE: Morning or evening they may be shattered; unnoticed, they perish forever.
text.DRB: From morning until evening they shall be cut down: and because none understandeth, they shall perish for ever.

text.NRSV: Their tent-cord is plucked up within them, is it not? They die, and that without wisdom.’
text.Rotherham: Is not their tent-rope within them, torn away? They die, disrobed of wisdom!
text.KJV: Doth not their excellency which is in them go away? they die, even without wisdom.
text.NABRE: The pegs of their tent are plucked up; they die without knowing wisdom.”
text.DRB: And they that shall be left, shall be taken away from them: they shall die, and not in wisdom.

text.NRSV: “Call now; is there anyone who will answer you? To which of the holy ones will you turn?
text.Rotherham: Call, I pray thee—is there one to answer thee? Or, to which of the holy ones, wilt thou turn?
text.KJV: Call now, if there be any that will answer thee; and to which of the saints wilt thou turn?
text.NABRE: Call now! Will anyone respond to you? To which of the holy ones will you turn?
text.DRB: Call therefore if there be that will answer thee, and turn to some of the Saints.

text.NRSV: Surely vexation kills the fool, and passion slays the simple.
text.Rotherham: For, to the foolish man, death is caused by vexation, and, the simple one, is slain by jealousy.
text.KJV: For wrath killeth the foolish man, and envy slayeth the silly one.
text.NABRE: Surely impatience kills the fool and indignation slays the simpleton.
text.DRB: Anger indeed killeth the foolish, and envy slayeth the little one.

text.NRSV: I have seen fools taking root, but suddenly I cursed their dwelling.
text.Rotherham: I, have seen the foolish taking root, and then hath his home decayed, in a moment:
text.KJV: I have seen the foolish taking root: but suddenly I cursed his habitation.
text.NABRE: I have seen a fool spreading his roots, but I cursed his household suddenly:
text.DRB: I have seen a fool with firm root, and I cursed his beauty by and by.

text.NRSV: Their children are far from rather safety, they are crushed in the gate, and there is no one to deliver them.
text.Rotherham: His children are far removed from safety, and they are crushed in the gate, and there is none to deliver:
text.KJV: His children are far from safety, and they are crushed in the gate, neither is there any to deliver them.
text.NABRE: May his children be far from safety; may they be crushed at the gate without a rescuer.
text.DRB: His children shall be made far from salvation, and shall be destroyed in the gate, and there shall be none to deliver.

text.NRSV: The hungry eat their harvest, and they take it even out of the thorns; and the thirsty pant after their wealth.
text.Rotherham: Whose harvest, the hungry, eateth up, and, even out of thorn hedges, he taketh it, and the snare gapeth for their substance.
text.KJV: Whose harvest the hungry eateth up, and taketh it even out of the thorns, and the robber swalloweth up their substance.
text.NABRE: What they have reaped may the hungry eat up, or God take away by blight, or the thirsty swallow their substance.
text.DRB: Whose harvest the hungry shall eat, and the armed shall take him by violence, and the thirsty shall drink his riches.

text.NRSV: For misery does not come from the dust, nor does trouble sprout from the ground;
text.Rotherham: For sorrow, cometh not forth out of the dust,—nor, out of the ground, sprouteth trouble.
text.KJV: Although affliction cometh not forth of the dust, neither doth trouble spring out of the ground;
text.NABRE: For not from dust does mischief come, nor from the soil does trouble sprout.
text.DRB: Nothing in the earth is done without a cause, and out of the ground sorrow shall not rise.

text.NRSV: but mortals are born to trouble as the sparks fly upward.
text.Rotherham: Though, man, to trouble, were born, as, sparks, on high, do soar,
text.KJV: Yet man is born unto trouble, as the sparks fly upward.
text.NABRE: Human beings beget mischief as sparks fly upward.
text.DRB: Man is born to labour, and the bird to flight.

text.NRSV: “As for me, I would seek God, and to God I would commit my cause.
text.Rotherham: Yet indeed, I, would seek unto El, and, unto Elohim, would I set forth any cause:—
text.KJV: I would seek unto God, and unto God would I commit my cause:
text.NABRE: In your place, I would appeal to God, and to God I would state my plea.
text.DRB: For the which thing I will beseech our Lord, and toward God I will set my speech:

text.NRSV: He does great things and unsearchable, marvelous things without number.
text.Rotherham: Who doeth great things, beyond all search,—Wondrous things, till they cannot be recounted;
text.KJV: Which doeth great things and unsearchable; marvellous things without number:
text.NABRE: He does things great and unsearchable, things marvelous and innumerable.
text.DRB: Who doeth great and unsearchable and marvelous things without number:

text.NRSV: He gives rain on the earth and sends waters on the fields;
text.Rotherham: Who giveth rain, upon the face of the earth, and sendeth forth waters, over the face of the open fields;
text.KJV: Who giveth rain upon the earth, and sendeth waters upon the fields:
text.NABRE: He gives rain upon the earth and sends water upon the fields;
text.DRB: Who giveth rain upon the face of the earth, and watereth all things with waters:

text.NRSV: he sets on high those who are lowly, and those who mourn are lifted to safety.
text.Rotherham: Setting the lowly on high, and, mourners, are uplifted to safety;
text.KJV: To set up on high those that be low; that those which mourn may be exalted to safety.
text.NABRE: He sets up the lowly on high, and those who mourn are raised to safety.
text.DRB: Who setteth the humble on high, and them that are in heaviness he comforteth with health:

text.NRSV: He frustrates the devices of the crafty, so that their hands achieve no success.
text.Rotherham: Who doth frustrate the schemes of the crafty, that their hands cannot achieve abiding success;
text.KJV: He disappointeth the devices of the crafty, so that their hands cannot perform their enterprise.
text.NABRE: He frustrates the plans of the cunning, so that their hands achieve no success;
text.DRB: Who dissipateth the cogitations of the malignant, that their hands cannot accomplish that which they began:

text.NRSV: He takes the wise in their own craftiness; and the schemes of the wily are brought to a quick end.
text.Rotherham: Who captureth the wise in their own craftiness, yea the headlong counsel of the crooked:
text.KJV: He taketh the wise in their own craftiness: and the counsel of the froward is carried headlong.
text.NABRE: He catches the wise in their own ruses, and the designs of the crafty are routed.
text.DRB: Who apprehendeth the wise in their subtilty, and dissipateth the counsel of the wicked:

text.NRSV: They meet with darkness in the daytime, and grope at noonday as in the night.
text.Rotherham: By day, they encounter darkness, and, as though it were night, they grope at high noon.
text.KJV: They meet with darkness in the daytime, and grope in the noonday as in the night.
text.NABRE: They meet with darkness in the daytime, at noonday they grope as though it were night.
text.DRB: By day they shall incur darkness, and as it were in the night, so shall they grope at noon days.

text.NRSV: But he saves the needy from the sword of their mouth, from the hand of the mighty.
text.Rotherham: But he saveth from the sword, out of their mouth, and, out of the hand of the strong, the needy.
text.KJV: But he saveth the poor from the sword, from their mouth, and from the hand of the mighty.
text.NABRE: But he saves the poor from the sword of their mouth, from the hand of the mighty.
text.DRB: Moreover he shall save the needy from the sword of their mouth, and the poor from the hand of the violent.

text.NRSV: So the poor have hope, and injustice shuts its mouth.
text.Rotherham: Thus to the poor hath come hope, and, perversity, hath shut her mouth.
text.KJV: So the poor hath hope, and iniquity stoppeth her mouth.
text.NABRE: Thus the needy have hope, and iniquity closes its mouth.
text.DRB: And to the needy there shall be hope, but iniquity shall draw together her mouth.

text.NRSV: “How happy is the one whom God reproves; therefore do not despise the discipline of the Almighty.
text.Rotherham: Lo! how happy is the man whom God correcteth! Therefore, the chastening of the Almighty, do not thou refuse;
text.KJV: Behold, happy is the man whom God correcteth: therefore despise not thou the chastening of the Almighty:
text.NABRE: Happy the one whom God reproves! The Almighty’s discipline do not reject.
text.DRB: Blessed is the man that is corrected of God: refuse not therefore the chastising of our Lord:

text.NRSV: For he wounds, but he binds up; he strikes, but his hands heal.
text.Rotherham: For, he, woundeth that he may bind up, He smiteth through, that, his own hands, may heal.
text.KJV: For he maketh sore, and bindeth up: he woundeth, and his hands make whole.
text.NABRE: For he wounds, but he binds up; he strikes, but his hands give healing.
text.DRB: Because he woundeth, and cureth: striketh, & his hands shall heal.

text.NRSV: He will deliver you from six troubles; in seven no harm shall touch you.
text.Rotherham: In six troubles, he will rescue thee, and, in seven, there shall smite thee no misfortune:
text.KJV: He shall deliver thee in six troubles: yea, in seven there shall no evil touch thee.
text.NABRE: Out of six troubles he will deliver you, and at the seventh no evil shall touch you.
text.DRB: In six tribulations he shall deliver thee, and in the seventh evil shall not touch thee.

text.NRSV: In famine he will redeem you from death, and in war from the power of the sword.
text.Rotherham: In famine, he will ransom thee from death, and in battle from the power of the sword;
text.KJV: In famine he shall redeem thee from death: and in war from the power of the sword.
text.NABRE: In famine he will deliver you from death, and in war from the power of the sword;
text.DRB: In famine he shall deliver thee from death, and in battle, from the hand of the sword.

text.NRSV: You shall be hidden from the scourge of the tongue, and shall not fear destruction when it comes.
text.Rotherham: During the scourge of the tongue, shalt thou be hid, neither shalt thou be afraid of destruction when it cometh;
text.KJV: Thou shalt be hid from the scourge of the tongue: neither shalt thou be afraid of destruction when it cometh.
text.NABRE: From the scourge of the tongue you shall be hidden, and you shall not fear approaching ruin.
text.DRB: From the scourge of the tongue thou shalt be hid; and thou shalt not fear calamity when it cometh.

text.NRSV: At destruction and famine you shall laugh, and shall not fear the wild animals of the earth.
text.Rotherham: At destruction and at hunger, shalt thou laugh, and, of the wild beast of the earth, be not thou afraid;
text.KJV: At destruction and famine thou shalt laugh: neither shalt thou be afraid of the beasts of the earth.
text.NABRE: At ruin and want you shall laugh; the beasts of the earth, do not fear.
text.DRB: In waste and famine thou shalt laugh, & the beasts of the earth thou shalt not fear.

text.NRSV: For you shall be in league with the stones of the field, and the wild animals shall be at peace with you.
text.Rotherham: For, with the stones of the field, shall be thy covenant, and, the wild beast of the field, hath been made thy friend;
text.KJV: For thou shalt be in league with the stones of the field: and the beasts of the field shall be at peace with thee.
text.NABRE: With the stones of the field shall your covenant be, and the wild beasts shall be at peace with you.
text.DRB: But with the stones of the lands thy covenant, and the beasts of the earth shall be peaceable to thee.

text.NRSV: You shall know that your tent is safe, you shall inspect your fold and miss nothing.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt know that, at peace, is thy tent, and shalt visit thy fold, and miss nothing;
text.KJV: And thou shalt know that thy tabernacle shall be in peace; and thou shalt visit thy habitation, and shalt not sin.
text.NABRE: And you shall know that your tent is secure; taking stock of your household, you shall miss nothing.
text.DRB: And thou shalt know that thy tabernacle hath peace, and visiting thy beauty, thou shalt not sin.

text.NRSV: You shall know that your descendants will be many, and your offspring like the grass of the earth.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt know, that numerous is thy seed, and, thine offspring, like the young shoots of the field.
text.KJV: Thou shalt know also that thy seed shall be great, and thine offspring as the grass of the earth.
text.NABRE: You shall know that your descendants are many, and your offspring like the grass of the earth.
text.DRB: Thou shalt know also that thy seed shall be manifold, and thy progeny as the grass of the earth.

text.NRSV: You shall come to your grave in ripe old age, as a shock of grain comes up to the threshing floor in its season.
text.Rotherham: Thou shalt come, yet robust, to the grave, as a stack of sheaves mounteth up in its season.
text.KJV: Thou shalt come to thy grave in a full age, like as a shock of corn cometh in in his season.
text.NABRE: You shall approach the grave in full vigor, as a shock of grain comes in at its season.
text.DRB: Thou shalt enter into the grave in aboundance, as a heap of wheat is carried in his time.

text.NRSV: See, we have searched this out; it is true. Hear, and know it for yourself.”
text.Rotherham: Lo! as for this, we have searched it out—so, it is, Hear it, and know, thou, for thyself.
text.KJV: Lo this, we have searched it, so it is; hear it, and know thou it for thy good.
text.NABRE: See, this we have searched out; so it is! This we have heard, and you should know.
text.DRB: Behold, this is even so, as we have searched out: which thou having heard revolve in thy mind.

text.NRSV: Then Job answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Job, and said:—
text.KJV: But Job answered and said,
text.NABRE: Then Job answered and said:
text.DRB: But Job answering, said:

text.NRSV: “O that my vexation were weighed, and all my calamity laid in the balances!
text.Rotherham: Oh that, weighed, were my vexation, and, my engulfing ruin—into the balances, they would lift up all at once!
text.KJV: Oh that my grief were throughly weighed, and my calamity laid in the balances together!
text.NABRE: Ah, could my anguish but be measured and my calamity laid with it in the scales,
text.DRB: would God my sins were weighed, whereby I have deserved wrath, and the calamity, which I suffer, in a balance.

text.NRSV: For then it would be heavier than the sand of the sea; therefore my words have been rash.
text.Rotherham: For, now, beyond the sand of the seas, would it be heavy, On this account, my words, have wandered.
text.KJV: For now it would be heavier than the sand of the sea: therefore my words are swallowed up.
text.NABRE: They would now outweigh the sands of the sea! Because of this I speak without restraint.
text.DRB: As the sand of the sea this would appear heavier, wherefore my words also are full of sorrow:

text.NRSV: For the arrows of the Almighty are in me; my spirit drinks their poison; the terrors of God are arrayed against me.
text.Rotherham: For, the arrows of the Almighty, are in me, The heat whereof, my spirit is drinking up, The, terrors of GOD, array themselves against me.
text.KJV: For the arrows of the Almighty are within me, the poison whereof drinketh up my spirit: the terrors of God do set themselves in array against me.
text.NABRE: For the arrows of the Almighty are in me, and my spirit drinks in their poison; the terrors of God are arrayed against me.
text.DRB: Because the arrows of our Lord be in me, the indignation whereof drinketh up my spirit, & the terrors of our Lord war against me.

text.NRSV: Does the wild donkey bray when it has grass, or the ox low over its fodder?
text.Rotherham: Doth the wild ass bray over grass? Or loweth the ox over his fodder?
text.KJV: Doth the wild ass bray when he hath grass? or loweth the ox over his fodder?
text.NABRE: Does the wild donkey bray when it has grass? Does the ox low over its fodder?
text.DRB: Will the wild ass roar when he hath grass? Or the oxe loweth when he shall stand before the full manger?

text.NRSV: Can that which is tasteless be eaten without salt, or is there any flavor in the white of an egg?
text.Rotherham: Can that which hath no savour be eaten without salt? Or is there any taste in the white of an egg?
text.KJV: Can that which is unsavoury be eaten without salt? or is there any taste in the white of an egg?
text.NABRE: Can anything insipid be eaten without salt? Is there flavor in the white of an egg?
text.DRB: Or can an unsavoury thing be eaten, that is not seasoned with salt? Or can a man taste that which being tasted bringeth death?

text.NRSV: My appetite refuses to touch them; they are like food that is loathsome to me.
text.Rotherham: My soul hath refused to touch, Those things, are like disease in my food.
text.KJV: The things that my soul refused to touch are as my sorrowful meat.
text.NABRE: I refuse to touch them; they are like loathsome food to me.
text.DRB: The things which before my soul would not touch, now for anguish are my meats.

text.NRSV: “O that I might have my request, and that God would grant my desire;
text.Rotherham: Oh that my request would come! and, my hope, oh that GOD would grant!
text.KJV: Oh that I might have my request; and that God would grant me the thing that I long for!
text.NABRE: Oh, that I might have my request, and that God would grant what I long for:
text.DRB: Who will grant that my petition may come: and that God would give me that which I expect?

text.NRSV: that it would please God to crush me, that he would let loose his hand and cut me off!
text.Rotherham: That it would please GOD to crush me, That he would set free his hand, and cut me off!
text.KJV: Even that it would please God to destroy me; that he would let loose his hand, and cut me off!
text.NABRE: Even that God would decide to crush me, that he would put forth his hand and cut me off!
text.DRB: And he that hath begun, the same would loose his hand, and cut me off?

text.NRSV: This would be my consolation; I would even exult in unrelenting pain; for I have not denied the words of the Holy One.
text.Rotherham: So might it still be my comfort, And I might exult in the anguish he would not spare,—That I had not concealed the sayings of the Holy One.
text.KJV: Then should I yet have comfort; yea, I would harden myself in sorrow: let him not spare; for I have not concealed the words of the Holy One.
text.NABRE: Then I should still have consolation and could exult through unremitting pain, because I have not transgressed the commands of the Holy One.
text.DRB: And this might be my comfort, that afflicting me with sorrow, he spare not, nor I gainsay the words of the Holy one.

text.NRSV: What is my strength, that I should wait? And what is my end, that I should be patient?
text.Rotherham: What is my strength, that I should hope? Or what mine end, that I should prolong my desire?
text.KJV: What is my strength, that I should hope? and what is mine end, that I should prolong my life?
text.NABRE: What strength have I that I should endure, and what is my limit that I should be patient?
text.DRB: For what is my strength, that I can sustain it? Or what is mine end, that I should do patiently?

text.NRSV: Is my strength the strength of stones, or is my flesh bronze?
text.Rotherham: Is my strength, the strength of stones? Or is, my flesh, of bronze?
text.KJV: Is my strength the strength of stones? or is my flesh of brass?
text.NABRE: Have I the strength of stones, or is my flesh of bronze?
text.DRB: Neither is my strength the strength of stones, neither is my flesh of brass.

text.NRSV: In truth I have no help in me, and any resource is driven from me.
text.Rotherham: Is there any help at all in me? Is not, abiding success, driven from me?
text.KJV: Is not my help in me? and is wisdom driven quite from me?
text.NABRE: Have I no helper, and has my good sense deserted me?
text.DRB: Behold there is no help for me in myself, and my familiar friends also are departed from me.

text.NRSV: “Those who withhold kindness from a friend forsake the fear of the Almighty.
text.Rotherham: The despairing, from his friend, should have lovingkindness, or, the reverence of the Almighty, he may forsake.
text.KJV: To him that is afflicted pity should be shewed from his friend; but he forsaketh the fear of the Almighty.
text.NABRE: A friend owes kindness to one in despair, though he has forsaken the fear of the Almighty.
text.DRB: He that taketh away mercy from his friend, forsaketh the fear of our Lord.

text.NRSV: My companions are treacherous like a wadi, like bed of torrents that run dry,
text.Rotherham: Mine own brethren, have proved treacherous like a torrent, like a channel of torrents which disappear:
text.KJV: My brethren have dealt deceitfully as a brook, and as the stream of brooks they pass away;
text.NABRE: My companions are undependable as a wadi, as watercourses that run dry in the wadies;
text.DRB: My brethren have passed by me, as the torrent that passeth swiftly in the valleys.

text.NRSV: which are turbid with ice, and into which the snow vanishes.
text.Rotherham: Which darken by reason of the cold, over them, is a covering made by the snow:
text.KJV: Which are blackish by reason of the ice, and wherein the snow is hid:
text.NABRE: Though they may be black with ice, and with snow heaped upon them,
text.DRB: They that fear the hoarfrost, snow shall fall upon them.

text.NRSV: In time of heat they disappear; when it is hot, they vanish from their place.
text.Rotherham: By the time they begin to thaw, they are dried up, as soon as it is warm, they have vanished out of their place.
text.KJV: What time they wax warm, they vanish: when it is hot, they are consumed out of their place.
text.NABRE: Yet once they flow, they cease to be; in the heat, they disappear from their place.
text.DRB: At the time, when they shall be dissipated they shall perish: & after they wax hot they shall be dissolved out of their place.

text.NRSV: The caravans turn aside from their course; they go up into the waste, and perish.
text.Rotherham: Caravans turn aside by their course, they go up into a waste, and are lost:
text.KJV: The paths of their way are turned aside; they go to nothing, and perish.
text.NABRE: Caravans wander from their routes; they go into the wasteland and perish.
text.DRB: The paths of their steps are entangled: they shall walk in vain, & shall perish.

text.NRSV: The caravans of Tema look, the travelers of Sheba hope.
text.Rotherham: The caravans of Tema looked about, the travelling companies of Sheba, hoped for them:
text.KJV: The troops of Tema looked, the companies of Sheba waited for them.
text.NABRE: The caravans of Tema search, the companies of Sheba have hopes;
text.DRB: Consider ye the paths of Thema, the ways of Saba, and expect a little while.

text.NRSV: They are disappointed because they were confident; they come there and are confounded.
text.Rotherham: They are ashamed that they had trusted, They have come up to one of them, and are confounded.
text.KJV: They were confounded because they had hoped; they came thither, and were ashamed.
text.NABRE: They are disappointed, though they were confident; they come there and are frustrated.
text.DRB: They are confounded, because I have hoped: they are come also even unto me, and are covered with shame.

text.NRSV: Truly you have now become a nothing; you see my calamity, and are afraid.
text.Rotherham: For, now, ye have come to him, ye see something fearful, and fear.
text.KJV: For now ye are nothing; ye see my casting down, and are afraid.
text.NABRE: It is thus that you have now become for me; you see a terrifying thing and are afraid.
text.DRB: Now you are come: and even now seeing my plague you are afraid.

text.NRSV: Have I said, ‘Make me a gift’? Or, ‘From your wealth offer a bribe for me’?
text.Rotherham: Is it that I said, Make me a gift, or, out of your abundance, offer a bribe on my behalf;
text.KJV: Did I say, Bring unto me? or, Give a reward for me of your substance?
text.NABRE: Have I said, “Give me something, make a bribe on my behalf from your possessions”?
text.DRB: Have I said: Bring ye to me, and of your substance give to me?

text.NRSV: Or, ‘Deliver me from the adversary’s hand’? Or, ‘Redeem me from the hand of ruthless men’?
text.Rotherham: And deliver me from the hand of the adversary? And, out of the hand of tyrants, ransom me?
text.KJV: Or, Deliver me from the enemy’s hand? or, Redeem me from the hand of the mighty?
text.NABRE: Or “Deliver me from the hand of the enemy, redeem me from oppressors”?
text.DRB: Or deliver me from the hand of the enemy, and out of the hand of the strong deliver me?

text.NRSV: “Teach me, and I will be silent; make me understand how I have gone wrong.
text.Rotherham: Show me, and, I, will hold my peace, And, wherein I have erred, cause me to understand.
text.KJV: Teach me, and I will hold my tongue: and cause me to understand wherein I have erred.
text.NABRE: Teach me, and I will be silent; make me understand how I have erred.
text.DRB: Teach ye me, and I will hold my peace: and if I perhaps have been ignorant in any thing, instruct ye me.

text.NRSV: How forceful are honest words! But what does reproduction by you reprove?
text.Rotherham: How pleasant are the sayings that are right! But what can a decision from you, decide?
text.KJV: How forcible are right words! but what doth your arguing reprove?
text.NABRE: How painful honest words can be; yet how unconvincing is your argument!
text.DRB: Why have you detracted from the words of truth, whereas there is none of you that can control me?

text.NRSV: Do you imagine that you can reprove words, when the speech of the despairing is wind?
text.Rotherham: To decide words, do ye intend, When, to the wind, are spoken the sayings of one in despair?
text.KJV: Do ye imagine to reprove words, and the speeches of one that is desperate, which are as wind?
text.NABRE: Do you consider your words as proof, but the sayings of a desperate man as wind?
text.DRB: To rebuke only you frame speeches, and you utter words in the wind.

text.NRSV: You would even cast lots over the orphan, and bargain over your friend.
text.Rotherham: Surely, the fatherless, ye would assail, and make merchandise of your friend!
text.KJV: Yea, ye overwhelm the fatherless, and ye dig a pit for your friend.
text.NABRE: You would even cast lots for the orphan, and would barter over your friend!
text.DRB: You rush in upon a pupil, and you endeavour to overthrow your friend.

text.NRSV: “But now, be pleased to look at me; for I will not lie to your face.
text.Rotherham: But, now, be pleased to turn to me, that it may be, to your faces, if I speak falsehood,
text.KJV: Now therefore be content, look upon me; for it is evident unto you if I lie.
text.NABRE: Come, now, give me your attention; surely I will not lie to your face.
text.DRB: Notwithstanding accomplish that which you have begun: give ear, and see whether I lie.

text.NRSV: Turn, I pray, let no wrong be done. Turn now, my vindication is at stake.
text.Rotherham: Reply, I pray you, let there be no perversity, Yea reply even yet, my vindication is in it!
text.KJV: Return, I pray you, let it not be iniquity; yea, return again, my righteousness is in it.
text.NABRE: Think it over; let there be no injustice. Think it over; I still am right.
text.DRB: Answer I beseech you without contention: and speaking that which is just, judge ye.

text.NRSV: Is there any wrong on my tongue? Cannot my taste discern calamity?
text.Rotherham: Is there, in my tongue, perversity? Or can, my sense, not discern, engulfing ruin?
text.KJV: Is there iniquity in my tongue? cannot my taste discern perverse things?
text.NABRE: Is there insincerity on my tongue, or cannot my taste discern falsehood?
text.DRB: And you shall not find iniquity in my tongue, neither shall folly sound in my jaws.

text.NRSV: “Do not mortals have a hard service on earth, and are not their days like the days of a laborer?
text.Rotherham: Is there not a warfare to a mortal, upon earth? And, as the days of a hireling, are not his days?
text.KJV: Is there not an appointed time to man upon earth? are not his days also like the days of an hireling?
text.NABRE: Is not life on earth a drudgery, its days like those of a hireling?
text.DRB: The life of man upon earth is a warfare, and his days, as the days of an hired man.

text.NRSV: Like a slave who longs for the shadow, and like laborers who look for their wages,
text.Rotherham: As, a bondman, panteth for the shadow, and as, a hireling, longeth for his wage,
text.KJV: As a servant earnestly desireth the shadow, and as an hireling looketh for the reward of his work:
text.NABRE: Like a slave who longs for the shade, a hireling who waits for wages,
text.DRB: As a servant desireth the shadow, and as the hired man tarrieth for the end of his work:

text.NRSV: so I am allotted months of emptiness, and nights of misery are appointed to me.
text.Rotherham: So, have I been made to inherit months of calamity, and, nights of weariness, have been appointed me.
text.KJV: So am I made to possess months of vanity, and wearisome nights are appointed to me.
text.NABRE: So I have been assigned months of futility, and troubled nights have been counted off for me.
text.DRB: so I also have had vain months, & have numbered to myself laborious nights.

text.NRSV: When I lie down I say, ‘When shall I rise?’ But the night is long, and I am full of tossing until dawn.
