text.Rotherham: As soon as I lie down, I say, When shall I arise? yet he lengtheneth out the evening, and I am wearied with tossings until the breeze of twilight.
text.KJV: When I lie down, I say, When shall I arise, and the night be gone? and I am full of tossings to and fro unto the dawning of the day.
text.NABRE: When I lie down I say, “When shall I arise?” then the night drags on; I am filled with restlessness until the dawn.
text.DRB: If I sleep, I shall say: When shall I arise? & again I shall expect the evening, & shall be replenished with sorrows even until darkness.

text.NRSV: My flesh is clothed with worms and dirt; my skin hardens, then breaks out afresh.
text.Rotherham: My flesh is clothed with worms and a coating of dust, My skin, hath hardened, and then run afresh:
text.KJV: My flesh is clothed with worms and clods of dust; my skin is broken, and become loathsome.
text.NABRE: My flesh is clothed with worms and scabs; my skin cracks and festers;
text.DRB: My flesh is clothed with rottenness & filth of dust, my skin is withered, & drawn together.

text.NRSV: My days are swifter than a weaver’s shuttle, and come to their end without hope.
text.Rotherham: My days, are swifter than a weaver’s shuttle, and they are spent, without hope.
text.KJV: My days are swifter than a weaver’s shuttle, and are spent without hope.
text.NABRE: My days are swifter than a weaver’s shuttle; they come to an end without hope.
text.DRB: My days have passed more swiftly, than the web is cut of the weaver, and are consumed without any hope.

text.NRSV: “Remember that my life is a breath; my eye will never again see good.
text.Rotherham: Remember thou, that, a wind, is my life, not again shall mine eye see blessing:
text.KJV: O remember that my life is wind: mine eye shall no more see good.
text.NABRE: Remember that my life is like the wind; my eye will not see happiness again.
text.DRB: Remember that my life is a wind, & mine eye shall not return to see good things.

text.NRSV: The eye that beholds me will see me no more; while your eyes are upon me, I shall be gone.
text.Rotherham: Nor shall see me—the eye that used to behold me, Thine eyes, are upon me, and I am not.
text.KJV: The eye of him that hath seen me shall see me no more: thine eyes are upon me, and I am not.
text.NABRE: The eye that now sees me shall no more behold me; when your eye is on me, I shall be gone.
text.DRB: Neither shall the sight of man behold me: thine eyes upon me, and I shall not stand.

text.NRSV: As the cloud fades and vanishes, so those who go down to Sheol do not come up;
text.Rotherham: A cloud faileth, and is gone, So, he that descendeth to hades, shall not come up:
text.KJV: As the cloud is consumed and vanisheth away: so he that goeth down to the grave shall come up no more.
text.NABRE: As a cloud dissolves and vanishes, so whoever goes down to Sheol shall not come up.
text.DRB: As a cloud is consumed, and passeth away: so he that shall descend to hell shall not ascend.

text.NRSV: they return no more to their houses, nor do their places know them any more.
text.Rotherham: He shall not return again to his house, and his own place shall be acquainted with him no more.
text.KJV: He shall return no more to his house, neither shall his place know him any more.
text.NABRE: They shall not return home again; their place shall know them no more.
text.DRB: Neither shall he return any more into his house, neither shall his place know him any more.

text.NRSV: “Therefore I will not restrain my mouth; I will speak in the anguish of my spirit; I will complain in the bitterness of my soul.
text.Rotherham: I also, cannot restrain my mouth,—I must speak, in the anguish of my spirit, I must find utterance, in the bitterness of my soul.
text.KJV: Therefore I will not refrain my mouth; I will speak in the anguish of my spirit; I will complain in the bitterness of my soul.
text.NABRE: My own utterance I will not restrain; I will speak in the anguish of my spirit; I will complain in the bitterness of my soul.
text.DRB: Wherefore I also will not spare my mouth, I will speak in the tribulation of my spirit: I will talk with the bitterness of my soul.

text.NRSV: Am I the Sea, or the Dragon, that you set a guard over me?
text.Rotherham: Am, I, a sea, or a sea-monster,—That thou shouldst set over me a watch?
text.KJV: Am I a sea, or a whale, that thou settest a watch over me?
text.NABRE: Am I the Sea, or the dragon, that you place a watch over me?
text.DRB: Why, am I a sea, or a whale, that thou hast compassed me with a prison?

text.NRSV: When I say, ‘My bed will comfort me, my couch will ease my complaint,’
text.Rotherham: When I say, My bed shall comfort me, my couch shall help to carry my complaint,
text.KJV: When I say, My bed shall comfort me, my couch shall ease my complaint;
text.NABRE: When I say, “My bed shall comfort me, my couch shall ease my complaint,”
text.DRB: If I say: My little bed shall comfort me, and I shall be relieved speaking with myself in my couch:

text.NRSV: then you scare me with dreams and terrify me with visions,
text.Rotherham: Then thou scarest me with dreams, and, by visions, dost thou terrify me:
text.KJV: Then thou scarest me with dreams, and terrifiest me through visions:
text.NABRE: Then you frighten me with dreams and terrify me with visions,
text.DRB: Thou wilt terrify me by dreams, and by visions shake me with horror.

text.NRSV: so that I would choose strangling and death rather than my body.
text.Rotherham: So that my soul chooseth strangling, Death, rather than [these] my bones!
text.KJV: So that my soul chooseth strangling, and death rather than my life.
text.NABRE: So that I should prefer strangulation and death rather than my existence.
text.DRB: For the which thing my soul hath chosen hanging, and my bones death.

text.NRSV: I loathe my life; I would not live forever. Let me alone, for my days are a breath.
text.Rotherham: I am wasted away, Not, to times age-abiding, can I live, Let me alone, for, a breath, are my days.
text.KJV: I loathe it; I would not live alway: let me alone; for my days are vanity.
text.NABRE: I waste away: I will not live forever; let me alone, for my days are but a breath.
text.DRB: I have despaired. I shall now live no longer: Spare me, for my days are nothing.

text.NRSV: What are human beings, that you make so much of them, that you set your mind on them,
text.Rotherham: What is a mortal, that thou shouldst nurture him? Or that thou shouldst fix upon him thy mind?
text.KJV: What is man, that thou shouldest magnify him? and that thou shouldest set thine heart upon him?
text.NABRE: What are human beings, that you make much of them, or pay them any heed?
text.DRB: What is man that thou magnifiest him? Or why settest thou thy heart toward him?

text.NRSV: visit them every morning, and test them every moment?
text.Rotherham: That thou shouldst inspect him morning by morning, moment by moment, shouldst test him?
text.KJV: And that thou shouldest visit him every morning, and try him every moment?
text.NABRE: You observe them every morning and try them at every moment!
text.DRB: Thou doest visit him early, and suddenly thou provest him:

text.NRSV: Will you not look away from me for a while, let me alone until I swallow my spittle?
text.Rotherham: How long wilt thou not look away from me? Wilt thou not let me alone, till I can swallow my spittle?
text.KJV: How long wilt thou not depart from me, nor let me alone till I swallow down my spittle?
text.NABRE: How long before you look away from me, and let me alone till I swallow my spit?
text.DRB: How long doest thou not spare me, nor suffer me that I swallow my spittle?

text.NRSV: If I sin, what do I do to you, you watcher of humanity? Why have you made me your target? Why have I become a burden to you?
text.Rotherham: I have sinned, What can I do for thee, thou watcher of men? Wherefore hast thou set me as thine object of attack, or have I become, unto thee, a burden?
text.KJV: I have sinned; what shall I do unto thee, O thou preserver of men? why hast thou set me as a mark against thee, so that I am a burden to myself?
text.NABRE: If I sin, what do I do to you, O watcher of mortals? Why have you made me your target? Why should I be a burden for you?
text.DRB: I have sinned, what shall I do to thee, o keeper of men? Why hast thou set me contrary to thee, and I am become burdenous to myself?

text.NRSV: Why do you not pardon my transgression and take away my iniquity? For now I shall lie in the earth; you will seek me, but I shall not be.”
text.Rotherham: And why wilt thou not remove my transgression, and take away mine iniquity? For, now, in the dust, should I lie down, and thou shouldst seek me diligently, and I should not be.
text.KJV: And why dost thou not pardon my transgression, and take away mine iniquity? for now shall I sleep in the dust; and thou shalt seek me in the morning, but I shall not be.
text.NABRE: Why do you not pardon my offense, or take away my guilt? For soon I shall lie down in the dust; and should you seek me I shall be gone.
text.DRB: Why doest thou not take away my sin, and why doest thou not take away mine iniquity? Behold now I shall sleep in the dust: and if thou seek me in the morning, I shall not be.

text.NRSV: Then Bildad the Shuhite answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Bildad the Shuhite, and said:—
text.KJV: Then answered Bildad the Shuhite, and said,
text.NABRE: Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:
text.DRB: But Baldad the Suhite answering, said:

text.NRSV: “How long will you say these things, and the words of your mouth be a great wind?
text.Rotherham: How long wilt thou speak these things? Or, as a mighty wind, shall be the sayings of thy mouth?
text.KJV: How long wilt thou speak these things? and how long shall the words of thy mouth be like a strong wind?
text.NABRE: How long will you utter such things? The words from your mouth are a mighty wind!
text.DRB: How long wilt thou speak such things, & shall the spirit of the word of thy mouth be multiplied?

text.NRSV: Does God pervert justice? Or does the Almighty pervert the right?
text.Rotherham: Should, GOD, pervert justice? Or, the Almighty, pervert righteousness?
text.KJV: Doth God pervert judgment? or doth the Almighty pervert justice?
text.NABRE: Does God pervert judgment, does the Almighty pervert justice?
text.DRB: Why, doth God supplant judgment? Or doth the Omnipotent subvert that which is just?

text.NRSV: If your children sinned against him, he delivered them into the power of their transgression.
text.Rotherham: Though, thy children, sinned against him, and he delivered them into the hand of their transgression,
text.KJV: If thy children have sinned against him, and he have cast them away for their transgression;
text.NABRE: If your children have sinned against him and he has left them in the grip of their guilt,
text.DRB: Although thy children have sinned to him, and he hath left them in the hand of their iniquity:

text.NRSV: If you will seek God and make supplication to the Almighty,
text.Rotherham: Yet, if, thou thyself, wilt diligently seek unto GOD,—and, unto the Almighty, wilt make supplication;
text.KJV: If thou wouldest seek unto God betimes, and make thy supplication to the Almighty;
text.NABRE: Still, if you yourself have recourse to God and make supplication to the Almighty,
text.DRB: Yet if thou wilt arise early to God, & wilt beseech the Omnipotent:

text.NRSV: if you are pure and upright, surely then he will rouse himself for you and restore to you your rightful place.
text.Rotherham: If, pure and upright, thou thyself, art, surely, now, will he answer thy prayer, and will prosper thy righteous habitation:
text.KJV: If thou wert pure and upright; surely now he would awake for thee, and make the habitation of thy righteousness prosperous.
text.NABRE: Should you be blameless and upright, surely now he will rouse himself for you and restore your rightful home.
text.DRB: If thou wilt walk clean & upright, he will forthwith awake unto thee, and will make the habitation of thy justice peaceable:

text.NRSV: Though your beginning was small, your latter days will be very great.
text.Rotherham: So shall thy beginning appear small,—when, thy latter end, he shall greatly increase!
text.KJV: Though thy beginning was small, yet thy latter end should greatly increase.
text.NABRE: Though your beginning was small, your future will flourish indeed.
text.DRB: In so much, that if thy former things have been little, thy later things may be multiplied exceedingly.

text.NRSV: “For inquire now of bygone generations, and consider what their ancestors have found;
text.Rotherham: For inquire, I pray thee, of a former generation, and prepare thyself for the research of their fathers;—
text.KJV: For enquire, I pray thee, of the former age, and prepare thyself to the search of their fathers:
text.NABRE: Inquire of the former generations, pay attention to the experience of their ancestors—
text.DRB: For ask the old generation, and search diligently the memory of the fathers.

text.NRSV: for we are but of yesterday, and we know nothing, for our days on earth are a shadow.
text.Rotherham: For, of yesterday, are, we, and cannot know, for, a shadow, are our days upon earth:
text.KJV: (For we are but of yesterday, and know nothing, because our days upon earth are a shadow:)
text.NABRE: As we are but of yesterday and have no knowledge, because our days on earth are but a shadow—
text.DRB: (For we are but as yesterday, & are ignorant that our days upon the earth are as a shadow.)

text.NRSV: Will they not teach you and tell you and utter words out of their understanding?
text.Rotherham: Shall, they, not teach thee—tell thee, and, out of their memory, bring forth words?
text.KJV: Shall not they teach thee, and tell thee, and utter words out of their heart?
text.NABRE: Will they not teach you and tell you and utter their words of understanding?
text.DRB: And they shall teach thee: they shall speak to thee, and from their heart shall utter words.

text.NRSV: “Can papyrus grow where there is no marsh? Can reeds flourish where there is no water?
text.Rotherham: Can the paper-reed grow up, without a marsh? Or the rush grow up, without water?
text.KJV: Can the rush grow up without mire? can the flag grow without water?
text.NABRE: Can the papyrus grow up without mire? Can the reed grass flourish without water?
text.DRB: Can the rush be green without moisture? Or a sedgy place grow without water?

text.NRSV: While yet in flower and not cut down, they wither before any other plant.
text.Rotherham: Though while still, in its freshness, it be not plucked off, yet, before any kind of grass, it doth wither:
text.KJV: Whilst it is yet in his greenness, and not cut down, it withereth before any other herb.
text.NABRE: While it is yet green and uncut, it withers quicker than any grass.
text.DRB: When it is yet in his flower, and is not plucked with the hand, it withereth before all herbs:

text.NRSV: Such are the paths of all who forget God; the hope of the godless shall perish.
text.Rotherham: So, shall be the latter end of all who forget GOD, and, the hope of the impious, shall perish:
text.KJV: So are the paths of all that forget God; and the hypocrite’s hope shall perish:
text.NABRE: So is the end of everyone who forgets God, and so shall the hope of the godless perish.
text.DRB: Even so the ways of all, that forget God, and the hope of the hypocrite shall perish:

text.NRSV: Their confidence is gossamer, a spider’s house their trust.
text.Rotherham: Whose trust shall be contemptible,—and, a spider’s web, his confidence:
text.KJV: Whose hope shall be cut off, and whose trust shall be a spider’s web.
text.NABRE: His confidence is but a gossamer thread, his trust is a spider’s house.
text.DRB: His folly shall not please him, and his confidence as the spiders' web.

text.NRSV: If one leans against their house, it will not stand; if one holds fast to it, it will not endure.
text.Rotherham: He leaneth upon his house, and it will not stand, he holdeth it fast, and it will not remain erect.
text.KJV: He shall lean upon his house, but it shall not stand: he shall hold it fast, but it shall not endure.
text.NABRE: He shall lean upon his house, but it shall not stand; he shall cling to it, but it shall not endure.
text.DRB: He shall lean upon his house, and it shall not stand: he shall stay it up, and it shall not rise:

text.NRSV: They thrive before the sun, and their shoots spread over the garden.
text.Rotherham: Full of moisture he is, before the sun, and, over his garden, his shoot goeth forth:
text.KJV: He is green before the sun, and his branch shooteth forth in his garden.
text.NABRE: He thrives in full sun, and over his garden his shoots go forth;
text.DRB: He seemeth moistened before the sun come, & in his rising his blossom shall go forth.

text.NRSV: Their roots twine around the stoneheap; they live among the rocks.
text.Rotherham: Over a heap, his roots are entwined, a place of stones, he descrieth;
text.KJV: His roots are wrapped about the heap, and seeth the place of stones.
text.NABRE: About a heap of stones his roots are entwined; among the rocks he takes hold.
text.DRB: Upon a heap of rocks his roots shall be thick, and among stones he shall abide.

text.NRSV: If they are destroyed from their place, then it will deny them, saying, ‘I have never seen you.’
text.Rotherham: If one destroy him out of his place, then will it disown him [saying] —I have not seen thee.
text.KJV: If he destroy him from his place, then it shall deny him, saying, I have not seen thee.
text.NABRE: Yet if one tears him from his place, it will disown him: “I have never seen you!”
text.DRB: If he swallow him up out of his place, he will deny him, and will say: I know thee not.

text.NRSV: See, these are its joys; from the dust others will sprout.
text.Rotherham: Lo! that, is the joy of his way,—and, out of the dust, shall others spring up.
text.KJV: Behold, this is the joy of his way, and out of the earth shall others grow.
text.NABRE: There he lies rotting beside the road, and out of the soil another sprouts.
text.DRB: For this is the joy of his way, that others may spring again of the earth.

text.NRSV: “See, God will not reject a blameless person, nor take the hand of evildoers.
text.Rotherham: Lo! GOD, will not reject a blameless man, neither will he grasp the hand of evil-doers:
text.KJV: Behold, God will not cast away a perfect man, neither will he help the evil doers:
text.NABRE: Behold, God will not cast away the upright; neither will he take the hand of the wicked.
text.DRB: God will not reject the simple, nor reach his hand to the malignant.

text.NRSV: He will yet fill your mouth with laughter, and your lips with shouts of joy.
text.Rotherham: At length he shall fill with laughter thy mouth, and thy lips, with a shout of triumph:
text.KJV: Till he fill thy mouth with laughing, and thy lips with rejoicing.
text.NABRE: Once more will he fill your mouth with laughter and your lips with rejoicing.
text.DRB: Until thy mouth be filled with laughter, & thy lips with jubilation.

text.NRSV: Those who hate you will be clothed with shame, and the tent of the wicked will be no more.”
text.Rotherham: They who hate thee, shall be clothed with shame, but, the tent of the lawless, shall not be!
text.KJV: They that hate thee shall be clothed with shame; and the dwelling place of the wicked shall come to nought.
text.NABRE: Those who hate you shall be clothed with shame, and the tent of the wicked shall be no more.
text.DRB: They that hate thee, shall be clothed with confusion: and the tabernacle of the impious shall not stand.

text.NRSV: Then Job answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Job, and said—
text.KJV: Then Job answered and said,
text.NABRE: Then Job answered and said:
text.DRB: And Job answering, said:

text.NRSV: “Indeed I know that this is so; but how can a mortal be just before God?
text.Rotherham: Of a truth, I know that so it is, But how can a mortal be just with GOD?
text.KJV: I know it is so of a truth: but how should man be just with God?
text.NABRE: I know well that it is so; but how can anyone be in the right before God?
text.DRB: Indeed I know it is so, and that man is not justified compared with God.

text.NRSV: If one wished to contend with him, one could not answer him once in a thousand.
text.Rotherham: If he choose to contend with him, he cannot answer him, one of a thousand:
text.KJV: If he will contend with him, he cannot answer him one of a thousand.
text.NABRE: Should one wish to contend with him, he could not answer him once in a thousand times.
text.DRB: If he will content with him, he cannot answer him one for a thousand.

text.NRSV: He is wise in heart, and mighty in strength—who has resisted him, and succeeded?—
text.Rotherham: Wise in heart, and alert in vigour, What man hath hardened himself against him, and prospered!
text.KJV: He is wise in heart, and mighty in strength: who hath hardened himself against him, and hath prospered?
text.NABRE: God is wise in heart and mighty in strength; who has withstood him and remained whole?
text.DRB: He is wise of heart, and strong of force: who hath resisted him, and hath had peace?

text.NRSV: he who removes mountains, and they do not know it, when he overturns them in his anger;
text.Rotherham: Who removeth mountains, unawares, Who overturneth them in his anger;
text.KJV: Which removeth the mountains, and they know not: which overturneth them in his anger.
text.NABRE: He removes the mountains before they know it; he overturns them in his anger.
text.DRB: He that transported mountains, and they whom he subverted in his fury, knew not.

text.NRSV: who shakes the earth out of its place, and its pillars tremble;
text.Rotherham: Who shaketh the earth, out of its place, and, the pillars thereof, shudder;
text.KJV: Which shaketh the earth out of her place, and the pillars thereof tremble.
text.NABRE: He shakes the earth out of its place, and the pillars beneath it tremble.
text.DRB: He that removeth the earth out of her place, and the pillars thereof are shaken.

text.NRSV: who commands the sun, and it does not rise; who seals up the stars;
text.Rotherham: Who commandeth the sun, and it breaketh not forth, and, about the stars, he putteth a seal;
text.KJV: Which commandeth the sun, and it riseth not; and sealeth up the stars.
text.NABRE: He commands the sun, and it does not rise; he seals up the stars.
text.DRB: He that commandeth the sun, and it riseth not: and shutteth up the stars as it were under a seal:

text.NRSV: who alone stretched out the heavens and trampled the waves of the Sea;
text.Rotherham: Who spreadeth out fire heavens, by himself alone! and marcheth along, on the heights of the sea;
text.KJV: Which alone spreadeth out the heavens, and treadeth upon the waves of the sea.
text.NABRE: He alone stretches out the heavens and treads upon the back of the sea.
text.DRB: He that alone spreadeth the heaven, & goeth upon the waves of the sea.

text.NRSV: who made the Bear and Orion, the Pleiades and the chambers of the south;
text.Rotherham: Who made the Bear, the Giant and the Cluster, and the chambers of the south;
text.KJV: Which maketh Arcturus, Orion, and Pleiades, and the chambers of the south.
text.NABRE: He made the Bear and Orion, the Pleiades and the constellations of the south;
text.DRB: He that maketh Arcturus, and Orion, and Hyades, and the inner parts of the south.

text.NRSV: who does great things beyond understanding, and marvelous things without number.
text.Rotherham: Who doeth great things, past finding out, and marvels, beyond number.
text.KJV: Which doeth great things past finding out; yea, and wonders without number.
text.NABRE: He does things great and unsearchable, things marvelous and innumerable.
text.DRB: He that doth great things and incomprehensible, and marvelous, of the which there is no number.

text.NRSV: Look, he passes by me, and I do not see him; he moves on, but I do not perceive him.
text.Rotherham: Lo! he cometh upon me, yet can I not see him, Yea he passeth on, yet can I not discern him.
text.KJV: Lo, he goeth by me, and I see him not: he passeth on also, but I perceive him not.
text.NABRE: Should he come near me, I do not see him; should he pass by, I am not aware of him;
text.DRB: If he come to me, I shall not see him: if he depart, I shall not understand.

text.NRSV: He snatches away; who can stop him? Who will say to him, ‘What are you doing?’
text.Rotherham: Lo! he snatcheth away, who can bring it back? Who shall say unto him, What wouldst thou do?
text.KJV: Behold, he taketh away, who can hinder him? who will say unto him, What doest thou?
text.NABRE: Should he seize me forcibly, who can resist? Who can say to him, “What are you doing?”
text.DRB: If suddenly he ask, who shall answer him? Or who can say: Why doest thou so?

text.NRSV: “God will not turn back his anger; the helpers of Rahab bowed beneath him.
text.Rotherham: As for GOD, if he withdraw not his anger, under him, will have submitted themselves—the proud helpers.
text.KJV: If God will not withdraw his anger, the proud helpers do stoop under him.
text.NABRE: He is God and he does not relent; the helpers of Rahab bow beneath him.
text.DRB: God whose wrath no man can resist, and under whom they stoop that carry the world.

text.NRSV: How then can I answer him, choosing my words with him?
text.Rotherham: How much less that, I, should answer him, should choose my words with him?
text.KJV: How much less shall I answer him, and choose out my words to reason with him?
text.NABRE: How then could I give him any answer, or choose out arguments against him!
text.DRB: How great am I then, that I may answer him, and speak in my words with him?

text.NRSV: Though I am innocent, I cannot answer him; I must appeal for mercy to my accuser.
text.Rotherham: Whom, though I were righteous, yet would I not answer, to be absolved, I would make supplication.
text.KJV: Whom, though I were righteous, yet would I not answer, but I would make supplication to my judge.
text.NABRE: Even though I were right, I could not answer, but should rather beg for what was due me.
text.DRB: Who although I have any just thing, will not answer, but will beseech my judge.

text.NRSV: If I summoned him and he answered me, I do not believe that he would listen to my voice.
text.Rotherham: Though I had called, and he had answered me, I could not believe, that he would lend an ear to my voice.
text.KJV: If I had called, and he had answered me; yet would I not believe that he had hearkened unto my voice.
text.NABRE: If I appealed to him and he answered me, I could not believe that he would listen to me;
text.DRB: And when he shall hear me invocating, I do not believe that he hath heard my voice.

text.NRSV: For he crushes me with a tempest, and multiplies my wounds without cause;
text.Rotherham: For, with a tempest, would he fall upon me, and would multiply my wounds without need;
text.KJV: For he breaketh me with a tempest, and multiplieth my wounds without cause.
text.NABRE: With a storm he might overwhelm me, and multiply my wounds for nothing;
text.DRB: For in a hurlwind shall he break me, and shall multiply my wounds yea without cause.

text.NRSV: he will not let me get my breath, but fills me with bitterness.
text.Rotherham: He would not suffer me to recover my breath, for he would surfeit me with bitter things.
text.KJV: He will not suffer me to take my breath, but filleth me with bitterness.
text.NABRE: He would not allow me to draw breath, but might fill me with bitter griefs.
text.DRB: He granteth not my spirit to rest, and he filleth me with bitterness.

text.NRSV: If it is a contest of strength, he is the strong one! If it is a matter of justice, who can summon him?
text.Rotherham: If it regardeth vigour, bold is he! If justice, who could summon him?
text.KJV: If I speak of strength, lo, he is strong: and if of judgment, who shall set me a time to plead?
text.NABRE: If it be a question of strength, he is mighty; or of judgment, who will call him to account?
text.DRB: If strength be demanded, he is most strong: if equity of judgment, no man dare give testimony for me.

text.NRSV: Though I am innocent, my own mouth would condemn me; though I am blameless, he would prove me perverse.
text.Rotherham: If I should justify myself, mine own mouth, would condemn me,—I blameless? then had it shewn me perverse.
text.KJV: If I justify myself, mine own mouth shall condemn me: if I say, I am perfect, it shall also prove me perverse.
text.NABRE: Though I were right, my own mouth might condemn me; were I innocent, it might put me in the wrong.
text.DRB: If I will justify myself, mine own mouth shall condemn me: if I will shew myself innocent, he shall prove me wicked.

text.NRSV: I am blameless; I do not know myself; I loathe my life.
text.Rotherham: I blameless? I should not know my own soul, I should despise my own life!
text.KJV: Though I were perfect, yet would I not know my soul: I would despise my life.
text.NABRE: I am innocent, but I cannot know it; I despise my life.
text.DRB: Although I shall be simple, the self-same shall my soul be ignorant of, and I shall be weary of my life,

text.NRSV: It is all one; therefore I say, he destroys both the blameless and the wicked.
text.Rotherham: One thing, there is, for which cause, I have said it, The blameless and the lawless, he bringeth to an end.
text.KJV: This is one thing, therefore I said it, He destroyeth the perfect and the wicked.
text.NABRE: It is all one! therefore I say: Both the innocent and the wicked he destroys.
text.DRB: One thing there is that I have spoken, both the innocent and the impious he consumeth.

text.NRSV: When disaster brings sudden death, he mocks at the calamity of the innocent.
text.Rotherham: If, a scourge, slay suddenly, at the despair of innocent ones, he mocketh.
text.KJV: If the scourge slay suddenly, he will laugh at the trial of the innocent.
text.NABRE: When the scourge slays suddenly, he scoffs at the despair of the innocent.
text.DRB: If he scourge, let him kill at once, and not laugh at the pains of innocents.

text.NRSV: The earth is given into the hand of the wicked; he covers the eyes of its judges—if it is not he, who then is it?
text.Rotherham: The earth, hath been given into the hand of a lawless one, The faces of her judges, he covereth, If not, then who is it?
text.KJV: The earth is given into the hand of the wicked: he covereth the faces of the judges thereof; if not, where, and who is he?
text.NABRE: The earth is given into the hands of the wicked; he covers the faces of its judges. If it is not he, who then is it?
text.DRB: The earth is given into the hands of the impious, he covereth the face of the judges thereof: and if it be not he, who is it then?

text.NRSV: “My days are swifter than a runner; they flee away, they see no good.
text.Rotherham: My days, therefore, are swifter than a runner, They have fled, they have seen no good.
text.KJV: Now my days are swifter than a post: they flee away, they see no good.
text.NABRE: My days are swifter than a runner, they flee away; they see no happiness;
text.DRB: My days have been swifter than a post: they have fled and have not seen good.

text.NRSV: They go by like skiffs of reed, like an eagle swooping on the prey.
text.Rotherham: They have passed away with boats of paper-reed, like a vulture [which] rusheth upon food.
text.KJV: They are passed away as the swift ships: as the eagle that hasteth to the prey.
text.NABRE: They shoot by like skiffs of reed, like an eagle swooping upon its prey.
text.DRB: They have passed by as ships carrying fruits, as an eagle flying to meat.

text.NRSV: If I say, ‘I will forget my complaint; I will put off my sad countenance and be cheerful,’
text.Rotherham: If I say, I will forget my complaint, I will lay aside my sad countenance, and brighten up,
text.KJV: If I say, I will forget my complaint, I will leave off my heaviness, and comfort myself:
text.NABRE: If I say: I will forget my complaining, I will lay aside my sadness and be of good cheer,
text.DRB: When I shall say: I will not speak so, I change my face, & am tormented with sorrow.

text.NRSV: I become afraid of all my suffering, for I know you will not hold me innocent.
text.Rotherham: I am afraid of all my pains, I know, that thou wilt not pronounce me innocent.
text.KJV: I am afraid of all my sorrows, I know that thou wilt not hold me innocent.
text.NABRE: Then I am in dread of all my pains; I know that you will not hold me innocent.
text.DRB: I feared all my works, knowing that thou didst not spare the offender.

text.NRSV: I shall be condemned; why then do I labor in vain?
text.Rotherham: I, shall be held guilty,—Wherefore then, in vain, should I toil?
text.KJV: If I be wicked, why then labour I in vain?
text.NABRE: It is I who will be accounted guilty; why then should I strive in vain?
text.DRB: But if so also I am impious, why have I laboured in vain?

text.NRSV: If I wash myself with soap and cleanse my hands with lye,
text.Rotherham: Though I bathe myself in snow water, and cleanse, in cleanness itself, my hands,
text.KJV: If I wash myself with snow water, and make my hands never so clean;
text.NABRE: If I should wash myself with soap and cleanse my hands with lye,
text.DRB: If I be washed as it were with snow-waters, & my hands shall shine as most clean:

text.NRSV: yet you will plunge me into filth, and my own clothes will abhor me.
text.Rotherham: Then, in a ditch, wouldst thou plunge me, and mine own clothes should abhor me:
text.KJV: Yet shalt thou plunge me in the ditch, and mine own clothes shall abhor me.
text.NABRE: Yet you would plunge me in the ditch, so that my garments would abhor me.
text.DRB: Yet shall thou dip me in filth, and my garments shall abhor me.

text.NRSV: For he is not a mortal, as I am, that I might answer him, that we should come to trial together.
text.Rotherham: For he is not a man like myself, whom I might answer, nor could we come together into judgment:
text.KJV: For he is not a man, as I am, that I should answer him, and we should come together in judgment.
text.NABRE: For he is not a man like myself, that I should answer him, that we should come together in judgment.
text.DRB: For neither I will answer a man that is like myself: nor that may be heard with me equally in judgment.

text.NRSV: There is no umpire between us, who might lay his hand on us both.
text.Rotherham: There is not, between us, a mediator, who might lay his hand upon us both.
text.KJV: Neither is there any daysman betwixt us, that might lay his hand upon us both.
text.NABRE: Would that there were an arbiter between us, who could lay his hand upon us both
text.DRB: There is none that may be able to reprove both, and to put his hand between both.

text.NRSV: Let him take his rod away from me, and let not dread of him terrify me.
text.Rotherham: Let him take from off me his rod, and, his terror, let it not startle me:
text.KJV: Let him take his rod away from me, and let not his fear terrify me:
text.NABRE: and withdraw his rod from me, So that his terrors did not frighten me;
text.DRB: Let him take his rod from me, and let not his dread terrify me.

text.NRSV: Then I would speak without fear of him, for I know I am not what I am thought to be.
text.Rotherham: I could speak, and not be afraid of him, although, not so, am, I, in myself!
text.KJV: Then would I speak, and not fear him; but it is not so with me.
text.NABRE: that I might speak without being afraid of him. Since this is not the case with me, I loathe my life.
text.DRB: I will speak, and will not fear him: for I cannot answer fearing.

text.NRSV: “I loathe my life; I will give free utterance to my complaint; I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.
text.Rotherham: My soul doth loathe my life,—I let loose my complaint, I speak, in the bitterness of my soul.
text.KJV: My soul is weary of my life; I will leave my complaint upon myself; I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.
text.NABRE: I will give myself up to complaint; I will speak from the bitterness of my soul.
text.DRB: My soul is weary of my life, I will let my speech pass against myself. I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.

text.NRSV: I will say to God, Do not condemn me; let me know why you contend with me.
text.Rotherham: I say unto GOD, Do not hold me guilty, Let me know, on what account thou contendest with me!
text.KJV: I will say unto God, Do not condemn me; shew me wherefore thou contendest with me.
text.NABRE: I will say to God: Do not put me in the wrong! Let me know why you oppose me.
text.DRB: I will say to God: Condemn me not: tell me why thou judgest me so.

text.NRSV: Does it seem good to you to oppress, to despise the work of your hands and favor the schemes of the wicked?
text.Rotherham: Is it seemly in thee, that thou shouldst oppress? that thou shouldst despise the labour of thine own hand, when, upon the counsel of the lawless, thou hast shone?
text.KJV: Is it good unto thee that thou shouldest oppress, that thou shouldest despise the work of thine hands, and shine upon the counsel of the wicked?
text.NABRE: Is it a pleasure for you to oppress, to spurn the work of your hands, and shine on the plan of the wicked?
text.DRB: Doth it seem good to thee, if thou calumniate me, and oppress me the work of thy hands, and help the counsel of the impious?

text.NRSV: Do you have eyes of flesh? Do you see as humans see?
text.Rotherham: Eyes of flesh, hast thou? or, as a mortal seeth, seest thou?
text.KJV: Hast thou eyes of flesh? or seest thou as man seeth?
text.NABRE: Have you eyes of flesh? Do you see as mortals see?
text.DRB: Hast thou eyes of flesh: or as a man seeth, shalt thou also see?

text.NRSV: Are your days like the days of mortals, or your years like human years,
text.Rotherham: As the days of a mortal, are thy days? or, thy years, as the days of a man?
text.KJV: Are thy days as the days of man? are thy years as man’s days,
text.NABRE: Are your days like the days of a mortal, and are your years like a human lifetime,
text.DRB: Are thy days as the days of man, and are thy years as the times of men:

text.NRSV: that you seek out my iniquity and search for my sin,
text.Rotherham: That thou shouldst seek for mine iniquity, and, for my sin, shouldst make search:
text.KJV: That thou enquirest after mine iniquity, and searchest after my sin?
text.NABRE: That you seek for guilt in me and search after my sins,
text.DRB: That thou seekest my iniquity, and searchest my sin?

text.NRSV: although you know that I am not guilty, and there is no one to deliver out of your hand?
text.Rotherham: Though it is, within thine own knowledge, that I would not be lawless, and, none, out of thy hand, can deliver?
text.KJV: Thou knowest that I am not wicked; and there is none that can deliver out of thine hand.
text.NABRE: Even though you know that I am not wicked, and that none can deliver me out of your hand?
text.DRB: And thou mayst know that I have done no impious thing, whereas there is no man that can deliver out of thy hand.

text.NRSV: Your hands fashioned and made me; and now you turn and destroy me.
text.Rotherham: Thine own hands, shaped me, and made me, all in unison round about, and yet thou hast confounded me.
text.KJV: Thine hands have made me and fashioned me together round about; yet thou dost destroy me.
text.NABRE: Your hands have formed me and fashioned me; will you then turn and destroy me?
text.DRB: Thy hands have made me, & framed me wholly round-about, & dost thou so suddenly cast me down headlong?

text.NRSV: Remember that you fashioned me like clay; and will you turn me to dust again?
text.Rotherham: Remember, I pray thee, that, as clay, thou didst make me, and, unto dust, thou wilt cause me to return.
text.KJV: Remember, I beseech thee, that thou hast made me as the clay; and wilt thou bring me into dust again?
text.NABRE: Oh, remember that you fashioned me from clay! Will you then bring me down to dust again?
text.DRB: Remember I beseech thee that as clay thou madest me, & into dust thou wilt bring me again.

text.NRSV: Did you not pour me out like milk and curdle me like cheese?
text.Rotherham: Didst thou not, like milk, pour me forth? and, as cheese, curdle me?
text.KJV: Hast thou not poured me out as milk, and curdled me like cheese?
text.NABRE: Did you not pour me out like milk, and thicken me like cheese?
text.DRB: Hast thou not as milk milked me, and curded me as cheese?

text.NRSV: You clothed me with skin and flesh, and knit me together with bones and sinews.
text.Rotherham: With skin and flesh, clothe me? and, with bones and sinews, interweave me?
text.KJV: Thou hast clothed me with skin and flesh, and hast fenced me with bones and sinews.
text.NABRE: With skin and flesh you clothed me, with bones and sinews knit me together.
text.DRB: With skin & flesh thou hast clothed me: with bones and sinews thou hast compacted me.

text.NRSV: You have granted me life and steadfast love, and your care has preserved my spirit.
text.Rotherham: Life and lovingkindness, thou didst bestow upon me,—and, thy watchful care, preserved my breath.
text.KJV: Thou hast granted me life and favour, and thy visitation hath preserved my spirit.
text.NABRE: Life and love you granted me, and your providence has preserved my spirit.
text.DRB: Life and mercy thou hast given to me, and thy visitation hath kept my spirit.

text.NRSV: Yet these things you hid in your heart; I know that this was your purpose.
text.Rotherham: Yet, these things, thou didst hide in thy heart, I know that, this, hath been with thee!
text.KJV: And these things hast thou hid in thine heart: I know that this is with thee.
text.NABRE: Yet these things you have hidden in your heart; I know they are your purpose:
text.DRB: Although thou conceal these things in thy heart, yet I know that thou rememberest all things.

text.NRSV: If I sin, you watch me, and do not acquit me of my iniquity.
text.Rotherham: If I have sinned, then couldst thou watch me, and, from mine iniquity, thou wouldst not acquit me:
text.KJV: If I sin, then thou markest me, and thou wilt not acquit me from mine iniquity.
text.NABRE: If I should sin, you would keep a watch on me, and from my guilt you would not absolve me.
text.DRB: If I have sinned and thou hast spared me for an hour: why doest thou not suffer me to be clean from mine iniquity?

text.NRSV: If I am wicked, woe to me! If I am righteous, I cannot lift up my head, for I am filled with disgrace and look upon my misery.
text.Rotherham: If I have been lawless, alas for me! Or, if I am righteous, I will not lift up my head, Surfeited with shame, look thou then on my humiliation.
text.KJV: If I be wicked, woe unto me; and if I be righteous, yet will I not lift up my head. I am full of confusion; therefore see thou mine affliction;
text.NABRE: If I should be wicked, alas for me! even if righteous, I dare not hold up my head, sated with shame, drenched in affliction!
text.DRB: And if I shall be impious, woe is to me: and if just, I shall not lift up my head, filled with affliction and misery.

text.NRSV: Bold as a lion you hunt me; you repeat your exploits against me.
text.Rotherham: When it is lifted up, like a howling lion, thou dost hunt me, Then again thou dost shew thyself marvellous against me.
text.KJV: For it increaseth. Thou huntest me as a fierce lion: and again thou shewest thyself marvellous upon me.
text.NABRE: Should it lift up, you hunt me like a lion: repeatedly you show your wondrous power against me,
text.DRB: And for pride as a lioness thou wilt take me, and returning thou doest marvelously torment me.

text.NRSV: You renew your witnesses against me, and increase your vexation toward me; you bring fresh troops against me.
text.Rotherham: Thou renewest thy witnesses before me, and dost increase thy vexation with me, Relays—yea an army, is with me.
text.KJV: Thou renewest thy witnesses against me, and increasest thine indignation upon me; changes and war are against me.
text.NABRE: You renew your attack upon me and multiply your harassment of me; in waves your troops come against me.
text.DRB: Thou renewest thy witnesses against me, & multipliest thy wrath toward me, and pains do war upon me.

text.NRSV: “Why did you bring me forth from the womb? Would that I had died before any eye had seen me,
text.Rotherham: Wherefore then, from the womb, didst thou bring me forth? I might have breathed my last, and, no eye, have seen me.
text.KJV: Wherefore then hast thou brought me forth out of the womb? Oh that I had given up the ghost, and no eye had seen me!
text.NABRE: Why then did you bring me forth from the womb? I should have died and no eye have seen me.
text.DRB: Why didst thou bring me forth out of the matrice? Who would God, I had been consumed, that eye might not see me.

text.NRSV: and were as though I had not been, carried from the womb to the grave.
text.Rotherham: As though I had not been, should I have become,—from the womb to the grave, might I have been borne.
text.KJV: I should have been as though I had not been; I should have been carried from the womb to the grave.
text.NABRE: I should be as though I had never lived; I should have been taken from the womb to the grave.
text.DRB: I had been as if I were not, carried from the womb to the grave.

text.NRSV: Are not the days of my life few? Let me alone, that I may find a little comfort
text.Rotherham: Are not my days, few?—then forbear, and set me aside, that I may brighten up for a little;
text.KJV: Are not my days few? cease then, and let me alone, that I may take comfort a little,
text.NABRE: Are not my days few? Stop! Let me alone, that I may recover a little
text.DRB: Shall not the fewness of my days be ended shortly? suffer me therefore, that I may a little lament my sorrow:

text.NRSV: before I go, never to return, to the land of gloom and deep darkness,
text.Rotherham: Before I go, and not return, unto a land of darkness and death-shade:
text.KJV: Before I go whence I shall not return, even to the land of darkness and the shadow of death;
text.NABRE: Before I go whence I shall not return, to the land of darkness and of gloom,
text.DRB: Before I go, and return not, unto the dark land, that is covered with the mist of death:

text.NRSV: the land of utter gloom and chaos, where light is as darkness.”
text.Rotherham: A land of obscurity, like thick darkness, of death-shade and disorder, and which shineth like thick darkness.
text.KJV: A land of darkness, as darkness itself; and of the shadow of death, without any order, and where the light is as darkness.
text.NABRE: The dark, disordered land where darkness is the only light.
text.DRB: Aland of misery and darkness, where is the shadow of death, and no order, but everlasting horror inhabiteth.

text.NRSV: Then Zophar the Naamathite answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Zophar the Naamathite, and said:—
text.KJV: Then answered Zophar the Naamathite, and said,
text.NABRE: And Zophar the Naamathite answered and said:
text.DRB: But Sophar the Naamathite answering, said:

text.NRSV: “Should a multitude of words go unanswered, and should one full of talk be vindicated?
text.Rotherham: Should, the multitude of words, not be answered? Or should, a man full of talk, be justified?
text.KJV: Should not the multitude of words be answered? and should a man full of talk be justified?
text.NABRE: Should not many words be answered, or must the garrulous man necessarily be right?
text.DRB: Why, shall he that speaketh many things, not hear also? Or shall a man full of words be justified?

text.NRSV: Should your babble put others to silence, and when you mock, shall no one shame you?
text.Rotherham: Shall, thy pratings, cause men to hold their peace? When thou hast mocked, shall there be none to put thee to shame?
text.KJV: Should thy lies make men hold their peace? and when thou mockest, shall no man make thee ashamed?
text.NABRE: Shall your babblings keep others silent, and shall you deride and no one give rebuke?
text.DRB: To thee only shall men hold their peace? & when thou hast mocked others, shalt thou be confuted of none?

text.NRSV: For you say, ‘My conduct is pure, and I am clean in God’s sight.’
text.Rotherham: Since thou hast said, Right is my doctrine, and pure am I in his eyes.
text.KJV: For thou hast said, My doctrine is pure, and I am clean in thine eyes.
text.NABRE: Shall you say: “My teaching is pure, and I am clean in your sight”?
text.DRB: For thou hast said: My word is pure, and I am clean in thy sight.

text.NRSV: But O that God would speak, and open his lips to you,
text.Rotherham: But, in very deed, oh that GOD would speak, that he would open his lips with thee:
text.KJV: But oh that God would speak, and open his lips against thee;
text.NABRE: But oh, that God would speak, and open his lips against you,
text.DRB: And I would wish that God would speak with thee, and would open his lips to thee.

text.NRSV: and that he would tell you the secrets of wisdom! For wisdom is many-sided. Know then that God exacts of you less than your guilt deserves.
text.Rotherham: That he would declare to thee the secrets of wisdom, for they are double to that which actually is,—Know then that GOD could bring into forgetfulness for thee, a portion of thine iniquity.
text.KJV: And that he would shew thee the secrets of wisdom, that they are double to that which is! Know therefore that God exacteth of thee less than thine iniquity deserveth.
text.NABRE: And tell you the secrets of wisdom, for good sense has two sides; So you might learn that God overlooks some of your sinfulness.
text.DRB: That he might shew thee the secrets of wisdom, and that his law is manifold, and thou mightest understand that thou art exacted much lesser things of him than thy iniquity deserveth.

text.NRSV: “Can you find out the deep things of God? Can you find out the limit of the Almighty?
text.Rotherham: The hidden depth of GOD canst thou discover? Or, unto the furthest limit of the Almighty, canst thou attain?
text.KJV: Canst thou by searching find out God? canst thou find out the Almighty unto perfection?
text.NABRE: Can you find out the depths of God? or find out the perfection of the Almighty?
text.DRB: Peradventure thou wilt comprehend the steps of God, and wilt find out the Omnipotent perfectly?

text.NRSV: It is higher than heaven—what can you do? Deeper than Sheol—what can you know?
text.Rotherham: The heights of the heavens, what canst thou do? Depths deeper than hades, what canst thou know?
text.KJV: It is as high as heaven; what canst thou do? deeper than hell; what canst thou know?
text.NABRE: It is higher than the heavens; what can you do? It is deeper than Sheol; what can you know?
text.DRB: He is higher than heaven, and what wilt thou do? Deeper than hell, and how wilt thou know?

text.NRSV: Its measure is longer than the earth, and broader than the sea.
text.Rotherham: Longer than the earth, is the measure thereof, and broader than the sea.
text.KJV: The measure thereof is longer than the earth, and broader than the sea.
text.NABRE: It is longer than the earth in measure, and broader than the sea.
text.DRB: The measure of him is longer than the earth, & broader than the sea.

text.NRSV: If he passes through, and imprisons, and assembles for judgment, who can hinder him?
text.Rotherham: If he sweep on, or shut up, or call together, Who then shall hinder him?
text.KJV: If he cut off, and shut up, or gather together, then who can hinder him?
text.NABRE: If he should seize and imprison or call to judgment, who then could turn him back?
text.DRB: If he shall overthrow all things, or shall straiten them into one, who shall say against him?

text.NRSV: For he knows those who are worthless; when he sees iniquity, will he not consider it?
text.Rotherham: For, he, knoweth men of falsity, and seeth iniquity, and him that doth not diligently consider.
text.KJV: For he knoweth vain men: he seeth wickedness also; will he not then consider it?
text.NABRE: For he knows the worthless and sees iniquity; will he then ignore it?
text.DRB: For he knoweth the vanity of men, & seeing iniquity doth he not consider?

text.NRSV: But a stupid person will get understanding, when a wild donkey is born human.
text.Rotherham: But, an empty person, will get sense, when, a wild ass’s colt, is born a man!
text.KJV: For vain man would be wise, though man be born like a wild ass’s colt.
text.NABRE: An empty head will gain understanding, when a colt of a wild jackass is born human.
text.DRB: Avain man is extolled into pride, & thinketh himself born free as a wild ass's colt.

text.NRSV: “If you direct your heart rightly, you will stretch out your hands toward him.
text.Rotherham: If, thou, hast prepared thy heart, and wilt spread forth, unto him, thy hands—
text.KJV: If thou prepare thine heart, and stretch out thine hands toward him;
text.NABRE: If you set your heart aright and stretch out your hands toward him,
text.DRB: But thou hast confirmed thy heart, & hast spread thy hands to him.

text.NRSV: If iniquity is in your hand, put it far away, and do not let wickedness reside in your tents.
text.Rotherham: If, iniquity, be in thy hand, Put it far away, and let there not dwell in thy tents perversity,
text.KJV: If iniquity be in thine hand, put it far away, and let not wickedness dwell in thy tabernacles.
text.NABRE: If iniquity is in your hand, remove it, and do not let injustice dwell in your tent,
text.DRB: If thou shalt take away from thee the iniquity that is in thy hand, & injustice remain not in thy tabernacle:

text.NRSV: Surely then you will lift up your face without blemish; you will be secure, and will not fear.
text.Rotherham: Surely, then, shalt thou lift up thy face free from blemish, and shalt be established, and not fear.
text.KJV: For then shalt thou lift up thy face without spot; yea, thou shalt be stedfast, and shalt not fear:
text.NABRE: Surely then you may lift up your face in innocence; you may stand firm and unafraid.
text.DRB: Then mayst thou lift up thy face without spot, & thou shalt be stable, & shalt not fear.

text.NRSV: You will forget your misery; you will remember it as waters that have passed away.
text.Rotherham: For, now, shalt thou forget, sorrow, Like waters passed away, shalt thou remember it.
text.KJV: Because thou shalt forget thy misery, and remember it as waters that pass away:
text.NABRE: For then you shall forget your misery, like water that has ebbed away you shall regard it.
text.DRB: Thou shalt also forget misery, & shalt remember it as waters that are passed.

text.NRSV: And your life will be brighter than the noonday; its darkness will be like the morning.
text.Rotherham: Above high noon, shall rise life’s continuance, Darkness, like a morning, shall appear,
text.KJV: And thine age shall be clearer than the noonday; thou shalt shine forth, thou shalt be as the morning.
text.NABRE: Then your life shall be brighter than the noonday; its gloom shall become like the morning,
text.DRB: And the brightness as it were of noon days, shall arise to thee at evening: and when thou shalt think thyself consumed, thou shalt rise as the day star.

text.NRSV: And you will have confidence, because there is hope; you will be protected and take your rest in safety.
text.Rotherham: And thou shalt he confident, that there is hope, and, when thou hast searched, securely shalt thou lie down;
text.KJV: And thou shalt be secure, because there is hope; yea, thou shalt dig about thee, and thou shalt take thy rest in safety.
text.NABRE: And you shall be secure, because there is hope; you shall look round you and lie down in safety;
text.DRB: And thou shalt have confidence, hope being set before thee, & buried thou shalt sleep secure.

text.NRSV: You will lie down, and no one will make you afraid; many will entreat your favor.
text.Rotherham: And shalt rest, with none to put thee in terror,—and many shall entreat thy favour.
text.KJV: Also thou shalt lie down, and none shall make thee afraid; yea, many shall make suit unto thee.
text.NABRE: you shall lie down and no one will disturb you. Many shall entreat your favor,
text.DRB: Thou shalt rest, and there shall be none to terrify thee: and very many shall beseech thy face.

text.NRSV: But the eyes of the wicked will fail; all way of escape will be lost to them, and their hope is to breathe their last.”
text.Rotherham: But, the eyes of the lawless, shall fail,—and, place of refuge, shall have vanished from them, and, their hope, be a breathing out of life.
text.KJV: But the eyes of the wicked shall fail, and they shall not escape, and their hope shall be as the giving up of the ghost.
text.NABRE: but the wicked, looking on, shall be consumed with envy. Escape shall be cut off from them, their only hope their last breath.
text.DRB: But the eyes of the impious shall decay, and escape shall fail them, and their hope the abomination of the soul.

text.NRSV: Then Job answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Job, and said:—
text.KJV: And Job answered and said,
text.NABRE: Then Job answered and said:
text.DRB: But Job answering, said:

text.NRSV: “No doubt you are the people, and wisdom will die with you.
text.Rotherham: Of a truth, ye, are the people, and, with you, wisdom, will die.
text.KJV: No doubt but ye are the people, and wisdom shall die with you.
text.NABRE: No doubt you are the people with whom wisdom shall die!
text.DRB: Are you then men alone, & shall wisdom die with you?

text.NRSV: But I have understanding as well as you; I am not inferior to you. Who does not know such things as these?
text.Rotherham: I also, have a mind like you, I, fall not short, of you, But who hath not such things as these?
text.KJV: But I have understanding as well as you; I am not inferior to you: yea, who knoweth not such things as these?
text.NABRE: But I have intelligence as well as you; I do not fall short of you; for who does not know such things as these?
text.DRB: I also have a heart even as you, neither am I inferior to you: for who is ignorant of these things, which you know?

text.NRSV: I am a laughingstock to my friends; I, who called upon God and he answered me, a just and blameless man, I am a laughingstock.
text.Rotherham: A laughing-stock to one’s neighbour, do I become, one who hath called upon GOD and he hath answered him! A laughing-stock—a righteous man without blame!
text.KJV: I am as one mocked of his neighbour, who calleth upon God, and he answereth him: the just upright man is laughed to scorn.
text.NABRE: I have become the sport of my neighbors: “The one whom God answers when he calls upon him, The just, the perfect man,” is a laughingstock;
text.DRB: He that is mocked of his friend as I, shall invocate God, and he will hear him: for the simplicity of the just man is scorned.

text.NRSV: Those at ease have contempt for misfortune, but it is ready for those whose feet are unstable.
text.Rotherham: For ruin, there is contempt, in the thought of the man at ease,—ready, for such as are of faltering foot!
text.KJV: He that is ready to slip with his feet is as a lamp despised in the thought of him that is at ease.
text.NABRE: The undisturbed esteem my downfall a disgrace such as awaits unsteady feet;
text.DRB: The lamp contemned in the cogitations of the rich, is prepared to the time appointed.

text.NRSV: The tents of robbers are at peace, and those who provoke God are secure, who bring their god in their hands.
text.Rotherham: At peace are the tents that belong to the spoilers, and there is security to them who provoke GOD, To him who bringeth a god in his hand.
text.KJV: The tabernacles of robbers prosper, and they that provoke God are secure; into whose hand God bringeth abundantly.
text.NABRE: Yet the tents of robbers are prosperous, and those who provoke God are secure, whom God has in his power.
text.DRB: The tabernacles of robbers abound, and they provoke God boldly, whereas he hath given all things into their hands.

text.NRSV: “But ask the animals, and they will teach you; the birds of the air, and they will tell you;
text.Rotherham: But, in very deed, ask, I pray thee, the beasts, and they will teach thee, and the bird of the heavens, and it will tell thee;
text.KJV: But ask now the beasts, and they shall teach thee; and the fowls of the air, and they shall tell thee:
text.NABRE: But now ask the beasts to teach you, the birds of the air to tell you;
text.DRB: For ask the beasts, and they shall teach thee: and the fowls of the air, and they shall tell thee.

text.NRSV: or ask the plants of the earth, and they will teach you; and the fish of the sea will declare to you.
text.Rotherham: Or address the earth, and it will teach thee, and the fishes of the sea, will recount it to thee:
text.KJV: Or speak to the earth, and it shall teach thee: and the fishes of the sea shall declare unto thee.
text.NABRE: Or speak to the earth to instruct you, and the fish of the sea to inform you.
text.DRB: Speak to the earth, and it shall answer thee, and the fishes of the sea shall tell.

text.NRSV: Who among all these does not know that the hand of the Lord has done this?
text.Rotherham: Who knoweth not, among all these, that, the hand of Yahweh, hath done this?
text.KJV: Who knoweth not in all these that the hand of the Lord hath wrought this?
text.NABRE: Which of all these does not know that the hand of God has done this?
text.DRB: Who is ignorant that the hand of our Lord hath made all these things?

text.NRSV: In his hand is the life of every living thing and the breath of every human being.
text.Rotherham: In whose hand is the soul of every living thing, and the spirit of all the flesh of men.
text.KJV: In whose hand is the soul of every living thing, and the breath of all mankind.
text.NABRE: In his hand is the soul of every living thing, and the life breath of all mortal flesh.
text.DRB: In whose hand is the soul of every living thing, and the spirit of all the flesh of man.

text.NRSV: Does not the ear test words as the palate tastes food?
text.Rotherham: Doth not, the ear, try, words? even as, the palate, tasteth for itself, food?
text.KJV: Doth not the ear try words? and the mouth taste his meat?
text.NABRE: Does not the ear judge words as the mouth tastes food?
text.DRB: Doth not the ear discern words, and the jaws of him that eateth, the taste?

text.NRSV: Is wisdom with the aged, and understanding in length of days?
text.Rotherham: In the Ancient, is wisdom, and [in] Length of Days, understanding:
text.KJV: With the ancient is wisdom; and in length of days understanding.
text.NABRE: So with old age is wisdom, and with length of days understanding.
text.DRB: In the ancients is wisdom, and in long time prudence.

text.NRSV: “With God are wisdom and might; he has counsel and understanding.
text.Rotherham: With Him, are wisdom and strength, to Him, pertain counsel and understanding.
text.KJV: With him is wisdom and strength, he hath counsel and understanding.
text.NABRE: With him are wisdom and might; his are counsel and understanding.
text.DRB: With him is wisdom and strength, he hath counsel and understanding.

text.NRSV: If he tears down, no one can rebuild; if he imprisons, no one can set free.
text.Rotherham: Lo! He pulleth down, and it cannot be built, He closeth up over a man, and it cannot be opened:
text.KJV: Behold, he breaketh down, and it cannot be built again: he shutteth up a man, and there can be no opening.
text.NABRE: If he knocks a thing down, there is no rebuilding; if he imprisons, there is no release.
text.DRB: If he shall destroy, there is no man that can build: if he shut up a man, there is none that can open.

text.NRSV: If he withholds the waters, they dry up; if he sends them out, they overwhelm the land.
text.Rotherham: Lo! He holdeth back the waters, and they dry up, or sendeth them out, and they transform the earth:
text.KJV: Behold, he withholdeth the waters, and they dry up: also he sendeth them out, and they overturn the earth.
text.NABRE: He holds back the waters and there is drought; he sends them forth and they overwhelm the land.
text.DRB: If he hold in the waters, all things shall be dried: and if he send them forth, they shall overthrow the earth.

text.NRSV: With him are strength and wisdom; the deceived and the deceiver are his.
text.Rotherham: With Him, is strength and effective wisdom, to Him, belong he that erreth, and he that causeth to err.
text.KJV: With him is strength and wisdom: the deceived and the deceiver are his.
text.NABRE: With him are strength and prudence; the misled and the misleaders are his.
text.DRB: With him is strength and wisdom: he knoweth both the deceiver, and him that is deceived.

text.NRSV: He leads counselors away stripped, and makes fools of judges.
text.Rotherham: Who leadeth away counsellors [as] a spoil, and, judges, He befooleth:
text.KJV: He leadeth counsellors away spoiled, and maketh the judges fools.
text.NABRE: He sends counselors away barefoot, makes fools of judges.
text.DRB: He bringeth counsellers to a foolish end, & judges to astonishment.

text.NRSV: He looses the sash of kings, and binds a waistcloth on their loins.
text.Rotherham: The fetters of kings, He looseth, or hath bound a slave’s waistcloth about their loins:
text.KJV: He looseth the bond of kings, and girdeth their loins with a girdle.
text.NABRE: He loosens the belt of kings, ties a waistcloth on their loins.
text.DRB: He looseth the belt of kings, & girdeth their reins with a cord.

text.NRSV: He leads priests away stripped, and overthrows the mighty.
text.Rotherham: Who leadeth away priests [as] a spoil, and, men firmly seated, He overturneth:
text.KJV: He leadeth princes away spoiled, and overthroweth the mighty.
text.NABRE: He sends priests away barefoot, leads the powerful astray.
text.DRB: He leadeth away Priests without glory, & supplanteth the great men.

text.NRSV: He deprives of speech those who are trusted, and takes away the discernment of the elders.
text.Rotherham: Setting aside the speech of the trusty, and, the discernment of elders, He taketh away:
text.KJV: He removeth away the speech of the trusty, and taketh away the understanding of the aged.
text.NABRE: He silences the trusted adviser, takes discretion from the elders.
text.DRB: Changing the lip of the true, and taking away the doctrine of the ancients.

text.NRSV: He pours contempt on princes, and looses the belt of the strong.
text.Rotherham: Pouring contempt upon nobles, and, the girdle of the mighty, hath He loosed:
text.KJV: He poureth contempt upon princes, and weakeneth the strength of the mighty.
text.NABRE: He pours shame on nobles, the waistband of the strong he loosens.
text.DRB: He poureth out contempt upon Princes, relieving them that had been oppressed.

text.NRSV: He uncovers the deeps out of darkness, and brings deep darkness to light.
text.Rotherham: Laying open deep things, out of darkness, and bringing out to light, the death-shade:
text.KJV: He discovereth deep things out of darkness, and bringeth out to light the shadow of death.
text.NABRE: He uncovers deep things from the darkness, brings the gloom into the light.
text.DRB: Who revealeth profound things out of darkness, and bringeth forth the shadow of death into light.

text.NRSV: He makes nations great, then destroys them; he enlarges nations, then leads them away.
text.Rotherham: Who giveth greatness to nations, or destroyeth them, Who spreadeth out nations, or leadeth them into exile:
text.KJV: He increaseth the nations, and destroyeth them: he enlargeth the nations, and straiteneth them again.
text.NABRE: He makes nations great and destroys them, spreads peoples abroad and abandons them.
text.DRB: Who multiplieth nations, & destroyeth them, and restoreth the destroyed whole again.

text.NRSV: He strips understanding from the leaders of the earth, and makes them wander in a pathless waste.
text.Rotherham: Who taketh away the sense of the chiefs of the people of the earth, and hath caused them to wander in a pathless waste:
text.KJV: He taketh away the heart of the chief of the people of the earth, and causeth them to wander in a wilderness where there is no way.
text.NABRE: He takes understanding from the leaders of the land, makes them wander in a pathless desert.
text.DRB: Who changeth the heart of the Princes of the people of the earth, and deceiveth them, that they may go in vain where is no passage.

text.NRSV: They grope in the dark without light; he makes them stagger like a drunkard.
text.Rotherham: They grope about in the dark, having no light, and He hath made them to reel, like a drunken man.
text.KJV: They grope in the dark without light, and he maketh them to stagger like a drunken man.
text.NABRE: They grope in the darkness without light; he makes them wander like drunkards.
text.DRB: They shall grope as in the dark, and not in the light, and he shall make them go amiss as drunken men.

text.NRSV: “Look, my eye has seen all this, my ear has heard and understood it.
text.Rotherham: Lo! all [this], hath mine own eye seen,—Mine ear hath heard and understood it:
text.KJV: Lo, mine eye hath seen all this, mine ear hath heard and understood it.
text.NABRE: All this my eye has seen; my ear has heard and perceived it.
text.DRB: Behold mine eye hath seen all these things, and mine ear hath heard, and I have understood every thing.

text.NRSV: What you know, I also know; I am not inferior to you.
text.Rotherham: Just as ye know, I too, know, I, fall not short, of you.
text.KJV: What ye know, the same do I know also: I am not inferior unto you.
text.NABRE: What you know, I also know; I do not fall short of you.
text.DRB: According to your knowledge I also do know: neither am I inferior to you.

text.NRSV: But I would speak to the Almighty, and I desire to argue my case with God.
text.Rotherham: But indeed, I, unto the Almighty, would speak, and, to direct my argument unto GOD, would I be well pleased.
text.KJV: Surely I would speak to the Almighty, and I desire to reason with God.
text.NABRE: But I would speak with the Almighty; I want to argue with God.
text.DRB: But yet I will speak to the Omnipotent, and I covet to dispute with God.

text.NRSV: As for you, you whitewash with lies; all of you are worthless physicians.
text.Rotherham: For, in truth, ye, do besmear with falsehood, Worthless physicians, all of you!
text.KJV: But ye are forgers of lies, ye are all physicians of no value.
text.NABRE: But you gloss over falsehoods, you are worthless physicians, every one of you!
text.DRB: First shewing you to be forgers of lying, and maintainers of perverse opinions.

text.NRSV: If you would only keep silent, that would be your wisdom!
text.Rotherham: Oh that ye would, altogether hold your peace, and it should serve you for wisdom!
text.KJV: O that ye would altogether hold your peace! and it should be your wisdom.
text.NABRE: Oh, that you would be altogether silent; that for you would be wisdom!
text.DRB: And would God ye would hold your peace, that you might be thought to be wise men.

text.NRSV: Hear now my reasoning, and listen to the pleadings of my lips.
text.Rotherham: Hear, I pray you, the argument of my mouth, and, to the pleadings of my lips, give heed:—
text.KJV: Hear now my reasoning, and hearken to the pleadings of my lips.
text.NABRE: Hear now my argument and listen to the accusations from my lips.
text.DRB: Hear ye therefore my correptions, and attend the judgment of my lips.

text.NRSV: Will you speak falsely for God, and speak deceitfully for him?
text.Rotherham: Is it, for God, ye would speak perversely? And, for him, would ye speak deceit:
text.KJV: Will ye speak wickedly for God? and talk deceitfully for him?
text.NABRE: Is it for God that you speak falsehood? Is it for him that you utter deceit?
text.DRB: Hath God need of your lie, that for him you speak guiles?

text.NRSV: Will you show partiality toward him, will you plead the case for God?
text.Rotherham: Even, for him, would ye be partial? Or, for GOD, would ye [so] plead?
text.KJV: Will ye accept his person? will ye contend for God?
text.NABRE: Is it for him that you show partiality? Do you make accusations on behalf of God?
text.DRB: Do you take his person, and do you endeavour to judge for God?

text.NRSV: Will it be well with you when he searches you out? Or can you deceive him, as one person deceives another?
text.Rotherham: Would it be well, when he searched you out? Or, as one might jest with a mortal, would ye jest, with him?
text.KJV: Is it good that he should search you out? or as one man mocketh another, do ye so mock him?
text.NABRE: Will it be well when he shall search you out? Can you deceive him as you do a mere human being?
text.DRB: Or shall it please him from whom nothing can be concealed, or shall he be deceived as a man, with your fraudulent dealings?

text.NRSV: He will surely rebuke you if in secret you show partiality.
text.Rotherham: He will, severely rebuke, you, if ye are secretly partial.
text.KJV: He will surely reprove you, if ye do secretly accept persons.
text.NABRE: He will openly rebuke you if in secret you show partiality.
text.DRB: He shall reprove you, because in secret you take his person.

text.NRSV: Will not his majesty terrify you, and the dread of him fall upon you?
text.Rotherham: Shall not, his majesty, overwhelm you? and, the dread of him, fall upon you?
text.KJV: Shall not his excellency make you afraid? and his dread fall upon you?
text.NABRE: Surely his majesty will frighten you and dread of him fall upon you.
text.DRB: Forthwith as he shall move himself, he shall trouble you: and his terror shall come violently upon you.

text.NRSV: Your maxims are proverbs of ashes, your defenses are defenses of clay.
text.Rotherham: Are not your memorable sayings, proverbs of ashes? Breastworks of clay, your breastworks?
text.KJV: Your remembrances are like unto ashes, your bodies to bodies of clay.
text.NABRE: Your reminders are ashy maxims, your fabrications mounds of clay.
text.DRB: Your memory shall be compared to ashes, and your necks shall be brought into clay.

text.NRSV: “Let me have silence, and I will speak, and let come on me what may.
text.Rotherham: Quietly let me alone, that, I, may speak out, then let come on me, what may.
text.KJV: Hold your peace, let me alone, that I may speak, and let come on me what will.
text.NABRE: Be silent! Let me alone that I may speak, no matter what happens to me.
text.DRB: Hold your peace a little while, that I may speak whatsoever my mind shall prompt me.

text.NRSV: I will take my flesh in my teeth, and put my life in my hand.
text.Rotherham: In any case, I will take up my flesh in my teeth, and, my life, will I put in my hand:
text.KJV: Wherefore do I take my flesh in my teeth, and put my life in mine hand?
text.NABRE: I will carry my flesh between my teeth, and take my life in my hand.
text.DRB: Why do I tear my flesh with my teeth, and carry my soul in my hands?

text.NRSV: See, he will kill me; I have no hope; but I will defend my ways to his face.
text.Rotherham: Lo! he may slay me, [yet], for him, will I wait,—Nevertheless, my ways—unto his face, will I show to be right:
text.KJV: Though he slay me, yet will I trust in him: but I will maintain mine own ways before him.
text.NABRE: Slay me though he might, I will wait for him; I will defend my conduct before him.
text.DRB: Although he shall kill me, I will trust in him: but yet I will reprove my ways in his sight.

text.NRSV: This will be my salvation, that the godless shall not come before him.
text.Rotherham: Even he, will be on my side—unto salvation, For, not before his face, shall any impious person come.
text.KJV: He also shall be my salvation: for an hypocrite shall not come before him.
text.NABRE: This shall be my salvation: no impious man can come into his presence.
text.DRB: And he shall be my saviour: for no hypocrite shall come in his sight.

text.NRSV: Listen carefully to my words, and let my declaration be in your ears.
text.Rotherham: Hear ye patiently my speech, and be my declaration in your ears.
text.KJV: Hear diligently my speech, and my declaration with your ears.
text.NABRE: Pay close attention to my speech, give my statement a hearing.
text.DRB: Hear ye my word, and receive the obscure sayings with your ears.

text.NRSV: I have indeed prepared my case; I know that I shall be vindicated.
text.Rotherham: Lo! I pray you, I have set forth in order a plea, I know that, I, shall be found right.
text.KJV: Behold now, I have ordered my cause; I know that I shall be justified.
text.NABRE: Behold, I have prepared my case, I know that I am in the right.
text.DRB: If I shall be judged, I know that I shall be found just.

text.NRSV: Who is there that will contend with me? For then I would be silent and die.
text.Rotherham: Who is it that shall contend with me? For, now, if I should hold my peace, why! I should breathe my last!
text.KJV: Who is he that will plead with me? for now, if I hold my tongue, I shall give up the ghost.
text.NABRE: If anyone can make a case against me, then I shall be silent and expire.
text.DRB: What is he that will be judged with me? Let him come: why am I consumed holding my peace?

text.NRSV: Only grant two things to me, then I will not hide myself from your face:
text.Rotherham: Only, two things, do thou not with me, then, from thy face, will I not hide me:—
text.KJV: Only do not two things unto me: then will I not hide myself from thee.
text.NABRE: Two things only do not use against me, then from your presence I need not hide:
text.DRB: Two things only do not to me, and then shall I not be hid from thy face:

text.NRSV: withdraw your hand far from me, and do not let dread of you terrify me.
text.Rotherham: Thy hand—from off me, take thou far away, and, thy terror, let it not startle me!
text.KJV: Withdraw thine hand far from me: and let not thy dread make me afraid.
text.NABRE: Withdraw your hand far from me, do not let the terror of you frighten me.
text.DRB: Make thy hand far from me, and let not thy fear terrify me.

text.NRSV: Then call, and I will answer; or let me speak, and you reply to me.
text.Rotherham: Then call thou, and, I, will answer, Or I will speak, and reply thou unto me.
text.KJV: Then call thou, and I will answer: or let me speak, and answer thou me.
text.NABRE: Then call me, and I will respond; or let me speak first, and answer me.
text.DRB: Call me, and I will answer thee: or else I will speak, and do thou answer me.

text.NRSV: How many are my iniquities and my sins? Make me know my transgression and my sin.
text.Rotherham: How many are mine iniquities and sins? My transgression and my sin, let me know!
text.KJV: How many are mine iniquities and sins? make me to know my transgression and my sin.
text.NABRE: What are my faults and my sins? My misdeed, my sin make known to me!
text.DRB: How great iniquities and sins I have, my wicked deeds and my offences shew thou me.

text.NRSV: Why do you hide your face, and count me as your enemy?
text.Rotherham: Wherefore, thy face, shouldst thou hide? Or count me, as an enemy to thee?
text.KJV: Wherefore hidest thou thy face, and holdest me for thine enemy?
text.NABRE: Why do you hide your face and consider me your enemy?
text.DRB: Why hidest thou thy face, and thinkest me thine enemy?

text.NRSV: Will you frighten a settled leaf and pursue dry chaff?
text.Rotherham: A driven leaf, wilt thou cause to tremble? Or, dry stubble, wilt thou pursue?
text.KJV: Wilt thou break a leaf driven to and fro? and wilt thou pursue the dry stubble?
text.NABRE: Will you harass a wind-driven leaf or pursue a withered straw?
text.DRB: Against the leaf, that is violently taken with the wind, thou shewest thy might, and persecutest dry stubble.
