text.NRSV: For you write bitter things against me, and make me inherit the iniquities of my youth.
text.Rotherham: For thou writest, against me, bitter things, and dost make me inherit the iniquities of my youth;
text.KJV: For thou writest bitter things against me, and makest me to possess the iniquities of my youth.
text.NABRE: For you draw up bitter indictments against me, and punish in me the faults of my youth.
text.DRB: For thou writest bitterness against me, and wilt consume me with the sins of my youth.

text.NRSV: You put my feet in the stocks, and watch all my paths; you set a bound to the soles of my feet.
text.Rotherham: And thou dost put—in the stocks—my feet, and observest all my paths, Against the roots of my feet, thou dost cut out a bound;
text.KJV: Thou puttest my feet also in the stocks, and lookest narrowly unto all my paths; thou settest a print upon the heels of my feet.
text.NABRE: You put my feet in the stocks; you watch all my paths and trace out all my footsteps,
text.DRB: Thou hast put my feet in the stocks, and hast observed all my paths, and hast considered the steps of my feet.

text.NRSV: One wastes away like a rotten thing, like a garment that is moth-eaten.”
text.Rotherham: And, a man himself, as a rotten thing, weareth out, as a garment which the moth hath eaten.
text.KJV: And he, as a rotten thing, consumeth, as a garment that is moth eaten.
text.NABRE: Though I wear out like a leather bottle, like a garment the moth has consumed.
text.DRB: Who as rottenness am to be consumed, and as a garment, that is eaten of the moth.

text.NRSV: “A mortal, born of woman, few of days and full of trouble,
text.Rotherham: Man that is born of a woman, is of few days, and full of trouble:
text.KJV: Man that is born of a woman is of few days, and full of trouble.
text.NABRE: Man born of woman is short-lived and full of trouble,
text.DRB: Man born of woman, living a short time, is replenished with many miseries.

text.NRSV: comes up like a flower and withers, flees like a shadow and does not last.
text.Rotherham: As a flower, he cometh forth—and fadeth, He fleeth also as a shadow, and continueth not.
text.KJV: He cometh forth like a flower, and is cut down: he fleeth also as a shadow, and continueth not.
text.NABRE: Like a flower that springs up and fades, swift as a shadow that does not abide.
text.DRB: Who as a flower cometh forth and is destroyed, and flyeth as a shadow, and never abideth in the same state.

text.NRSV: Do you fix your eyes on such a one? Do you bring me into judgment with you?
text.Rotherham: And yet upon such a one as this, hast thou opened thine eye? And, him, wouldst thou bring into judgment with thee?
text.KJV: And dost thou open thine eyes upon such an one, and bringest me into judgment with thee?
text.NABRE: Upon such a one will you set your eyes, bringing me into judgment before you?
text.DRB: And doest thou count it a worthy thing to open thine eyes upon such an one, and to bring him with thee into judgment?

text.NRSV: Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? No one can.
text.Rotherham: Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? Not one!
text.KJV: Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? not one.
text.NABRE: Can anyone make the unclean clean? No one can.
text.DRB: Who can make clean him that is conceived of unclean seed? Is it not thou which only art?

text.NRSV: Since their days are determined, and the number of their months is known to you, and you have appointed the bounds that they cannot pass,
text.Rotherham: If determined am his days, the number of his months, is with thee, Fixed times for him, thou hast appointed and he cannot go beyond.
text.KJV: Seeing his days are determined, the number of his months are with thee, thou hast appointed his bounds that he cannot pass;
text.NABRE: Since his days are determined— you know the number of his months; you have fixed the limit which he cannot pass—
text.DRB: The days of man are short, and the number of his months is with thee, thou hast appointed his limits which cannot be passed.

text.NRSV: look away from them, and desist, that they may enjoy, like laborers, their days.
text.Rotherham: Look sway from him, that he may rest, Till he shall pay off, as a hireling, his day.
text.KJV: Turn from him, that he may rest, till he shall accomplish, as an hireling, his day.
text.NABRE: Look away from him and let him be, while, like a hireling, he completes his day.
text.DRB: Depart a little from him, that he may rest, until his day wished for come, even as the hired man's.

text.NRSV: “For there is hope for a tree, if it is cut down, that it will sprout again, and that its shoots will not cease.
text.Rotherham: Though there is—for a tree—hope,—if it should be cut down, that, again, it will grow, and, the tender branch thereof, will not cease;
text.KJV: For there is hope of a tree, if it be cut down, that it will sprout again, and that the tender branch thereof will not cease.
text.NABRE: For a tree there is hope; if it is cut down, it will sprout again, its tender shoots will not cease.
text.DRB: Atree hath hope: if it be cut, it waxeth green again, and the boughs thereof spring.

text.NRSV: Though its root grows old in the earth, and its stump dies in the ground,
text.Rotherham: If its root, should become old in the earth, and, in the dust, its stock should die:
text.KJV: Though the root thereof wax old in the earth, and the stock thereof die in the ground;
text.NABRE: Even though its root grow old in the earth and its stump die in the dust,
text.DRB: If his root be old in the earth, and the trunk thereof be dead in the dust,

text.NRSV: yet at the scent of water it will bud and put forth branches like a young plant.
text.Rotherham: Through the scent of water, it may break forth, and produce branches like a sapling,
text.KJV: Yet through the scent of water it will bud, and bring forth boughs like a plant.
text.NABRE: Yet at the first whiff of water it sprouts and puts forth branches like a young plant.
text.DRB: At the scent of water it shall spring, and bring forth leaves, as when it was first planted.

text.NRSV: But mortals die, and are laid low; humans expire, and where are they?
text.Rotherham: Yet, man, dieth, and is prostrate, Yea the son of earth doth cease to breathe, and where is he?
text.KJV: But man dieth, and wasteth away: yea, man giveth up the ghost, and where is he?
text.NABRE: But when a man dies, all vigor leaves him; when a mortal expires, where then is he?
text.DRB: But when man shall be dead, and naked, and consumed, where is he I pray?

text.NRSV: As waters fail from a lake, and a river wastes away and dries up,
text.Rotherham: Waters, have failed from, the sea, and, a river, may waste and dry up;
text.KJV: As the waters fail from the sea, and the flood decayeth and drieth up:
text.NABRE: As when the waters of a lake fail, or a stream shrivels and dries up,
text.DRB: As if the waters should depart out of the sea, and a river made empty should be dried up.

text.NRSV: so mortals lie down and do not rise again; until the heavens are no more, they will not awake or be roused out of their sleep.
text.Rotherham: So, a man, hath lain down, and shall not arise, until there are no heavens, they shall not awake, nor be roused up out of their sleep.
text.KJV: So man lieth down, and riseth not: till the heavens be no more, they shall not awake, nor be raised out of their sleep.
text.NABRE: So mortals lie down, never to rise. Until the heavens are no more, they shall not awake, nor be roused out of their sleep.
text.DRB: So man when he is asleep shall not rise again, till heaven perish, he shall not awake, nor rise up out of his sleep.

text.NRSV: O that you would hide me in Sheol, that you would conceal me until your wrath is past, that you would appoint me a set time, and remember me!
text.Rotherham: Oh that, in hades, thou wouldst hide me! that thou wouldst keep me secret, until the turn of thine anger, that thou wouldst set for me a fixed time, and remember me:
text.KJV: O that thou wouldest hide me in the grave, that thou wouldest keep me secret, until thy wrath be past, that thou wouldest appoint me a set time, and remember me!
text.NABRE: Oh, that you would hide me in Sheol, shelter me till your wrath is past, fix a time to remember me!
text.DRB: Who will grant me this, that in hell thou protect me, and hide me till thy fury pass, and appoint me a time, wherein thou wilt remember me?

text.NRSV: If mortals die, will they live again? All the days of my service I would wait until my release should come.
text.Rotherham: If a man die, can he live again? All the days of my warfare, would I wait, until my relief should come:—
text.KJV: If a man die, shall he live again? all the days of my appointed time will I wait, till my change come.
text.NABRE: If a man were to die, and live again, all the days of my drudgery I would wait for my relief to come.
text.DRB: Shall man that is dead, thinkest thou, live again? All the days, in which I am now in warfare, I expect until my change do come.

text.NRSV: You would call, and I would answer you; you would long for the work of your hands.
text.Rotherham: Thou shouldst call, and, I, would answer thee,—For the work of thine own hand, thou shouldst long.
text.KJV: Thou shalt call, and I will answer thee: thou wilt have a desire to the work of thine hands.
text.NABRE: You would call, and I would answer you; you would long for the work of your hands.
text.DRB: Thou shalt call me, and I shall answer thee: to the work of thy hands thou shalt reach thy right hand.

text.NRSV: For then you would not number my steps; you would not keep watch over my sin;
text.Rotherham: For, now, my steps, thou countest, Thou wilt not pass over my sin:
text.KJV: For now thou numberest my steps: dost thou not watch over my sin?
text.NABRE: Surely then you would count my steps, and not keep watch for sin in me.
text.DRB: Thou indeed hast numbered my steps: but thou wilt spare my sins.

text.NRSV: my transgression would be sealed up in a bag, and you would cover over my iniquity.
text.Rotherham: Sealed up in a bag, is my transgression, and thou hast glued over mine iniquity.
text.KJV: My transgression is sealed up in a bag, and thou sewest up mine iniquity.
text.NABRE: My misdeeds would be sealed up in a pouch, and you would cover over my guilt.
text.DRB: Thou hast sealed my offences as it were in a bag, but hast cured mine iniquity.

text.NRSV: “But the mountain falls and crumbles away, and the rock is removed from its place;
text.Rotherham: But, in very deed, a mountain falling, will lie prostrate, or, a rock moved out of its place:
text.KJV: And surely the mountain falling cometh to nought, and the rock is removed out of his place.
text.NABRE: Mountains fall and crumble, rocks move from their place,
text.DRB: Amountain falling slideth down, and a rock is removed out of his place.

text.NRSV: the waters wear away the stones; the torrents wash away the soil of the earth; so you destroy the hope of mortals.
text.Rotherham: Stones, have been hollowed out by waters, the floods thereof wash away the dust of the earth, and, the hope of mortal man, thou hast destroyed:
text.KJV: The waters wear the stones: thou washest away the things which grow out of the dust of the earth; and thou destroyest the hope of man.
text.NABRE: And water wears away stone, and floods wash away the soil of the land— so you destroy the hope of mortals!
text.DRB: Waters make stones hollow, and with inundation the earth by little and little is consumed: and men therefore thou shalt destroy in like manner.

text.NRSV: You prevail forever against them, and they pass away; you change their countenance, and send them away.
text.Rotherham: Thou dost overpower him utterly, and he departeth, Disfiguring his face, so, hast thou sent him away.
text.KJV: Thou prevailest for ever against him, and he passeth: thou changest his countenance, and sendest him away.
text.NABRE: You prevail once for all against them and they pass on; you dismiss them with changed appearance.
text.DRB: Thou hast strengthened him a little, that he might pass away for ever: thou shalt change his face, and shalt send him forth.

text.NRSV: Their children come to honor, and they do not know it; they are brought low, and it is not perceived.
text.Rotherham: His sons, come to honour, and he knoweth it not, Or they are brought low, and he perceiveth it not of them.
text.KJV: His sons come to honour, and he knoweth it not; and they are brought low, but he perceiveth it not of them.
text.NABRE: If their children are honored, they are not aware of it; or if disgraced, they do not know about them.
text.DRB: Whether his children shall be noble, or unnoble, he shall not understand.

text.NRSV: They feel only the pain of their own bodies, and mourn only for themselves.”
text.Rotherham: But, his flesh, for himself, is in pain, and, his soul, for himself, doth mourn.
text.KJV: But his flesh upon him shall have pain, and his soul within him shall mourn.
text.NABRE: Only for themselves, their pain; only for themselves, their mourning.
text.DRB: But yet his flesh whilst he shall live shall have sorrow, and his soul shall mourn upon himself.

text.NRSV: Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Eliphaz the Temanite, and said:
text.KJV: Then answered Eliphaz the Temanite, and said,
text.NABRE: Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered and said:
text.DRB: But Eliphaz the Themanite answering, said:

text.NRSV: “Should the wise answer with windy knowledge, and fill themselves with the east wind?
text.Rotherham: Should, a wise man, answer unreal knowledge? or fill, with the east wind, his inner man?
text.KJV: Should a wise man utter vain knowledge, and fill his belly with the east wind?
text.NABRE: Does a wise man answer with windy opinions, or puff himself up with the east wind?
text.DRB: Will a wise man answer as it were speaking into the wind, and fill his stomach with burning?

text.NRSV: Should they argue in words that do not profit, or in speeches that do no good?
text.Rotherham: Disputing with discourse that doth no good, or with speech, wherein is no profit?
text.KJV: Should he reason with unprofitable talk? or with speeches wherewith he can do no good?
text.NABRE: Does he argue in speech that does not avail, and in words that are to no profit?
text.DRB: Thou reprovest him in words, that is not equal to thee, & speakest that which is not expedient for thee.

text.NRSV: But you are doing away with the fear of God, and hindering meditation before God.
text.Rotherham: But, thou, wouldst take away reverence, and wouldst attain unto meditation before GOD.
text.KJV: Yea, thou castest off fear, and restrainest prayer before God.
text.NABRE: You in fact do away with piety, you lessen devotion toward God,
text.DRB: As much as is in thee, thou hast evacuated fear, and hast taken away prayers before God.

text.NRSV: For your iniquity teaches your mouth, and you choose the tongue of the crafty.
text.Rotherham: For thine own mouth would teach thine iniquity, and thou wouldst choose the tongue of the crafty.
text.KJV: For thy mouth uttereth thine iniquity, and thou choosest the tongue of the crafty.
text.NABRE: Because your wickedness instructs your mouth, and you choose to speak like the crafty.
text.DRB: For thine iniquity hath taught thy mouth, and thou doest imitate the tongue of blasphemers.

text.NRSV: Your own mouth condemns you, and not I; your own lips testify against you.
text.Rotherham: Thine own mouth shall condemn thee, and, not I, And, thine own lips, shall testify against thee.
text.KJV: Thine own mouth condemneth thee, and not I: yea, thine own lips testify against thee.
text.NABRE: Your own mouth condemns you, not I; your own lips refute you.
text.DRB: Thine own mouth shall condemn thee, and not I: and thy lips shall answer thee.

text.NRSV: “Are you the firstborn of the human race? Were you brought forth before the hills?
text.Rotherham: The first of mankind, wast thou born? Or, before the hills, wast thou brought forth?
text.KJV: Art thou the first man that was born? or wast thou made before the hills?
text.NABRE: Were you the first to be born? Were you brought forth before the hills?
text.DRB: Wast thou the first man born, and formed before the little hills?

text.NRSV: Have you listened in the council of God? And do you limit wisdom to yourself?
text.Rotherham: In the secret council of GOD, hast thou been wont to hearken? Or canst thou attain for thyself unto wisdom?
text.KJV: Hast thou heard the secret of God? and dost thou restrain wisdom to thyself?
text.NABRE: Do you listen in on God’s council and restrict wisdom to yourself?
text.DRB: Hast thou heard God's counsel, and shall his wisdom be inferior to thee?

text.NRSV: What do you know that we do not know? What do you understand that is not clear to us?
text.Rotherham: What knowest thou, that we know not? [What] understandest thou, and the same, is not with us?
text.KJV: What knowest thou, that we know not? what understandest thou, which is not in us?
text.NABRE: What do you know that we do not know, or understand that we do not?
text.DRB: What doest thou know, that we are ignorant of? What doest thou understand that we know not?

text.NRSV: The gray-haired and the aged are on our side, those older than your father.
text.Rotherham: Both hoary and venerable, are among us, one mightier than thy father in days!
text.KJV: With us are both the grayheaded and very aged men, much elder than thy father.
text.NABRE: There are gray-haired old men among us, more advanced in years than your father.
text.DRB: There are both old men & ancients among us, much elder than thy fathers.

text.NRSV: Are the consolations of God too small for you, or the word that deals gently with you?
text.Rotherham: Too small for thee, are the consolations of GOD? or a word spoken gently with thee?
text.KJV: Are the consolations of God small with thee? is there any secret thing with thee?
text.NABRE: Are the consolations of God not enough for you, and speech that deals gently with you?
text.DRB: Is it a great matter that God should comfort thee? But thy naughty words hinder it.

text.NRSV: Why does your heart carry you away, and why do your eyes flash,
text.Rotherham: How doth thine own heart carry thee away, and how thine eyes do roll!
text.KJV: Why doth thine heart carry thee away? and what do thy eyes wink at,
text.NABRE: Why does your heart carry you away, and why do your eyes flash,
text.DRB: Why doth thy heart elevate thee, & as thinking great things, hast thou astonished eyes?

text.NRSV: so that you turn your spirit against God, and let such words go out of your mouth?
text.Rotherham: For thy spirit, replieth against GOD, and thou bringest forth—out of thy mouth—words!
text.KJV: That thou turnest thy spirit against God, and lettest such words go out of thy mouth?
text.NABRE: So that you turn your anger against God and let such words escape your mouth!
text.DRB: Why doeth thy spirit swell against God, to utter such words out of thy mouth?

text.NRSV: What are mortals, that they can be clean? Or those born of woman, that they can be righteous?
text.Rotherham: What is a mortal, that he should be pure? or that righteous should be one born of a woman?
text.KJV: What is man, that he should be clean? and he which is born of a woman, that he should be righteous?
text.NABRE: How can any mortal be blameless, anyone born of woman be righteous?
text.DRB: What is man, that he should be without spot, & that the born of a woman should appear just?

text.NRSV: God puts no trust even in his holy ones, and the heavens are not clean in his sight;
text.Rotherham: Lo! in his holy ones, he putteth not confidence, and, the heavens, are not pure in his eyes:
text.KJV: Behold, he putteth no trust in his saints; yea, the heavens are not clean in his sight.
text.NABRE: If in his holy ones God places no confidence, and if the heavens are not without blame in his sight,
text.DRB: Behold among his saints none is immutable, & the heavens are not clean in his sight.

text.NRSV: how much less one who is abominable and corrupt, one who drinks iniquity like water!
text.Rotherham: How much less when one is detested and corrupt, a man who drinketh in—like water—perversity.
text.KJV: How much more abominable and filthy is man, which drinketh iniquity like water?
text.NABRE: How much less so is the abominable and corrupt: people who drink in iniquity like water!
text.DRB: How much more is man abominable, and unprofitable, who drinketh iniquity as it were water?

text.NRSV: “I will show you; listen to me; what I have seen I will declare—
text.Rotherham: I will tell thee—hear me, Since this I have seen, I must needs declare it.
text.KJV: I will shew thee, hear me; and that which I have seen I will declare;
text.NABRE: I will show you, if you listen to me; what I have seen I will tell—
text.DRB: I will shew thee, hear me: that which I have seen I will tell thee.

text.NRSV: what sages have told, and their ancestors have not hidden,
text.Rotherham: Which, wise men, tell, and deny not [that which is] from their fathers.
text.KJV: Which wise men have told from their fathers, and have not hid it:
text.NABRE: What the wise relate and have not contradicted since the days of their ancestors,
text.DRB: Wise men confess and hide not their fathers:

text.NRSV: to whom alone the land was given, and no stranger passed among them.
text.Rotherham: To them alone, was the earth given, and no alien passed through their midst:
text.KJV: Unto whom alone the earth was given, and no stranger passed among them.
text.NABRE: To whom alone the land was given, when no foreigner moved among them:
text.DRB: to whom only the earth was given, and stranger hath not passed by them.

text.NRSV: The wicked writhe in pain all their days, through all the years that are laid up for the ruthless.
text.Rotherham: All the days of the lawless man, he, doth writhe with pain, and, the number of years, is hidden from the tyrant;
text.KJV: The wicked man travaileth with pain all his days, and the number of years is hidden to the oppressor.
text.NABRE: The wicked is in torment all his days, and limited years are in store for the ruthless;
text.DRB: The impious is proud all his days, and the number of the years of his tyranny is uncertain.

text.NRSV: Terrifying sounds are in their ears; in prosperity the destroyer will come upon them.
text.Rotherham: A noise of dreadful things, is in his ears, In prosperity, the destroyer cometh upon him;
text.KJV: A dreadful sound is in his ears: in prosperity the destroyer shall come upon him.
text.NABRE: The sound of terrors is in his ears; when all is prosperous, a spoiler comes upon him.
text.DRB: The sound of terror is always in his ears: and when there is peace, he always suspecteth treason.

text.NRSV: They do not believe that they will return out of darkness, and they are destined for the sword.
text.Rotherham: He hath no confidence to come back out of darkness, he, being destined to the power oft the sword;
text.KJV: He believeth not that he shall return out of darkness, and he is waited for of the sword.
text.NABRE: He despairs of escaping the darkness, and looks ever for the sword;
text.DRB: He believeth not that he may return from darkness to light, looking round about for the sword on every side.

text.NRSV: They wander abroad for bread, saying, ‘Where is it?’ They know that a day of darkness is ready at hand;
text.Rotherham: A wanderer, he, for bread, [saying] Where [is it]? He knoweth that, prepared by his own hand, is the day of darkness;
text.KJV: He wandereth abroad for bread, saying, Where is it? he knoweth that the day of darkness is ready at his hand.
text.NABRE: A wanderer, food for vultures, he knows destruction is imminent.
text.DRB: When he shall move himself to seek bread, he knoweth that the day of darkness is prepared in his hand.

text.NRSV: distress and anguish terrify them; they prevail against them, like a king prepared for battle.
text.Rotherham: Distress and anguish shall startle him, It shall overpower him, like a king ready for the onset:
text.KJV: Trouble and anguish shall make him afraid; they shall prevail against him, as a king ready to the battle.
text.NABRE: A day of darkness fills him with dread; distress and anguish overpower him, like a king expecting an attack.
text.DRB: Tribulation shall terrify him, and distress shall compass him, as a king that is prepared to battle.

text.NRSV: Because they stretched out their hands against God, and bid defiance to the Almighty,
text.Rotherham: Because he had stretched out—against GOD—his hand, and, against the Almighty, had been wont to behave himself proudly;
text.KJV: For he stretcheth out his hand against God, and strengtheneth himself against the Almighty.
text.NABRE: Because he has stretched out his hand against God and arrogantly challenged the Almighty,
text.DRB: For he hath stretched his hand against God, & is strengthened against the Omnipotent.

text.NRSV: running stubbornly against him with a thick-bossed shield;
text.Rotherham: He used to run against him with uplifted neck, with the stout bosses of his bucklers;
text.KJV: He runneth upon him, even on his neck, upon the thick bosses of his bucklers:
text.NABRE: Rushing defiantly against him, with the stout bosses of his shields.
text.DRB: He hath run against him with neck set upright, and is armed with a fat neck.

text.NRSV: because they have covered their faces with their fat, and gathered fat upon their loins,
text.Rotherham: For he had covered his face with his fatness, and had gathered a superabundance on his loins;
text.KJV: Because he covereth his face with his fatness, and maketh collops of fat on his flanks.
text.NABRE: Although he has covered his face with his crassness, padded his loins with blubber,
text.DRB: Fatness hath covered his face, and from his sides there hangeth tallow.

text.NRSV: they will live in desolate cities, in houses that no one should inhabit, which are destined to become heaps of ruins;
text.Rotherham: And had inhabited demolished cities, houses, wherein men would not dwell, that were destined to become heaps.
text.KJV: And he dwelleth in desolate cities, and in houses which no man inhabiteth, which are ready to become heaps.
text.NABRE: He shall dwell in ruined cities, in houses that are deserted, crumbling into rubble.
text.DRB: He hath dwelt in desolate cities, and in desert houses, that are brought into hillocks.

text.NRSV: they will not be rich, and their wealth will not endure, nor will they strike root in the earth;
text.Rotherham: He shall not be rich, nor shall his substance continue, neither shall their shadow stretch along on the earth;
text.KJV: He shall not be rich, neither shall his substance continue, neither shall he prolong the perfection thereof upon the earth.
text.NABRE: He shall not be rich, his possessions shall not endure; his property shall not spread over the land.
text.DRB: He shall not be enriched, neither shall his substance continue, neither shall he put his root in the earth.

text.NRSV: they will not escape from darkness; the flame will wither their shoots, and their blossom will be swept away by the wind.
text.Rotherham: He shall not depart out of darkness, his young branch, shall the flame dry up, and he shall depart, by the breath of his own mouth!
text.KJV: He shall not depart out of darkness; the flame shall dry up his branches, and by the breath of his mouth shall he go away.
text.NABRE: A flame shall sear his early growth, and with the wind his blossoms shall disappear.
text.DRB: He shall not depart out of darkness, the flame shall dry his boughs, and he shall be taken away with the spirit of his own mouth.

text.NRSV: Let them not trust in emptiness, deceiving themselves; for emptiness will be their recompense.
text.Rotherham: Let no one trust in him that—by vanity—is deceived, for, vanity, shall be his recompense;
text.KJV: Let not him that is deceived trust in vanity: for vanity shall be his recompence.
text.NABRE: Let him not trust in his height, misled, even though his height be like the palm tree.
text.DRB: He shall not believe vainly deceived with error, that he may be redeemed with any price.

text.NRSV: It will be paid in full before their time, and their branch will not be green.
text.Rotherham: Before his day, shall it be accomplished, with, his palm-top, not covered with leaves;
text.KJV: It shall be accomplished before his time, and his branch shall not be green.
text.NABRE: He shall wither before his time, his branches no longer green.
text.DRB: Before his days be accomplished, he shall perish: and his hands shall wither.

text.NRSV: They will shake off their unripe grape, like the vine, and cast off their blossoms, like the olive tree.
text.Rotherham: He shall wrong—like a vine—his sour grapes, and shall cast off—as an olive-tree—his blossom.
text.KJV: He shall shake off his unripe grape as the vine, and shall cast off his flower as the olive.
text.NABRE: He shall be like a vine that sheds its grapes unripened, like an olive tree casting off its blossom.
text.DRB: His cluster in the first flower shall be hurt as a vine, and as the olive tree casting his flower.

text.NRSV: For the company of the godless is barren, and fire consumes the tents of bribery.
text.Rotherham: For, the family of the impious, is unfruitful, and, a fire, hath devoured the tents of bribery;
text.KJV: For the congregation of hypocrites shall be desolate, and fire shall consume the tabernacles of bribery.
text.NABRE: For the breed of the impious shall be sterile, and fire shall consume the tents of extortioners.
text.DRB: For the congregation of the hypocrite is barren, and fire shall devour their tabernacles, which gladly take gifts.

text.NRSV: They conceive mischief and bring forth evil and their heart prepares deceit.”
text.Rotherham: Conceiving mischief, and bringing forth iniquity, yea, their inmost soul, prepareth deceit.
text.KJV: They conceive mischief, and bring forth vanity, and their belly prepareth deceit.
text.NABRE: They conceive malice, bring forth deceit, give birth to fraud.
text.DRB: He conceived sorrow, and hath brought forth iniquity, and his womb prepareth guiles.

text.NRSV: Then Job answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Job, and said:—
text.KJV: Then Job answered and said,
text.NABRE: Then Job answered and said:
text.DRB: But Job answering said:

text.NRSV: “I have heard many such things; miserable comforters are you all.
text.Rotherham: I have heard many such things, Wearisome comforters, are ye all!
text.KJV: I have heard many such things: miserable comforters are ye all.
text.NABRE: I have heard this sort of thing many times. Troublesome comforters, all of you!
text.DRB: I have heard oftentimes such things, heavy comforters you are all.

text.NRSV: Have windy words no limit? Or what provokes you that you keep on talking?
text.Rotherham: Is there to be an end to windy words? Or what so strongly exciteth thee, that thou must respond?
text.KJV: Shall vain words have an end? or what emboldeneth thee that thou answerest?
text.NABRE: Is there no end to windy words? What sickness makes you rattle on?
text.DRB: Shall words full of wind have an end? Or is any thing troublesome to thee, if thou speak?

text.NRSV: I also could talk as you do, if you were in my place; I could join words together against you, and shake my head at you.
text.Rotherham: I also, like you, could speak,—If your soul were in the place of my soul, I could string together words against you, and could therewith shake over you my head.
text.KJV: I also could speak as ye do: if your soul were in my soul’s stead, I could heap up words against you, and shake mine head at you.
text.NABRE: I also could talk as you do, were you in my place. I could declaim over you, or wag my head at you;
text.DRB: I also could speak things like to you: and would God your soul were for my soul.

text.NRSV: I could fortify you with my mouth, and the solace of my lips would assuage your pain.
text.Rotherham: I could make you determined, by my mouth, and then my lip-solace should restrain you.
text.KJV: But I would strengthen you with my mouth, and the moving of my lips should asswage your grief.
text.NABRE: I could strengthen you with talk, with mere chatter give relief.
text.DRB: I also would comfort you with words, and would wag my head upon you.

text.NRSV: “If I speak, my pain is not assuaged, and if I forbear, how much of it leaves me?
text.Rotherham: Though I do speak, unassuaged is my stinging pain,—And, if I forbear, of what am I relieved?
text.KJV: Though I speak, my grief is not asswaged: and though I forbear, what am I eased?
text.NABRE: If I speak, my pain is not relieved; if I stop speaking, nothing changes.
text.DRB: I would strengthen you with my mouth, and would move my lips, as sparing you.

text.NRSV: Surely now God has worn me out; he has made desolate all my company.
text.Rotherham: But, now, hath he wearied me, thou hast destroyed all my family;
text.KJV: But now he hath made me weary: thou hast made desolate all my company.
text.NABRE: But now he has exhausted me; you have stunned all my companions.
text.DRB: But what shall I do? If I speak, my pain will not rest: and if I hold my peace, it will not depart from me.

text.NRSV: And he has shriveled me up, which has been a witness against me; my leanness has risen up against me, and it testifies to my face.
text.Rotherham: And, having captured me, it hath served, as a witness; and so my wasting away hath risen up against me, in my face, it answereth.
text.KJV: And thou hast filled me with wrinkles, which is a witness against me: and my leanness rising up in me beareth witness to my face.
text.NABRE: You have shriveled me up; it is a witness, my gauntness rises up to testify against me;
text.DRB: But now my sorrow hath oppressed me, & all my limbs are brought to nothing.

text.NRSV: He has torn me in his wrath, and hated me; he has gnashed his teeth at me; my adversary sharpens his eyes against me.
text.Rotherham: His anger, hath torn and persecuted me, He hath gnashed upon me with his teeth, Mine adversary, hath sharpened his eyes for me.
text.KJV: He teareth me in his wrath, who hateth me: he gnasheth upon me with his teeth; mine enemy sharpeneth his eyes upon me.
text.NABRE: His wrath tears and assails me, he gnashes his teeth against me; My enemy looks daggers at me.
text.DRB: My wrinkles give testimony against me, and a false speaker is raised up against my face contradicting me.

text.NRSV: They have gaped at me with their mouths; they have struck me insolently on the cheek; they mass themselves together against me.
text.Rotherham: They have gaped upon me with their mouth, With reproach, have they smitten my cheek, Together, against me, have they closed their ranks.
text.KJV: They have gaped upon me with their mouth; they have smitten me upon the cheek reproachfully; they have gathered themselves together against me.
text.NABRE: They gape at me with their mouths; They strike me on the cheek with insults; they are all enlisted against me.
text.DRB: He hath gathered his fury upon me, and threatening me hath gnashed against me with his teeth, mine enemy hath beheld me with terrible eyes.

text.NRSV: God gives me up to the ungodly, and casts me into the hands of the wicked.
text.Rotherham: GOD doth abandon me to him that is perverse, and, into the hands of the lawless, he throweth me headlong.
text.KJV: God hath delivered me to the ungodly, and turned me over into the hands of the wicked.
text.NABRE: God has given me over to the impious; into the hands of the wicked he has cast me.
text.DRB: They have opened their mouths upon me, and exprobating have strucken my cheek, they are filled with my pains.

text.NRSV: I was at ease, and he broke me in two; he seized me by the neck and dashed me to pieces; he set me up as his target;
text.Rotherham: At ease, was I when he shattered me, Yea he seized me by my neck, and dashed me in pieces, then set me up for himself as a mark:
text.KJV: I was at ease, but he hath broken me asunder: he hath also taken me by my neck, and shaken me to pieces, and set me up for his mark.
text.NABRE: I was in peace, but he dislodged me, seized me by the neck, dashed me to pieces. He has set me up for a target;
text.DRB: God hath shut me up with the wicked man, and hath delivered me to the hands of the impious.

text.NRSV: his archers surround me. He slashes open my kidneys, and shows no mercy; he pours out my gall on the ground.
text.Rotherham: His archers came round against me, He clave asunder my reins, and spared not, He poured out, on the earth, my gall:
text.KJV: His archers compass me round about, he cleaveth my reins asunder, and doth not spare; he poureth out my gall upon the ground.
text.NABRE: his arrows strike me from all directions. He pierces my sides without mercy, pours out my gall upon the ground.
text.DRB: Isometime that wealthy-one suddenly am broken: he hath held my neck, broken me, and set me to himself as it were a mark.

text.NRSV: He bursts upon me again and again; he rushes at me like a warrior.
text.Rotherham: He made a breach in me, breach upon breach, He ran upon me, like a mighty man.
text.KJV: He breaketh me with breach upon breach, he runneth upon me like a giant.
text.NABRE: He pierces me, thrust upon thrust, rushes at me like a warrior.
text.DRB: He hath compassed me with his spears, he hath wounded my loins, he hath not spared, and hath poured out on the earth my bowels.

text.NRSV: I have sewed sackcloth upon my skin, and have laid my strength in the dust.
text.Rotherham: Sackcloth, sewed I on my skin, and rolled—in the dust—my horn:
text.KJV: I have sewed sackcloth upon my skin, and defiled my horn in the dust.
text.NABRE: I have sewn sackcloth on my skin, laid my horn low in the dust.
text.DRB: He hath cut me with wound upon wound, he hath come violently upon me as it were a giant.

text.NRSV: My face is red with weeping, and deep darkness is on my eyelids,
text.Rotherham: My face, is reddened from weeping, and, upon mine eyelashes, is the death-shade:—
text.KJV: My face is foul with weeping, and on my eyelids is the shadow of death;
text.NABRE: My face is inflamed with weeping, darkness covers my eyes,
text.DRB: I have sowed sackcloth upon my skin, and have covered my flesh with ashes.

text.NRSV: though there is no violence in my hands, and my prayer is pure.
text.Rotherham: Though no violence was in my hands, and, my prayer, was pure.
text.KJV: Not for any injustice in mine hands: also my prayer is pure.
text.NABRE: Although my hands are free from violence, and my prayer sincere.
text.DRB: My face is swollen with weeping, and my eye-lids are dim.

text.NRSV: “O earth, do not cover my blood; let my outcry find no resting place.
text.Rotherham: O earth! do not cover my blood, and let there be no place for mine outcry.
text.KJV: O earth, cover not thou my blood, and let my cry have no place.
text.NABRE: O earth, do not cover my blood, nor let my outcry come to rest!
text.DRB: These things have I suffered without the iniquity of my hand, whereas I had clean prayers to God.

text.NRSV: Even now, see, my witness is in heaven, and he that vouches for me is on high.
text.Rotherham: Even now, lo! in the heavens, is my witness,
text.KJV: Also now, behold, my witness is in heaven, and my record is on high.
text.NABRE: Even now my witness is in heaven, my advocate is on high.
text.DRB: Earth cover not my blood, neither let my cry find place in thee to be hid.

text.NRSV: My friends scorn me; my eye pours out tears to God,
text.Rotherham: And, he that voucheth for me is on high. My friends are, they who scorn me, Unto GOD, hath mine eye shed tears:—
text.KJV: My friends scorn me: but mine eye poureth out tears unto God.
text.NABRE: My friends it is who wrong me; before God my eyes shed tears,
text.DRB: For behold my witness is in heaven, and he that knoweth my conscience on high.

text.NRSV: that he would maintain the right of a mortal with God, as one does for a neighbor.
text.Rotherham: That one might plead, for a man, with GOD,—Even a son of man, for his friend!
text.KJV: O that one might plead for a man with God, as a man pleadeth for his neighbour!
text.NABRE: That justice may be done for a mortal with God: as for a man with his neighbor.
text.DRB: My friends full of words: mine eye distilleth unto God.

text.NRSV: For when a few years have come, I shall go the way from which I shall not return.”
text.Rotherham: When, a few years, come, then, by a path by which I shall not return, shall I depart.
text.KJV: When a few years are come, then I shall go the way whence I shall not return.
text.NABRE: For my years are numbered, and I go the road of no return.
text.DRB: And would God a man might so be judged with God, as the son of man is judged with his companion.

text.NRSV: “My spirit is broken, my days are extinct, the grave is ready for me.
text.Rotherham: My spirit, is broken, My days, are extinguished, Graves, are left me.
text.KJV: My breath is corrupt, my days are extinct, the graves are ready for me.
text.NABRE: My spirit is broken, my days finished, my burial at hand.
text.DRB: My spirit shall be extenuated, my days shall be shortened, and the grave only remaineth for me.

text.NRSV: Surely there are mockers around me, and my eye dwells on their provocation.
text.Rotherham: Verily there are mockers, with me! And, on their insults, mine eye doth rest.
text.KJV: Are there not mockers with me? and doth not mine eye continue in their provocation?
text.NABRE: Surely mockers surround me, at their provocation, my eyes grow dim.
text.DRB: I have not sinned, and mine eye abideth in bitterness.

text.NRSV: “Lay down a pledge for me with yourself; who is there that will give surety for me?
text.Rotherham: Appoint it, I pray thee—be thou surety for me with thyself, Who is there that, on my side, can pledge himself?
text.KJV: Lay down now, put me in a surety with thee; who is he that will strike hands with me?
text.NABRE: Put up a pledge for me with you: who is there to give surety for me?
text.DRB: Deliver me, and set me beside thee, and let any man's hand fight against me.

text.NRSV: Since you have closed their minds to understanding, therefore you will not let them triumph.
text.Rotherham: For, their heart, hast thou kept back from understanding, On this account, thou wilt not exalt them.
text.KJV: For thou hast hid their heart from understanding: therefore shalt thou not exalt them.
text.NABRE: You darken their minds to knowledge; therefore you will not exalt them.
text.DRB: Thou hast made their heart far from discipline, and therefore they shall not be exalted.

text.NRSV: Those who yield up their friends for a share of a possession—the eyes of their children will fail.
text.Rotherham: He that, for a share, denounceth friends, even, the eyes of his children, shall be dim.
text.KJV: He that speaketh flattery to his friends, even the eyes of his children shall fail.
text.NABRE: For a share of property he informs on friends, while the eyes of his children grow dim.
text.DRB: He promiseth a prey to his fellows, and the eyes of his children shall fail.

text.NRSV: “He has made me a byword of the peoples, and I am one before whom people spit.
text.Rotherham: But he hath set me, as the byword of peoples, And, one to be spit on in the face, do I become.
text.KJV: He hath made me also a byword of the people; and aforetime I was as a tabret.
text.NABRE: I am made a byword of the people; I am one at whom people spit.
text.DRB: He hath set me as it were for a proverb of the common people, and I am an example before them.

text.NRSV: My eye has grown dim from grief, and all my members are like a shadow.
text.Rotherham: Therefore hath mine eye become dim from vexation, and, my members, are like a shadow, all of them.
text.KJV: Mine eye also is dim by reason of sorrow, and all my members are as a shadow.
text.NABRE: My eyes are blind with anguish, and my whole frame is like a shadow.
text.DRB: Mine eye is dim for indignation, and my members are brought as it were to nothing.

text.NRSV: The upright are appalled at this, and the innocent stir themselves up against the godless.
text.Rotherham: Upright men shall be astounded over this, and, the innocent, against the impious, shall rouse themselves.
text.KJV: Upright men shall be astonied at this, and the innocent shall stir up himself against the hypocrite.
text.NABRE: The upright are astonished at this, the innocent aroused against the wicked.
text.DRB: The just shall be astonished upon this, and the innocent shall be raised up against the hypocrite.

text.NRSV: Yet the righteous hold to their way, and those with clean hands grow stronger and stronger.
text.Rotherham: That the righteous may hold on his way, and, the clean of hands, increase in strength.
text.KJV: The righteous also shall hold on his way, and he that hath clean hands shall be stronger and stronger.
text.NABRE: The righteous holds to his way, the one with clean hands increases in strength.
text.DRB: And the just shall hold his way, & with clean hands shall add strength.

text.NRSV: But you, come back now, all of you, and I shall not find a wise person among you.
text.Rotherham: But indeed, as for them all, will ye bethink yourselves and enter into it, I pray you? Or shall I not find, among you, one who is wise?
text.KJV: But as for you all, do ye return, and come now: for I cannot find one wise man among you.
text.NABRE: But turn now, and come on again; I do not find a wise man among you!
text.DRB: Be all you therefore converted, and come, and I shall not find among you any wise man.

text.NRSV: My days are past, my plans are broken off, the desires of my heart.
text.Rotherham: My days, are past, my purposes, are broken off, the possessions of my heart!
text.KJV: My days are past, my purposes are broken off, even the thoughts of my heart.
text.NABRE: My days pass by, my plans are at an end, the yearning of my heart.
text.DRB: My days have passed, my cogitations are dissipated, tormenting my heart:

text.NRSV: They make night into day; ‘The light,’ they say, ‘is near to the darkness.’
text.Rotherham: Night for day, they appoint, Light, is near, by reason of darkness!
text.KJV: They change the night into day: the light is short because of darkness.
text.NABRE: They would change the night into day; where there is darkness they talk of approaching light.
text.DRB: Night they have turned into day, and again after darkness I hope for light.

text.NRSV: If I look for Sheol as my house, if I spread my couch in darkness,
text.Rotherham: If I wait for hades as my house, in darkness, have spread out my couch;
text.KJV: If I wait, the grave is mine house: I have made my bed in the darkness.
text.NABRE: If my only hope is dwelling in Sheol, and spreading my couch in darkness,
text.DRB: If I shall expect, hell is my house, and in darkness I have made my bed.

text.NRSV: if I say to the Pit, ‘You are my father,’ and to the worm, ‘My mother,’ or ‘My sister,’
text.Rotherham: To corruption, have exclaimed, My father, thou! My mother! and My sister! to the worm.
text.KJV: I have said to corruption, Thou art my father: to the worm, Thou art my mother, and my sister.
text.NABRE: If I am to say to the pit, “You are my father,” and to the worm “my mother,” “my sister,”
text.DRB: I have said to rottenness: Thou art my father, my mother, and my sister, to worms.

text.NRSV: where then is my hope? Who will see my hope?
text.Rotherham: Where then would be my hope? And, as for my blessedness, who should see it!
text.KJV: And where is now my hope? as for my hope, who shall see it?
text.NABRE: Where then is my hope, my happiness, who can see it?
text.DRB: Where is now then my expectation, and my patience who considereth.

text.NRSV: Will it go down to the bars of Sheol? Shall we descend together into the dust?”
text.Rotherham: With me to hades, would they go down, If, wholly—into the dust, is the descent!
text.KJV: They shall go down to the bars of the pit, when our rest together is in the dust.
text.NABRE: Will they descend with me into Sheol? Shall we go down together into the dust?
text.DRB: All my things shall descend into most deep hell: there at the least, shall I have rest thinkest thou?

text.NRSV: Then Bildad the Shuhite answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Bildad the Shuhite, and said:—
text.KJV: Then answered Bildad the Shuhite, and said,
text.NABRE: Then Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:
text.DRB: But Baldad the Suhite answering, said:

text.NRSV: “How long will you hunt for words? Consider, and then we will speak.
text.Rotherham: How long will ye make a perversion of words? Ye should understand, and, afterwards, we could speak.
text.KJV: How long will it be ere ye make an end of words? mark, and afterwards we will speak.
text.NABRE: When will you put an end to words? Reflect, and then we can have discussion.
text.DRB: How long will ye speak vaunting words? Understand ye first, and so let us speak.

text.NRSV: Why are we counted as cattle? Why are we stupid in your sight?
text.Rotherham: Wherefore are we accounted like beasts? or appear stupid, in thine eyes?
text.KJV: Wherefore are we counted as beasts, and reputed vile in your sight?
text.NABRE: Why are we accounted like beasts, equal to them in your sight?
text.DRB: Why are we reputed as beasts, and accounted vile before you?

text.NRSV: You who tear yourself in your anger—shall the earth be forsaken because of you, or the rock be removed out of its place?
text.Rotherham: One tearing in pieces his own soul in his anger,—For thy sake, shall the earth be forsaken? or the rock be moved out of its place?
text.KJV: He teareth himself in his anger: shall the earth be forsaken for thee? and shall the rock be removed out of his place?
text.NABRE: You who tear yourself in your anger— shall the earth be neglected on your account or the rock be moved out of its place?
text.DRB: Which destroyest thy soul in thy fury, shall the earth be forsaken for thee, and shall rocks be transported out of their place?

text.NRSV: “Surely the light of the wicked is put out, and the flame of their fire does not shine.
text.Rotherham: Even the light of the lawless, shall go out,—Neither shall shine the flame of his fire;
text.KJV: Yea, the light of the wicked shall be put out, and the spark of his fire shall not shine.
text.NABRE: Truly, the light of the wicked is extinguished; the flame of his fire casts no light.
text.DRB: Shall not the light of the impious be extinguished, and the flame of his fire not shine?

text.NRSV: The light is dark in their tent, and the lamp above them is put out.
text.Rotherham: The light, hath darkened in his tent, Yea, his lamp above him, goeth out;
text.KJV: The light shall be dark in his tabernacle, and his candle shall be put out with him.
text.NABRE: In his tent light is darkness; the lamp above him goes out.
text.DRB: The light shall be dark in his tabernacle, and the candle that is over him, shall be extinguished.

text.NRSV: Their strong steps are shortened, and their own schemes throw them down.
text.Rotherham: The steppings of his strength are hemmed in, and his own counsel casteth him down;
text.KJV: The steps of his strength shall be straitened, and his own counsel shall cast him down.
text.NABRE: His vigorous steps are hemmed in, his own counsel casts him down.
text.DRB: The steps of his power shall be straitened, and his own counsel shall cast him down headlong.

text.NRSV: For they are thrust into a net by their own feet, and they walk into a pitfall.
text.Rotherham: For he is thrust into a net by his own feet, and, upon a trap, he marcheth;
text.KJV: For he is cast into a net by his own feet, and he walketh upon a snare.
text.NABRE: A net catches him by the feet, he wanders into a pitfall.
text.DRB: For he hath thrust his feet into a net, and walketh in the mashes thereof.

text.NRSV: A trap seizes them by the heel; a snare lays hold of them.
text.Rotherham: There catcheth him—by the heel—a gin, there holdeth him fast—a noose:
text.KJV: The gin shall take him by the heel, and the robber shall prevail against him.
text.NABRE: A trap seizes him by the heel, a snare lays hold of him.
text.DRB: The sole of his foot shall be held in a snare, and thirst shall burn against him.

text.NRSV: A rope is hid for them in the ground, a trap for them on the path.
text.Rotherham: Concealed in the ground is a cord for him,—and a snare for him, on the path.
text.KJV: The snare is laid for him in the ground, and a trap for him in the way.
text.NABRE: A noose is hidden for him on the ground, a netting for him on the path.
text.DRB: His gin is hid in the earth, and his spring upon the path.

text.NRSV: Terrors frighten them on every side, and chase them at their heels.
text.Rotherham: Round about, terrors have startled him, and have driven him to his feet.
text.KJV: Terrors shall make him afraid on every side, and shall drive him to his feet.
text.NABRE: On every side terrors frighten him; they harry him at each step.
text.DRB: Fears shall terrify him on every side, and his feet shall entangle him.

text.NRSV: Their strength is consumed by hunger, and calamity is ready for their stumbling.
text.Rotherham: Let his strength be famished, and, calamity, be ready at his side;
text.KJV: His strength shall be hungerbitten, and destruction shall be ready at his side.
text.NABRE: His strength is famished, disaster is ready at his side,
text.DRB: Let his strength be extenuated with famine, and let hunger invade his ribs.

text.NRSV: By disease their skin is consumed, the firstborn of Death consumes their limbs.
text.Rotherham: Let it devour the members of his body, Let the firstborn of death devour his members;
text.KJV: It shall devour the strength of his skin: even the firstborn of death shall devour his strength.
text.NABRE: His skin is eaten to the limbs, the firstborn of Death eats his limbs.
text.DRB: Let it devour the beauty of his skin, let death the first-born consume his arms.

text.NRSV: They are torn from the tent in which they trusted, and are brought to the king of terrors.
text.Rotherham: Uprooted, out of his tent, be his confidence, and let it drive him down to the king of terrors;
text.KJV: His confidence shall be rooted out of his tabernacle, and it shall bring him to the king of terrors.
text.NABRE: He is plucked from the security of his tent; and marched off to the king of terrors.
text.DRB: Let his confidence be plucked away out of his tabernacle, and let destruction as a king tread upon him.

text.NRSV: In their tents nothing remains; sulfur is scattered upon their habitations.
text.Rotherham: There shall dwell in his tent, what is naught-of-his, Let brimstone be strewed over his dwelling;
text.KJV: It shall dwell in his tabernacle, because it is none of his: brimstone shall be scattered upon his habitation.
text.NABRE: Fire lodges in his tent, over his abode brimstone is scattered.
text.DRB: Let the companions of him, that is not, dwell in his tabernacle, let brimstone be sprinkled in his tent.

text.NRSV: Their roots dry up beneath, and their branches wither above.
text.Rotherham: Beneath, let his roots be dried up, and, above, be cut off his branch;
text.KJV: His roots shall be dried up beneath, and above shall his branch be cut off.
text.NABRE: Below, his roots dry up, and above, his branches wither.
text.DRB: Let his roots be dried downward, and his harvest destroyed upward.

text.NRSV: Their memory perishes from the earth, and they have no name in the street.
text.Rotherham: His memorial, have perished out of the land, and let him have no name over the face of the open field;
text.KJV: His remembrance shall perish from the earth, and he shall have no name in the street.
text.NABRE: His memory perishes from the earth, and he has no name in the countryside.
text.DRB: Let the memory of him perish from the earth, and let not his name be renowned in the streets.

text.NRSV: They are thrust from light into darkness, and driven out of the world.
text.Rotherham: Let them thrust him out of light into darkness, Yea, out of the world, let them chase him;
text.KJV: He shall be driven from light into darkness, and chased out of the world.
text.NABRE: He is driven from light into darkness, and banished from the world.
text.DRB: He shall expel him out of light into darkness, and shall transport him out of the world.

text.NRSV: They have no offspring or descendant among their people, and no survivor where they used to live.
text.Rotherham: Let him have neither scion nor seed among his people, neither any survivor in his place of sojourn:
text.KJV: He shall neither have son nor nephew among his people, nor any remaining in his dwellings.
text.NABRE: He has neither offshoot nor offspring among his people, no survivor where once he dwelt.
text.DRB: His seed shall not be, nor his progeny in his people, nor any remnants in his countries.

text.NRSV: They of the west are appalled at their day, and horror seizes them of the east.
text.Rotherham: Over his day, have they been astounded who come behind, and, them who are in advance, a shudder hath seized.
text.KJV: They that come after him shall be astonied at his day, as they that went before were affrighted.
text.NABRE: Those who come after shall be appalled at his fate; those who went before are seized with horror.
text.DRB: In his day the last shall be astonished, and horror shall invade the first.

text.NRSV: Surely such are the dwellings of the ungodly, such is the place of those who do not know God.”
text.Rotherham: Surely, these, are the dwellings of him that is perverse, and, this, is the place of him that knoweth not GOD.
text.KJV: Surely such are the dwellings of the wicked, and this is the place of him that knoweth not God.
text.NABRE: So is it then with the dwelling of the impious; such is the place of the one who does not know God!
text.DRB: These are then the tabernacles of the wicked man, and this is the place of him that knoweth not God.

text.NRSV: Then Job answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Job, and said:—
text.KJV: Then Job answered and said,
text.NABRE: Then Job answered and said:
text.DRB: But Job answering, said:

text.NRSV: “How long will you torment me, and break me in pieces with words?
text.Rotherham: How long will ye grieve my soul? or crush me with words?
text.KJV: How long will ye vex my soul, and break me in pieces with words?
text.NABRE: How long will you afflict my spirit, grind me down with words?
text.DRB: How long do you afflict my soul, and wear me with words?

text.NRSV: These ten times you have cast reproach upon me; are you not ashamed to wrong me?
text.Rotherham: These ten times, have ye reviled me, Shameless ye wrong me.
text.KJV: These ten times have ye reproached me: ye are not ashamed that ye make yourselves strange to me.
text.NABRE: These ten times you have humiliated me, have assailed me without shame!
text.DRB: Behold, ten times you confound me, and are not ashamed oppressing me.

text.NRSV: And even if it is true that I have erred, my error remains with me.
text.Rotherham: And even if indeed I have erred, with myself lodgeth mine error.
text.KJV: And be it indeed that I have erred, mine error remaineth with myself.
text.NABRE: Even if it were true that I am at fault, my fault would remain with me;
text.DRB: For although I have been ignorant, mine ignorance shall be with me.

text.NRSV: If indeed you magnify yourselves against me, and make my humiliation an argument against me,
text.Rotherham: If indeed, against me, ye must needs magnify yourselves, and plead, against me, my reproach.
text.KJV: If indeed ye will magnify yourselves against me, and plead against me my reproach:
text.NABRE: If truly you exalt yourselves at my expense, and use my shame as an argument against me,
text.DRB: But you are set up against me, & reprove me with reproaches.

text.NRSV: know then that God has put me in the wrong, and closed his net around me.
text.Rotherham: Know, then, that, God, hath overthrown me, and, within his net, enclosed me.
text.KJV: Know now that God hath overthrown me, and hath compassed me with his net.
text.NABRE: Know then that it is God who has dealt unfairly with me, and compassed me round with his net.
text.DRB: At the least now understand ye, that God hath afflicted me not with equal judgment, and hath compassed me with his scourges.

text.NRSV: Even when I cry out, ‘Violence!’ I am not answered; I call aloud, but there is no justice.
text.Rotherham: Lo! I cry—out: Violence! but receive no answer, I cry aloud, but there is no vindication;
text.KJV: Behold, I cry out of wrong, but I am not heard: I cry aloud, but there is no judgment.
text.NABRE: If I cry out “Violence!” I am not answered. I shout for help, but there is no justice.
text.DRB: Behold I shall cry suffering violence, and no man will hear: I shall cry out, and there is none to judge.

text.NRSV: He has walled up my way so that I cannot pass, and he has set darkness upon my paths.
text.Rotherham: My way, hath he walled up, that I cannot pass, and, upon my paths, hath he made darkness rest;
text.KJV: He hath fenced up my way that I cannot pass, and he hath set darkness in my paths.
text.NABRE: He has barred my way and I cannot pass; veiled my path in darkness;
text.DRB: He hath hedged my path round about and I cannot pass, and in my way hath put darkness.

text.NRSV: He has stripped my glory from me, and taken the crown from my head.
text.Rotherham: My glory—from off me, hath he stripped, and hath removed the crown of my head;
text.KJV: He hath stripped me of my glory, and taken the crown from my head.
text.NABRE: He has stripped me of my glory, taken the diadem from my brow.
text.DRB: He hath spoiled me of my glory, and hath taken the crown from my head.

text.NRSV: He breaks me down on every side, and I am gone, he has uprooted my hope like a tree.
text.Rotherham: He hath ruined me on every side, and I am gone, and he hath taken away—like a tree—my hope;
text.KJV: He hath destroyed me on every side, and I am gone: and mine hope hath he removed like a tree.
text.NABRE: He breaks me down on every side, and I am gone; he has uprooted my hope like a tree.
text.DRB: He hath destroyed me on every side, and I perish, and as it were from a tree plucked hath he taken away my hope.

text.NRSV: He has kindled his wrath against me, and counts me as his adversary.
text.Rotherham: Yea he hath kindled against me his anger, and accounted me towards him like unto his adversaries;
text.KJV: He hath also kindled his wrath against me, and he counteth me unto him as one of his enemies.
text.NABRE: He has kindled his wrath against me; he counts me one of his enemies.
text.DRB: His fury is wrath against me, and he hath so accounted me as his enemy.

text.NRSV: His troops come on together; they have thrown up siegeworks against me, and encamp around my tent.
text.Rotherham: Together, enter his troops and have cast up, against me, their mound, and have encamped all around my tent;
text.KJV: His troops come together, and raise up their way against me, and encamp round about my tabernacle.
text.NABRE: His troops advance as one; they build up their road to attack me, encamp around my tent.
text.DRB: His servants have come together, and have made themselves a way by me, and have besieged my tabernacle round about.

text.NRSV: “He has put my family far from me, and my acquaintances are wholly estranged from me.
text.Rotherham: My Brethren—from beside me, hath he moved far away, and, mine acquaintance, are wholly estranged from me;
text.KJV: He hath put my brethren far from me, and mine acquaintance are verily estranged from me.
text.NABRE: My family has withdrawn from me, my friends are wholly estranged.
text.DRB: He hath made my brethren far from me, and my acquaintance as strangers have departed from me.

text.NRSV: My relatives and my close friends have failed me;
text.Rotherham: Failed me, have my near of kin, and, mine intimate acquaintances, have forgotten me;
text.KJV: My kinsfolk have failed, and my familiar friends have forgotten me.
text.NABRE: My relatives and companions neglect me, my guests have forgotten me.
text.DRB: My kinsmen have forsaken me, and they that knew me have forgotten me.

text.NRSV: the guests in my house have forgotten me; my serving maids count me as a stranger; I have become an alien in their eyes.
text.Rotherham: Ye guests of my house and my maidens, A stranger, have ye accounted me, An alien, have I become in their eyes;
text.KJV: They that dwell in mine house, and my maids, count me for a stranger: I am an alien in their sight.
text.NABRE: Even my maidservants consider me a stranger; I am a foreigner in their sight.
text.DRB: The guests of my house, and my maidservants have counted me an alien, and I have been as it were a stranger in their eyes.

text.NRSV: I call to my servant, but he gives me no answer; I must myself plead with him.
text.Rotherham: To mine own servant, I called, and he would not answer, With mine own mouth, I kept entreating him;
text.KJV: I called my servant, and he gave me no answer; I intreated him with my mouth.
text.NABRE: I call my servant, but he gives no answer, though I plead aloud with him.
text.DRB: I called my servant, and he did not answer me, with mine own mouth I besought him.

text.NRSV: My breath is repulsive to my wife; I am loathsome to my own family.
text.Rotherham: My breath, is strange to my wife, and I am loathsome to the sons of my own mother;
text.KJV: My breath is strange to my wife, though I intreated for the children’s sake of mine own body.
text.NABRE: My breath is abhorrent to my wife; I am loathsome to my very children.
text.DRB: My wife hath abhorred my breath, and I prayed the children of my womb.

text.NRSV: Even young children despise me; when I rise, they talk against me.
text.Rotherham: Even young children, despise me, I rise up, and they speak against me;
text.KJV: Yea, young children despised me; I arose, and they spake against me.
text.NABRE: Even young children despise me; when I appear, they speak against me.
text.DRB: Fools also despised me, and when I was departed from them, they backbited me.

text.NRSV: All my intimate friends abhor me, and those whom I loved have turned against me.
text.Rotherham: All the men of mine intimate circle abhor me, and, these whom I loved, have turned against me;
text.KJV: All my inward friends abhorred me: and they whom I loved are turned against me.
text.NABRE: All my intimate friends hold me in horror; those whom I loved have turned against me!
text.DRB: My counsellers sometime have abhorred me: & he whom I loved most hath turned against me.

text.NRSV: My bones stick to my skin and to my flesh, and I have escaped by the skin of my teeth.
text.Rotherham: Unto my skin and unto my flesh, have my bones cleaved, and I have escaped with the akin of my teeth.
text.KJV: My bone cleaveth to my skin and to my flesh, and I am escaped with the skin of my teeth.
text.NABRE: My bones cling to my skin, and I have escaped by the skin of my teeth.
text.DRB: The flesh being consumed, my bone hath cleaved to my skin, and there are left only lips about my teeth.

text.NRSV: Have pity on me, have pity on me, O you my friends, for the hand of God has touched me!
text.Rotherham: Pity me! pity me! ye, my friends, for, the hand of GOD, hath stricken me!
text.KJV: Have pity upon me, have pity upon me, O ye my friends; for the hand of God hath touched me.
text.NABRE: Pity me, pity me, you my friends, for the hand of God has struck me!
text.DRB: Have mercy upon me, have mercy upon me, at the least you my friends, because the hand of our Lord hath touched me.

text.NRSV: Why do you, like God, pursue me? Why are you not satisfied with my flesh?
text.Rotherham: Wherefore should ye persecute me as GOD? and, with my flesh, should not he satisfied?
text.KJV: Why do ye persecute me as God, and are not satisfied with my flesh?
text.NABRE: Why do you pursue me like God, and prey insatiably upon me?
text.DRB: Why do you as God persecute me, and are filled with my flesh?

text.NRSV: “O that my words were written down! O that they were inscribed in a book!
text.Rotherham: Oh, then, that my words, could be written, Oh that, in a record, they could be inscribed:
text.KJV: Oh that my words were now written! oh that they were printed in a book!
text.NABRE: Oh, would that my words were written down! Would that they were inscribed in a record:
text.DRB: Who will grant me that my words may be written? Who will give me that they may be drawn in a Book,

text.NRSV: O that with an iron pen and with lead they were engraved on a rock forever!
text.Rotherham: That, with a stylus of iron and [with] lead, for all time—in the rock, they could be graven!
text.KJV: That they were graven with an iron pen and lead in the rock for ever!
text.NABRE: That with an iron chisel and with lead they were cut in the rock forever!
text.DRB: with iron pen, & in plate of lead, or else with stile might be graven in flint-stone?

text.NRSV: For I know that my Redeemer lives, and that at the last he will stand upon the earth;
text.Rotherham: But, I, know that, my redeemer, liveth, and, as the Last over [my] dust, will he arise;
text.KJV: For I know that my redeemer liveth, and that he shall stand at the latter day upon the earth:
text.NABRE: As for me, I know that my vindicator lives, and that he will at last stand forth upon the dust.
text.DRB: For I know that my Redeemer liveth, & in the last day I shall rise out of the earth.

text.NRSV: and after my skin has been thus destroyed, then in my flesh I shall see God,
text.Rotherham: And, though, after my skin is struck off, this [followeth], yet, apart from my flesh, shall I see GOD:
text.KJV: And though after my skin worms destroy this body, yet in my flesh shall I see God:
text.NABRE: This will happen when my skin has been stripped off, and from my flesh I will see God:
text.DRB: And I shall be compassed again with my skin, and in my flesh I shall see God.

text.NRSV: whom I shall see on my side, and my eyes shall behold, and not another. My heart faints within me!
text.Rotherham: Whom, I myself, shall see, on my side, and, mine own eyes, [shall] have looked upon, and not those of a stranger. Exhausted are my deepest desires in my bosom!
text.KJV: Whom I shall see for myself, and mine eyes shall behold, and not another; though my reins be consumed within me.
text.NABRE: I will see for myself, my own eyes, not another’s, will behold him: my inmost being is consumed with longing.
text.DRB: Whom I myself shall see, & mine eyes shall behold, & none other: this my hope is laid up in my bosom.

text.NRSV: If you say, ‘How we will persecute him!’ and, ‘The root of the matter is found in him’;
text.Rotherham: Surely ye should say—Why should we persecute him? seeing, the root of the matter, is found in me.
text.KJV: But ye should say, Why persecute we him, seeing the root of the matter is found in me?
text.NABRE: But you who say, “How shall we persecute him, seeing that the root of the matter is found in him?”
text.DRB: Why then do you say now: Let us persecute him, & let us find root of word against him?

text.NRSV: be afraid of the sword, for wrath brings the punishment of the sword, so that you may know there is a judgment.”
text.Rotherham: Be ye afraid—on your part—of the face of the sword, because, wrath, [bringeth] the punishments of the sword, to the end ye may know the Almighty.
text.KJV: Be ye afraid of the sword: for wrath bringeth the punishments of the sword, that ye may know there is a judgment.
text.NABRE: Be afraid of the sword for yourselves, for your anger is a crime deserving the sword; that you may know that there is a judgment.
text.DRB: Fly therefore from the face of the sword, because the sword is the revenger of iniquities: and know ye that there is judgment.

text.NRSV: Then Zophar the Naamathite answered:
text.Rotherham: Then responded Zophar the Naamathite, and said:—
text.KJV: Then answered Zophar the Naamathite, and said,
text.NABRE: Then Zophar the Naamathite answered and said:
text.DRB: But Sophar the Naamathite answering, said:

text.NRSV: “Pay attention! My thoughts answer me, because of my haste within me.
text.Rotherham: Not so, do my thoughts answer me, and to this end, is my haste within me:
text.KJV: Therefore do my thoughts cause me to answer, and for this I make haste.
text.NABRE: So now my thoughts provide an answer for me, because of the feelings within me.
text.DRB: Therefore my diverse cogitations succeed one another, and my mind is rapt into sundry things.

text.NRSV: I hear censure that insults me, and a spirit beyond my understanding answers me.
text.Rotherham: The correction meant to confound me, I must hear, but, the spirit—out of my understanding, will give me a reply.
text.KJV: I have heard the check of my reproach, and the spirit of my understanding causeth me to answer.
text.NABRE: A rebuke that puts me to shame I hear, and from my understanding a spirit gives me a reply.
text.DRB: The doctrine, wherewith thou reprovest me, I will hear, and the spirit of my understanding shall answer me.

text.NRSV: Do you not know this from of old, ever since mortals were placed on earth,
text.Rotherham: Knowest thou, this—from antiquity, from the placing of man upon earth:—
text.KJV: Knowest thou not this of old, since man was placed upon earth,
text.NABRE: Do you not know this: from of old, since human beings were placed upon the earth,
text.DRB: This I know from the beginning, since man was placed upon the earth,

text.NRSV: that the exulting of the wicked is short, and the joy of the godless but for a moment?
text.Rotherham: That, the joy-shout of the lawless, is short, and, the rejoicing of the impious, for a moment?
text.KJV: That the triumphing of the wicked is short, and the joy of the hypocrite but for a moment?
text.NABRE: The triumph of the wicked is short and the joy of the impious but for a moment?
text.DRB: That the praise of the impious is short, and the joy of the hypocrite as it were for a moment:

text.NRSV: Even though they mount up high as the heavens, and their head reaches to the clouds,
text.Rotherham: Though his elevation mount up to the heavens, and, his head, to the clouds, doth reach,
text.KJV: Though his excellency mount up to the heavens, and his head reach unto the clouds;
text.NABRE: Though his pride mount up to the heavens and his head reach to the clouds,
text.DRB: If his pride rise up even to the heaven, and his head touch the clouds:

text.NRSV: they will perish forever like their own dung; those who have seen them will say, ‘Where are they?’
text.Rotherham: Like his own stubble, shall he utterly perish, They who had seen him, shall say, Where is he?
text.KJV: Yet he shall perish for ever like his own dung: they which have seen him shall say, Where is he?
text.NABRE: Yet he perishes forever like the dung he uses for fuel, and onlookers say, “Where is he?”
text.DRB: As a dunghill in the end he shall be destroyed, and they that had seen him, shall say: Where is he?

text.NRSV: They will fly away like a dream, and not be found; they will be chased away like a vision of the night.
text.Rotherham: Like a dream, shall he fly away, and they shall not find him, yea he shall be chased away, as a vision of the night.
text.KJV: He shall fly away as a dream, and shall not be found: yea, he shall be chased away as a vision of the night.
text.NABRE: Like a dream he takes flight and cannot be found; he fades away like a vision of the night.
text.DRB: As a dream that flyeth away he shall not be found, he shall pass as a vision by night:

text.NRSV: The eye that saw them will see them no more, nor will their place behold them any longer.
text.Rotherham: The eye that hath scanned him, shall not do it again, neither, any more, shall his place behold him:
text.KJV: The eye also which saw him shall see him no more; neither shall his place any more behold him.
text.NABRE: The eye which saw him does so no more; nor shall his dwelling again behold him.
text.DRB: The eye that had seen him, shall not see him, neither shall his place behold him any more.

text.NRSV: Their children will seek the favor of the poor, and their hands will give back their wealth.
text.Rotherham: His children, shall seek the favour of the poor, and, his own hand, shall give back his wealth.
text.KJV: His children shall seek to please the poor, and his hands shall restore their goods.
text.NABRE: His sons will restore to the poor, and his hands will yield up his riches.
text.DRB: His children shall come to naught with poverty, and his hands shall render him his sorrow.

text.NRSV: Their bodies are full of youthful vigor, but it will lie down with them in the dust.
text.Rotherham: His bones, are full of youthful vigour, yet, with him—in the dust, shall it lie down.
text.KJV: His bones are full of the sin of his youth, which shall lie down with him in the dust.
text.NABRE: Though his bones are full of youthful vigor, it shall lie with him in the dust.
text.DRB: His bones shall be filled with the vices of his youth, & they shall sleep with him in the dust.

text.NRSV: “Though wickedness is sweet in their mouth, though they hide it under their tongue,
text.Rotherham: Though, a sweet taste in his mouth, be given by vice, though he hide it under his tongue;
text.KJV: Though wickedness be sweet in his mouth, though he hide it under his tongue;
text.NABRE: Though wickedness is sweet in his mouth, and he hides it under his tongue,
text.DRB: For when evil shall be sweet in his mouth, he will hide it under his tongue.

text.NRSV: though they are loath to let it go, and hold it in their mouth,
text.Rotherham: Though he spare it, and will not let it go, but retain it in the midst of his mouth,
text.KJV: Though he spare it, and forsake it not; but keep it still within his mouth:
text.NABRE: Though he retains it and will not let it go but keeps it still within his mouth,
text.DRB: He will spare it, & not leave it, and will hide it in his throat.

text.NRSV: yet their food is turned in their stomachs; it is the venom of asps within them.
text.Rotherham: His food, in his stomach, is changed, the gall of adders, within him!
text.KJV: Yet his meat in his bowels is turned, it is the gall of asps within him.
text.NABRE: Yet in his stomach the food shall turn; it shall be venom of asps inside him.
text.DRB: His bread in his belly shall be turned into the gall of asps within him.

text.NRSV: They swallow down riches and vomit them up again; God casts them out of their bellies.
text.Rotherham: Wealth, hath he swallowed, and hath vomited the same, Out of his belly, shall, GOD, drive it forth:
text.KJV: He hath swallowed down riches, and he shall vomit them up again: God shall cast them out of his belly.
text.NABRE: The riches he swallowed he shall vomit up; God shall make his belly disgorge them.
text.DRB: The riches which he hath devoured, he shall vomit out, & God shall draw them forth out of his belly.

text.NRSV: They will suck the poison of asps; the tongue of a viper will kill them.
text.Rotherham: The poison of adders, shall he suck, The tongue of the viper shall slay him;
text.KJV: He shall suck the poison of asps: the viper’s tongue shall slay him.
text.NABRE: The poison of asps he shall drink in; the viper’s fangs shall slay him.
text.DRB: He shall suck the head of asps, and the viper's tongue shall kill him.

text.NRSV: They will not look on the rivers, the streams flowing with honey and curds.
text.Rotherham: Let him not see in the channels the flowings of torrents of honey and milk.
text.KJV: He shall not see the rivers, the floods, the brooks of honey and butter.
text.NABRE: He shall see no streams of oil, no torrents of honey or milk.
