text.NRSV: to which I have given the steppe for its home, the salt land for its dwelling place?
text.Rotherham: Whose house I have made the waste plain, and his dwellings, the land of salt:
text.KJV: Whose house I have made the wilderness, and the barren land his dwellings.
text.NABRE: I have made the wilderness his home and the salt flats his dwelling.
text.DRB: To whom I have given a house in the wilderness, and his tabernacles in the land of saltness.

text.NRSV: It scorns the tumult of the city; it does not hear the shouts of the driver.
text.Rotherham: He laugheth at the throng of the city, The shoutings of the driver, he heareth not;
text.KJV: He scorneth the multitude of the city, neither regardeth he the crying of the driver.
text.NABRE: He scoffs at the uproar of the city, hears no shouts of a driver.
text.DRB: He contemneth the multitude of the city, the cry of the exactor he heareth not.

text.NRSV: It ranges the mountains as its pasture, and it searches after every green thing.
text.Rotherham: He espieth the mountains, his pasture-ground, and, after every green thing, maketh search.
text.KJV: The range of the mountains is his pasture, and he searcheth after every green thing.
text.NABRE: He ranges the mountains for pasture, and seeks out every patch of green.
text.DRB: He looketh about the mountains of his pasture, and seeketh out all green places.

text.NRSV: “Is the wild ox willing to serve you? Will it spend the night at your crib?
text.Rotherham: Will the Wild-Ox be pleased to be thy servant? or lodge for the night by thy crib?
text.KJV: Will the unicorn be willing to serve thee, or abide by thy crib?
text.NABRE: Will the wild ox consent to serve you, or pass the nights at your manger?
text.DRB: Will the Rhinoceros serve thee, and will he tarry at thy stall?

text.NRSV: Can you bind it in the furrow with ropes, or will it harrow the valleys after you?
text.Rotherham: Canst thou bind the wild-ox, so that—with the ridge—shall run his cord? Or will he harrow the furrows after thee?
text.KJV: Canst thou bind the unicorn with his band in the furrow? or will he harrow the valleys after thee?
text.NABRE: Will you bind the wild ox with a rope in the furrow, and will he plow the valleys after you?
text.DRB: Shalt thou tie the Rhinoceros with thy collar to plough, or will he break the clods of the valleys after thee?

text.NRSV: Will you depend on it because its strength is great, and will you leave your labor to it?
text.Rotherham: Wilt thou trust in him, because of the greatness of his strength? Wilt thou leave unto him thy toil?
text.KJV: Wilt thou trust him, because his strength is great? or wilt thou leave thy labour to him?
text.NABRE: Will you depend on him for his great strength and leave to him the fruits of your toil?
text.DRB: Shalt thou have confidence in his great strength, and leave thy labours unto him?

text.NRSV: Do you have faith in it that it will return, and bring your grain to your threshing floor?
text.Rotherham: Wilt thou put faith in him, that he will bring back thy seed? and that, corn for thy threshing-floor, he will gather?
text.KJV: Wilt thou believe him, that he will bring home thy seed, and gather it into thy barn?
text.NABRE: Can you rely on him to bring in your grain and gather in the yield of your threshing floor?
text.DRB: Wilt thou credit him that he will render thee the seed, and gather together thy barn floor?

text.NRSV: “The ostrich’s wings flap wildly, though its pinions and plumage are not like the stork’s.
text.Rotherham: The wing of the Ostrich that waveth itself joyfully, Is it the pinion of lovingkindness or the plumage?
text.KJV: Gavest thou the goodly wings unto the peacocks? or wings and feathers unto the ostrich?
text.NABRE: The wings of the ostrich flap away; her plumage is lacking in feathers.
text.DRB: The wing of the Ostrich is like to the wings of the Herodius, and of the hawk.

text.NRSV: For it leaves its eggs to the earth, and lets them be warmed on the ground,
text.Rotherham: For she leaveth—to the earth—her eggs, and, on the dust, she letteth them be warmed;
text.KJV: Which leaveth her eggs in the earth, and warmeth them in dust,
text.NABRE: When she abandons her eggs on the ground and lets them warm in the sand,
text.DRB: When she leaveth her eggs on the earth, thou perhaps wilt heat them in the dust.

text.NRSV: forgetting that a foot may crush them, and that a wild animal may trample them.
text.Rotherham: And hath forgotten, that, a foot, may crush them,—or, the wild beast, tread on them!
text.KJV: And forgetteth that the foot may crush them, or that the wild beast may break them.
text.NABRE: She forgets that a foot may crush them, that the wild beasts may trample them;
text.DRB: She forgeteth that foot may tread upon them, or beast of the field break them.

text.NRSV: It deals cruelly with its young, as if they were not its own; though its labor should be in vain, it has no fear;
text.Rotherham: Dealing hardly with her young, as none-of-hers, In vain, her labour, without dread.
text.KJV: She is hardened against her young ones, as though they were not hers: her labour is in vain without fear;
text.NABRE: She cruelly disowns her young and her labor is useless; she has no fear.
text.DRB: She is hardened toward her young, as though they were not hers, she hath laboured in vain no fear compelling her.

text.NRSV: because God has made it forget wisdom, and given it no share in understanding.
text.Rotherham: For GOD hath suffered her to forget wisdom, and given her no share in understanding.
text.KJV: Because God hath deprived her of wisdom, neither hath he imparted to her understanding.
text.NABRE: For God has withheld wisdom from her and given her no share in understanding.
text.DRB: For God hath deprived her of wisdom, neither hath he given her understanding.

text.NRSV: When it spreads its plumes aloft, it laughs at the horse and its rider.
text.Rotherham: What time, on high, she vibrateth her wings, she laugheth at the horse and his rider.
text.KJV: What time she lifteth up herself on high, she scorneth the horse and his rider.
text.NABRE: Yet when she spreads her wings high, she laughs at a horse and rider.
text.DRB: When time shall be, she setteth up the wings on high: she scorneth the horse and his rider.

text.NRSV: “Do you give the horse its might? Do you clothe its neck with mane?
text.Rotherham: Couldst thou give—to the Horse—strength? Couldst thou clothe his neck with the quivering mane?
text.KJV: Hast thou given the horse strength? hast thou clothed his neck with thunder?
text.NABRE: Do you give the horse his strength, and clothe his neck with a mane?
text.DRB: Shalt thou give strength to the horse, or put neighing about his neck?

text.NRSV: Do you make it leap like the locust? Its majestic snorting is terrible.
text.Rotherham: Couldst thou cause him to leap like a locust? The majesty of his snort, is a terror!
text.KJV: Canst thou make him afraid as a grasshopper? the glory of his nostrils is terrible.
text.NABRE: Do you make him quiver like a locust, while his thunderous snorting spreads terror?
text.DRB: Shalt thou raise him up as Locusts? The glory of his nostrils is terror.

text.NRSV: It paws violently, exults in its strength; it goes out to meet the weapons.
text.Rotherham: He diggeth into the plain, and rejoiceth in vigour, he goeth forth to meet armour;
text.KJV: He paweth in the valley, and rejoiceth in his strength: he goeth on to meet the armed men.
text.NABRE: He paws the valley, he rejoices in his strength, and charges into battle.
text.DRB: He diggeth the earth with his hoof, he pranceth boldly, he goeth forward to meet the armed men.

text.NRSV: It laughs at fear, and is not dismayed; it does not turn back from the sword.
text.Rotherham: He laugheth at dread, and is not dismayed, neither turneth he back, from the face of the sword;
text.KJV: He mocketh at fear, and is not affrighted; neither turneth he back from the sword.
text.NABRE: He laughs at fear and cannot be terrified; he does not retreat from the sword.
text.DRB: He contemneth fear, neither yieldeth he to the sword.

text.NRSV: Upon it rattle the quiver, the flashing spear, and the javelin.
text.Rotherham: Against him, whiz [the arrows of] the quiver, the flashing head of spear and javelin;
text.KJV: The quiver rattleth against him, the glittering spear and the shield.
text.NABRE: Around him rattles the quiver, flashes the spear and the javelin.
text.DRB: Upon him shall the quiver sound, the spear shall glister and the shield.

text.NRSV: With fierceness and rage it swallows the ground; it cannot stand still at the sound of the trumpet.
text.Rotherham: With stamping and rage, he drinketh up the ground,—he will not stand still when the horn soundeth;
text.KJV: He swalloweth the ground with fierceness and rage: neither believeth he that it is the sound of the trumpet.
text.NABRE: Frenzied and trembling he devours the ground; he does not hold back at the sound of the trumpet;
text.DRB: Fervent and fuming he suppeth the earth, neither doth he make account when the noise of the trumpet soundeth.

text.NRSV: When the trumpet sounds, it says ‘Aha!’ From a distance it smells the battle, the thunder of the captains, and the shouting.
text.Rotherham: As oft as the horn soundeth, he saith, Aha! And, from afar, he scenteth the battle,—the thunder of commanders and the war-cry.
text.KJV: He saith among the trumpets, Ha, ha; and he smelleth the battle afar off, the thunder of the captains, and the shouting.
text.NABRE: at the trumpet’s call he cries, “Aha!” Even from afar he scents the battle, the roar of the officers and the shouting.
text.DRB: When he shall hear the trumpet he saith: Vah, he smelleth battle far off, the exhortation of the captains, and the cry of the army.

text.NRSV: “Is it by your wisdom that the hawk soars, and spreads its wings toward the south?
text.Rotherham: Is it, by thine understanding, that the Bird of Passage betaketh him to his pinions? spreadeth out his wings to the south?
text.KJV: Doth the hawk fly by thy wisdom, and stretch her wings toward the south?
text.NABRE: Is it by your understanding that the hawk soars, that he spreads his wings toward the south?
text.DRB: Doth the hawk wax feathered by thy wisdom, spreading her wings to the South?

text.NRSV: Is it at your command that the eagle mounts up and makes its nest on high?
text.Rotherham: Or, at thy bidding, that the Eagle mounteth, and that he setteth on high his nest?
text.KJV: Doth the eagle mount up at thy command, and make her nest on high?
text.NABRE: Does the eagle fly up at your command to build his nest up high?
text.DRB: Shall the eagle mount at thy commandment, and put her nest in high places?

text.NRSV: It lives on the rock and makes its home in the fastness of the rocky crag.
text.Rotherham: The crag, he inhabiteth, and so lodgeth himself, on the tooth of the crag, and high fort;
text.KJV: She dwelleth and abideth on the rock, upon the crag of the rock, and the strong place.
text.NABRE: On a cliff he dwells and spends the night, on the spur of cliff or fortress.
text.DRB: She abideth in rocks, and tarrieth among cragged flints, and stony hills where is no access.

text.NRSV: From there it spies out the prey; its eyes behold it from far away.
text.Rotherham: From thence, he searcheth out food, far away, his eyes do pierce;
text.KJV: From thence she seeketh the prey, and her eyes behold afar off.
text.NABRE: From there he watches for his food; his eyes behold it afar off.
text.DRB: Thence she beholdeth the prey, and her eyes see a far off.

text.NRSV: Its young ones suck up blood; and where the slain are, there it is.”
text.Rotherham: And, his young brood, suck up blood, and, where the slain are, there, is he.
text.KJV: Her young ones also suck up blood: and where the slain are, there is she.
text.NABRE: His young ones greedily drink blood; where the slain are, there is he.
text.DRB: Her young ones shall lick blood: and wheresoever the carcass shall be, she is present by and by.

text.NRSV: And the Lord said to Job:
text.Rotherham: And Yahweh responded to Job, and said:—
text.KJV: Moreover the Lord answered Job, and said,
text.NABRE: The Lord then answered Job and said:
text.DRB: And our Lord answering Job out of the whirlwind, said:

text.NRSV: “Shall a faultfinder contend with the Almighty? Anyone who argues with God must respond.”
text.Rotherham: Shall a reprover contend, with the Almighty? He that disputeth with GOD, let him answer it!
text.KJV: Shall he that contendeth with the Almighty instruct him? he that reproveth God, let him answer it.
text.NABRE: Will one who argues with the Almighty be corrected? Let him who would instruct God give answer!
text.DRB: Gird thy loins as a man: I will ask thee, & do thou tell me.

text.NRSV: Then Job answered the Lord:
text.Rotherham: Then Job responded to Yahweh, and said:—
text.KJV: Then Job answered the Lord, and said,
text.NABRE: Then Job answered the Lord and said:
text.DRB: Shalt thou make my judgment of none effect: and condemn me, that thou mayst be justified?

text.NRSV: “See, I am of small account; what shall I answer you? I lay my hand on my mouth.
text.Rotherham: Lo! I am of no account, what shall I reply to thee? My hand, have I laid on my mouth:
text.KJV: Behold, I am vile; what shall I answer thee? I will lay mine hand upon my mouth.
text.NABRE: Look, I am of little account; what can I answer you? I put my hand over my mouth.
text.DRB: And hast thou an arm as God, and dost thou thunder with like voice?

text.NRSV: I have spoken once, and I will not answer; twice, but will proceed no further.”
text.Rotherham: Once, have I spoken, but I will not proceed, yea twice, but I will not add.
text.KJV: Once have I spoken; but I will not answer: yea, twice; but I will proceed no further.
text.NABRE: I have spoken once, I will not reply; twice, but I will do so no more.
text.DRB: Put beauty about thee, and set up thyself aloft, and be glorious, and put on goodly garments.

text.NRSV: Then the Lord answered Job out of the whirlwind:
text.Rotherham: So then Yahweh responded to Job, out of a storm, and said:—
text.KJV: Then answered the Lord unto Job out of the whirlwind, and said,
text.NABRE: Then the Lord answered Job out of the storm and said:
text.DRB: Disperse the proud in thy fury, and beholding every arrogant man, humble him.

text.NRSV: “Gird up your loins like a man; I will question you, and you declare to me.
text.Rotherham: Gird, I pray thee—as a strong man—thy loins, I will ask thee, and inform thou me.
text.KJV: Gird up thy loins now like a man: I will demand of thee, and declare thou unto me.
text.NABRE: Gird up your loins now, like a man. I will question you, and you tell me the answers!
text.DRB: Behold all the proud, and confound them, and destroy the impious in their place.

text.NRSV: Will you even put me in the wrong? Will you condemn me that you may be justified?
text.Rotherham: Wilt thou even frustrate my justice? Wilt thou condemn me, that thou mayest appear right?
text.KJV: Wilt thou also disannul my judgment? wilt thou condemn me, that thou mayest be righteous?
text.NABRE: Would you refuse to acknowledge my right? Would you condemn me that you may be justified?
text.DRB: Hide them in the dust together, and plunge their faces in the pit.

text.NRSV: Have you an arm like God, and can you thunder with a voice like his?
text.Rotherham: But if, an arm like GOD, thou hast, and, with a voice like his, thou canst thunder,
text.KJV: Hast thou an arm like God? or canst thou thunder with a voice like him?
text.NABRE: Have you an arm like that of God, or can you thunder with a voice like his?
text.DRB: And I will confess, that thy right hand is able to save thee.

text.NRSV: “Adorn yourself with majesty and dignity; clothe yourself with glory and splendor.
text.Rotherham: Deck thyself, I pray thee, with majesty and grandeur, Yea, with dignity and splendour, thou shalt clothe thyself;
text.KJV: Deck thyself now with majesty and excellency; and array thyself with glory and beauty.
text.NABRE: Adorn yourself with grandeur and majesty, and clothe yourself with glory and splendor.
text.DRB: Behold Behemoth whom I made with thee, shall eat hay as it were an oxe.

text.NRSV: Pour out the overflowings of your anger, and look on all who are proud, and abase them.
text.Rotherham: Pour out thy transports of anger, and look on every one who is high, and lay him low;
text.KJV: Cast abroad the rage of thy wrath: and behold every one that is proud, and abase him.
text.NABRE: Let loose the fury of your wrath; look at everyone who is proud and bring them down.
text.DRB: His strength is in his loins, and his power in the navel of his belly.

text.NRSV: Look on all who are proud, and bring them low; tread down the wicked where they stand.
text.Rotherham: Look on every one who is high, and humble him, yea tread down the lawless, on the spot:
text.KJV: Look on every one that is proud, and bring him low; and tread down the wicked in their place.
text.NABRE: Look at everyone who is proud, and humble them. Tear down the wicked in their place,
text.DRB: He gathereth together his tail as the cedar tree, the sinews of his stones are perplex.

text.NRSV: Hide them all in the dust together; bind their faces in the world below.
text.Rotherham: Hide them in the dust all together, Their faces, bind thou in darkness;
text.KJV: Hide them in the dust together; and bind their faces in secret.
text.NABRE: bury them in the dust together; in the hidden world imprison them.
text.DRB: His bones are as pipes of brass, his gristle as it were plates of iron.

text.NRSV: Then I will also acknowledge to you that your own right hand can give you victory.
text.Rotherham: And, even I myself, will praise thee, in that thine own right hand can bring thee salvation.
text.KJV: Then will I also confess unto thee that thine own right hand can save thee.
text.NABRE: Then will I too praise you, for your own right hand can save you.
text.DRB: He is the beginning of the ways of God, which made him, he shall apply his sword.

text.NRSV: “Look at Behemoth, which I made just as I made you; it eats grass like an ox.
text.Rotherham: Behold, I pray thee, the Hippopotamus, which I made with thee, Grass—like the ox, he eateth;
text.KJV: Behold now behemoth, which I made with thee; he eateth grass as an ox.
text.NABRE: Look at Behemoth, whom I made along with you, who feeds on grass like an ox.
text.DRB: To him the mountains bear grass: all the beasts of the field shall play there.

text.NRSV: Its strength is in its loins, and its power in the muscles of its belly.
text.Rotherham: Behold, I pray thee, his strength in his loins, and his force, in the muscles of his belly;
text.KJV: Lo now, his strength is in his loins, and his force is in the navel of his belly.
text.NABRE: See the strength in his loins, the power in the sinews of his belly.
text.DRB: He sleepeth under the shadow, in the secret of the reed, and in moist places.

text.NRSV: It makes its tail stiff like a cedar; the sinews of its thighs are knit together.
text.Rotherham: He bendeth down his tail like a cedar, the sinews of his thighs, are twisted together;
text.KJV: He moveth his tail like a cedar: the sinews of his stones are wrapped together.
text.NABRE: He carries his tail like a cedar; the sinews of his thighs are like cables.
text.DRB: Shadows do protect his shadow, the willows of the torrent shall compass him.

text.NRSV: Its bones are tubes of bronze, its limbs like bars of iron.
text.Rotherham: His bones, are barrels of bronze, his frame, is like hammered bars of iron:
text.KJV: His bones are as strong pieces of brass; his bones are like bars of iron.
text.NABRE: His bones are like tubes of bronze; his limbs are like iron rods.
text.DRB: Lo, he shall sup up the river, & shall not marvel: and he hath confidence that Jordan may run into his mouth.

text.NRSV: “It is the first of the great acts of God; only its Maker can approach it with the sword.
text.Rotherham: He, is the beginning of the ways of GOD, Let his maker, present him his sword:
text.KJV: He is the chief of the ways of God: he that made him can make his sword to approach unto him.
text.NABRE: He is the first of God’s ways, only his maker can approach him with a sword.
text.DRB: In his eyes as with a hook he shall take him, and with stakes he shall bore through his nostrils.

text.NRSV: For the mountains yield food for it where all the wild animals play.
text.Rotherham: Surely the mountains bring, produce, to him, where, all the wild beasts of the field, do play;
text.KJV: Surely the mountains bring him forth food, where all the beasts of the field play.
text.NABRE: For the mountains bring him produce, and all wild animals make sport there.
text.DRB: Canst thou draw out the Leviathan with a hook, and with a rope shalt thou tie his tongue?

text.NRSV: Under the lotus plants it lies, in the covert of the reeds and in the marsh.
text.Rotherham: Under the lotus-trees, he lieth down, in a covert of reed and swamp;
text.KJV: He lieth under the shady trees, in the covert of the reed, and fens.
text.NABRE: Under lotus trees he lies, in coverts of the reedy swamp.
text.DRB: Shalt thou put a ring in his nostrils, or bore through his jaw with a buckle?

text.NRSV: The lotus trees cover it for shade; the willows of the wadi surround it.
text.Rotherham: The lotus-trees cover him with their shade, the willows of the torrent-bed compass him about;
text.KJV: The shady trees cover him with their shadow; the willows of the brook compass him about.
text.NABRE: The lotus trees cover him with their shade; all about him are the poplars in the wadi.
text.DRB: Will he multiply prayers to thee, or speak to thee gentle words?

text.NRSV: Even if the river is turbulent, it is not frightened; it is confident though Jordan rushes against its mouth.
text.Rotherham: Lo! the river becometh insolent—he is not alarmed! He is confident, though a Jordan burst forth to his mouth:
text.KJV: Behold, he drinketh up a river, and hasteth not: he trusteth that he can draw up Jordan into his mouth.
text.NABRE: If the river grows violent, he is not disturbed; he is tranquil though the Jordan surges about his mouth.
text.DRB: Will he make a covenant with thee, and shalt thou take him to be a servant for ever?

text.NRSV: Can one take it with hooks or pierce its nose with a snare?”
text.Rotherham: Before his eyes, shall he be caught? With a hook, can one pierce his nose?
text.KJV: He taketh it with his eyes: his nose pierceth through snares.
text.NABRE: Who can capture him by his eyes, or pierce his nose with a trap?
text.DRB: Shalt thou delude him as a bird, or tie him for thy handmaids?

text.NRSV: “Can you draw out Leviathan with a fishhook, or press down its tongue with a cord?
text.Rotherham: Canst thou draw out the Crocodile with a fish-hook? Or, with a cord, canst thou fasten down his tongue?
text.KJV: Canst thou draw out leviathan with an hook? or his tongue with a cord which thou lettest down?
text.NABRE: Whoever might vainly hope to do so need only see him to be overthrown.
text.DRB: Not as cruel will I raise him: for who can resist my countenance?

text.NRSV: Can you put a rope in its nose, or pierce its jaw with a hook?
text.Rotherham: Wilt thou put a rush-cord on his nose? or, with a thorn, wilt thou pierce his jaw?
text.KJV: Canst thou put an hook into his nose? or bore his jaw through with a thorn?
text.NABRE: No one is fierce enough to arouse him; who then dares stand before me?
text.DRB: Who hath given me before, that I may render unto him? All things that are under heaven be mine.

text.NRSV: Will it make many supplications to you? Will it speak soft words to you?
text.Rotherham: Will he multiply unto thee supplications, or will he speak unto thee softly?
text.KJV: Will he make many supplications unto thee? will he speak soft words unto thee?
text.NABRE: Whoever has assailed me, I will pay back— Everything under the heavens is mine.
text.DRB: I will not spare him, and his mighty words, and framed to beseech.

text.NRSV: Will it make a covenant with you to be taken as your servant forever?
text.Rotherham: Will he solemnise a covenant with thee? Wilt thou take him for a life-long servant?
text.KJV: Will he make a covenant with thee? wilt thou take him for a servant for ever?
text.NABRE: I need hardly mention his limbs, his strength, and the fitness of his equipment.
text.DRB: Who shall reveal the face of his garment: and who shall enter into the midst of his mouth?

text.NRSV: Will you play with it as with a bird, or will you put it on a leash for your girls?
text.Rotherham: Wilt thou sport with him, as with a little bird? Or wilt thou bind him, for thy maidens?
text.KJV: Wilt thou play with him as with a bird? or wilt thou bind him for thy maidens?
text.NABRE: Who can strip off his outer garment, or penetrate his double armor?
text.DRB: Who shall open the gate of his countenance? Dread is round about his teeth.

text.NRSV: Will traders bargain over it? Will they divide it up among the merchants?
text.Rotherham: Shall the companions bargain over him? or will they part him among the traders?
text.KJV: Shall the companions make a banquet of him? shall they part him among the merchants?
text.NABRE: Who can force open the doors of his face, close to his terrible teeth?
text.DRB: His body as shields that are cast, compact with scales fast cleaving together.

text.NRSV: Can you fill its skin with harpoons, or its head with fishing spears?
text.Rotherham: Wilt thou fill, with darts, his skin? or, with fish-spears, his head?
text.KJV: Canst thou fill his skin with barbed irons? or his head with fish spears?
text.NABRE: Rows of scales are on his back, tightly sealed together;
text.DRB: One is joined to another, and not so much as any air entereth between them:

text.NRSV: Lay hands on it; think of the battle; you will not do it again!
text.Rotherham: Lay thou upon him thy hand, remember the battle—no more!
text.KJV: Lay thine hand upon him, remember the battle, do no more.
text.NABRE: They are fitted so close to each other that no air can come between them;
text.DRB: One shall stick to another, and holding each other, they shall not be separated.

text.NRSV: Any hope of capturing it will be disappointed; were not even the gods overwhelmed at the sight of it?
text.Rotherham: Lo! any hope of him, hath been found deceptive, Even at the sight of him, shall not one be overwhelmed?
text.KJV: Behold, the hope of him is in vain: shall not one be cast down even at the sight of him?
text.NABRE: So joined to one another that they hold fast and cannot be parted.
text.DRB: His sneezing is as the shining of fire, and his eyes as the twinklings of the morning.

text.NRSV: No one is so fierce as to dare to stir it up. Who can stand before it?
text.Rotherham: None so bold, that he will rouse him! Who then is he that, before me, can stand?
text.KJV: None is so fierce that dare stir him up: who then is able to stand before me?
text.NABRE: When he sneezes, light flashes forth; his eyes are like the eyelids of the dawn.
text.DRB: Out of his mouth proceed lamps, as it were torches of lighted fire.

text.NRSV: Who has given to me, that I should repay him? Whatever is under the whole heaven is mine.
text.Rotherham: Who hath forestalled me, that I may repay him? Under all the heavens, mine it is!
text.KJV: Who hath prevented me, that I should repay him? whatsoever is under the whole heaven is mine.
text.NABRE: Out of his mouth go forth torches; sparks of fire leap forth.
text.DRB: Out of his nostrils proceedeth smoke, as it were of a pot heated and boiling.

text.NRSV: “I will not keep silence concerning its limbs, or its mighty strength, or its splendid frame.
text.Rotherham: I will not pass by in silence his parts, or the matter of strength, or the grace of his armour.
text.KJV: I will not conceal his parts, nor his power, nor his comely proportion.
text.NABRE: From his nostrils comes smoke as from a seething pot or bowl.
text.DRB: His breath maketh coals to burn, & a flame cometh forth out of his mouth.

text.NRSV: Who can strip off its outer garment? Who can penetrate its double coat of mail?
text.Rotherham: Who hath removed his outer garment, through his double row of teeth, who would enter?
text.KJV: Who can discover the face of his garment? or who can come to him with his double bridle?
text.NABRE: His breath sets coals afire; a flame comes from his mouth.
text.DRB: In his neck shall strength abide, and neediness goeth before his face.

text.NRSV: Who can open the doors of its face? All around its teeth is terror.
text.Rotherham: The doors of his face, who hath opened? The circles of his teeth, are a terror!
text.KJV: Who can open the doors of his face? his teeth are terrible round about.
text.NABRE: Strength abides in his neck, and power leaps before him.
text.DRB: The members of his flesh cleave together one to another: he shall send lightnings against him, and he shall not be carried to another place.

text.NRSV: Its back has rows of shields, shut up closely as with a seal.
text.Rotherham: A pride, are his arched sides, closed up, with a firm seal;
text.KJV: His scales are his pride, shut up together as with a close seal.
text.NABRE: The folds of his flesh stick together, it is cast over him and immovable.
text.DRB: His heart shall be hardened as a stone, and shall be stiffly compact as the smith's stithy.

text.NRSV: One is so near to another that no air can come between them.
text.Rotherham: One to another, they join, and, air, cannot enter between them;
text.KJV: One is so near to another, that no air can come between them.
text.NABRE: His heart is cast as hard as stone; cast as the lower millstone.
text.DRB: When he shall be taken away, the Angels shall fear, and being feared shall be purged.

text.NRSV: They are joined one to another; they clasp each other and cannot be separated.
text.Rotherham: Each to its fellow, they cleave, they grasp each other, and cannot be parted;
text.KJV: They are joined one to another, they stick together, that they cannot be sundered.
text.NABRE: When he rises up, the gods are afraid; when he crashes down, they fall back.
text.DRB: When the sword shall apprehend him, neither spear, nor breast-plate shall be able to abide.

text.NRSV: Its sneezes flash forth light, and its eyes are like the eyelids of the dawn.
text.Rotherham: His sneezings, flash forth light, and, his eyes, are like the eyelashes of the dawn;
text.KJV: By his neesings a light doth shine, and his eyes are like the eyelids of the morning.
text.NABRE: Should a sword reach him, it will not avail; nor will spear, dart, or javelin.
text.DRB: For he shall esteem iron as chaff, & brass, as rotten wood.

text.NRSV: From its mouth go flaming torches; sparks of fire leap out.
text.Rotherham: Out of his mouth, torches dart forth, sparks of fire, escape;
text.KJV: Out of his mouth go burning lamps, and sparks of fire leap out.
text.NABRE: He regards iron as chaff, and bronze as rotten wood.
text.DRB: The bow-man shall not put him to flight, the stones of the sling, to him are turned into stubble.

text.NRSV: Out of its nostrils comes smoke, as from a boiling pot and burning rushes.
text.Rotherham: Out of his nostrils, proceedeth smoke, like a blown pot and rushes;
text.KJV: Out of his nostrils goeth smoke, as out of a seething pot or caldron.
text.NABRE: No arrow will put him to flight; slingstones used against him are but straw.
text.DRB: As stubble will he esteem the hammer, and he will laugh him to scorn that shaketh the spear.

text.NRSV: Its breath kindles coals, and a flame comes out of its mouth.
text.Rotherham: His breath, setteth coals ablaze, and, a flame, out of his mouth, proceedeth;
text.KJV: His breath kindleth coals, and a flame goeth out of his mouth.
text.NABRE: Clubs he regards as straw; he laughs at the crash of the spear.
text.DRB: The beams of the sun shall be under him, & he shall strew gold under him as durt.

text.NRSV: In its neck abides strength, and terror dances before it.
text.Rotherham: In his neck, lodgeth strength, and, before him, danceth dismay;
text.KJV: In his neck remaineth strength, and sorrow is turned into joy before him.
text.NABRE: Under him are sharp pottery fragments, spreading a threshing sledge upon the mire.
text.DRB: He shall make the deep sea to boil as a pot, and shall put it as when ointments boil.

text.NRSV: The folds of its flesh cling together; it is firmly cast and immovable.
text.Rotherham: The dewlaps of his flesh, cleave together, hardened upon him, they cannot be moved;
text.KJV: The flakes of his flesh are joined together: they are firm in themselves; they cannot be moved.
text.NABRE: He makes the depths boil like a pot; he makes the sea like a perfume bottle.
text.DRB: Apath shall shine after him, he shall esteem the depth as waxing old.

text.NRSV: Its heart is as hard as stone, as hard as the lower millstone.
text.Rotherham: His heart, is hardened like a stone, yea hardened, like the nether millstone;
text.KJV: His heart is as firm as a stone; yea, as hard as a piece of the nether millstone.
text.NABRE: His heart is cast as hard as stone; cast as the lower millstone.
text.DRB: There is no power upon the earth, that may be compared with him, who is made to fear no man.

text.NRSV: When it raises itself up the gods are afraid; at the crashing they are beside themselves.
text.Rotherham: At his rising up, mighty men are afraid, by reason of terror, they are beside themselves:
text.KJV: When he raiseth up himself, the mighty are afraid: by reason of breakings they purify themselves.
text.NABRE: When he rises up, the gods are afraid; when he crashes down, they fall back.
text.DRB: He seeth every high thing, he is king over all the children of pride.

text.NRSV: Though the sword reaches it, it does not avail, nor the spear, the dart, or the javelin.
text.Rotherham: As for him that assaileth him, the sword availeth not, spear, dart, or coat of mail:
text.KJV: The sword of him that layeth at him cannot hold: the spear, the dart, nor the habergeon.
text.NABRE: Should a sword reach him, it will not avail; nor will spear, dart, or javelin.

text.NRSV: It counts iron as straw, and bronze as rotten wood.
text.Rotherham: He counteth iron as broken straw, and bronze as rotten wood:
text.KJV: He esteemeth iron as straw, and brass as rotten wood.
text.NABRE: He regards iron as chaff, and bronze as rotten wood.

text.NRSV: The arrow cannot make it flee; slingstones, for it, are turned to chaff.
text.Rotherham: The arrow, will not make him flee, Into chaff, are sling-stones changed by him:
text.KJV: The arrow cannot make him flee: slingstones are turned with him into stubble.
text.NABRE: No arrow will put him to flight; slingstones used against him are but straw.

text.NRSV: Clubs are counted as chaff; it laughs at the rattle of the javelins.
text.Rotherham: As a straw, is a club accounted, and he laugheth at the whir of the javelin;
text.KJV: Darts are counted as stubble: he laugheth at the shaking of a spear.
text.NABRE: Clubs he regards as straw; he laughs at the crash of the spear.

text.NRSV: Its underparts are like sharp potsherds; it spreads itself like a threshing sledge on the mire.
text.Rotherham: His underparts, are points of potsherd, a pointed threshing roller spreadeth out upon the slime:
text.KJV: Sharp stones are under him: he spreadeth sharp pointed things upon the mire.
text.NABRE: Under him are sharp pottery fragments, spreading a threshing sledge upon the mire.

text.NRSV: It makes the deep boil like a pot; it makes the sea like a pot of ointment.
text.Rotherham: He causeth to boil, as a cauldron, the raging deep, the sea, he maketh like a brewing vessel:
text.KJV: He maketh the deep to boil like a pot: he maketh the sea like a pot of ointment.
text.NABRE: He makes the depths boil like a pot; he makes the sea like a perfume bottle.

text.NRSV: It leaves a shining wake behind it; one would think the deep to be white-haired.
text.Rotherham: After him, he lighteth up a path, one might think the resounding deep to be hoary!
text.KJV: He maketh a path to shine after him; one would think the deep to be hoary.
text.NABRE: Behind him he leaves a shining path; you would think the deep had white hair.

text.NRSV: On earth it has no equal, a creature without fear.
text.Rotherham: There is not—upon the dust—his like, that hath been made to be without fear;
text.KJV: Upon earth there is not his like, who is made without fear.
text.NABRE: Upon the earth there is none like him, he was made fearless.

text.NRSV: It surveys everything that is lofty; it is king over all that are proud.”
text.Rotherham: Every thing lofty, he beholdeth, he, is king over all ravenous beasts.
text.KJV: He beholdeth all high things: he is a king over all the children of pride.
text.NABRE: He looks over all who are haughty, he is king over all proud beasts.

text.NRSV: Then Job answered the Lord:
text.Rotherham: Then Job responded to Yahweh, and said:—
text.KJV: Then Job answered the Lord, and said,
text.NABRE: Then Job answered the Lord and said:
text.DRB: And Job answering our Lord, said:

text.NRSV: “I know that you can do all things, and that no purpose of yours can be thwarted.
text.Rotherham: I know that, all things, thou canst do, and that no purpose can be withholden from thee.
text.KJV: I know that thou canst do every thing, and that no thought can be withholden from thee.
text.NABRE: I know that you can do all things, and that no purpose of yours can be hindered.
text.DRB: I know that thou canst do all things, and no cogitation is hid from thee.

text.NRSV: ‘Who is this that hides counsel without knowledge?’ Therefore I have uttered what I did not understand, things too wonderful for me, which I did not know.
text.Rotherham: Who is it that hideth counsel without knowledge? Therefore, have I declared, but not understood, things too wonderful for me, which I could not know.
text.KJV: Who is he that hideth counsel without knowledge? therefore have I uttered that I understood not; things too wonderful for me, which I knew not.
text.NABRE: “Who is this who obscures counsel with ignorance?” I have spoken but did not understand; things too marvelous for me, which I did not know.
text.DRB: Who is this, that concealeth counsel without knowledge? Therefore have I spoken unwisely, and the things that did exceed my knowledge beyond measure.

text.NRSV: ‘Hear, and I will speak; I will question you, and you declare to me.’
text.Rotherham: Hear thou, I pray thee, and, I, will speak, I will ask thee, and inform thou me.
text.KJV: Hear, I beseech thee, and I will speak: I will demand of thee, and declare thou unto me.
text.NABRE: “Listen, and I will speak; I will question you, and you tell me the answers.”
text.DRB: Hear (I beseech thee) and I will speak: I will ask thee, and do thou tell me.

text.NRSV: I had heard of you by the hearing of the ear, but now my eye sees you;
text.Rotherham: By the hearing of the ear, had I heard thee, but, now, mine own eye, hath seen thee.
text.KJV: I have heard of thee by the hearing of the ear: but now mine eye seeth thee.
text.NABRE: By hearsay I had heard of you, but now my eye has seen you.
text.DRB: With the hearing of the ear I have heard thee, but now my eye seeth thee.

text.NRSV: therefore I despise myself, and repent in dust and ashes.”
text.Rotherham: For this cause, I tremble and repent, on dust and ashes.
text.KJV: Wherefore I abhor myself, and repent in dust and ashes.
text.NABRE: Therefore I disown what I have said, and repent in dust and ashes.
text.DRB: Therefore I reprehend myself, and I do penance in imbers and ashes.

text.NRSV: After the Lord had spoken these words to Job, the Lord said to Eliphaz the Temanite: “My wrath is kindled against you and against your two friends; for you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Job has.
text.Rotherham: And it came to pass, after Yahweh had spoken these words unto Job, that Yahweh, said unto Eliphaz the Temanite, Kindled is mine anger against thee and against thy two friends, for ye have not spoken concerning me the thing that is right, like my servant Job.
text.KJV: And it was so, that after the Lord had spoken these words unto Job, the Lord said to Eliphaz the Temanite, My wrath is kindled against thee, and against thy two friends: for ye have not spoken of me the thing that is right, as my servant Job hath.
text.NABRE: IX. Epilogue Job’s Restoration. And after the Lord had spoken these words to Job, the Lord said to Eliphaz the Temanite, “My anger blazes against you and your two friends! You have not spoken rightly concerning me, as has my servant Job.
text.DRB: And after our Lord spake these words to Job, he said to Eliphaz the Themanite: My fury is wrath against thee, and against thy two friends, because you have not spoken right before me, as my servant Job.

text.NRSV: Now therefore take seven bulls and seven rams, and go to my servant Job, and offer up for yourselves a burnt offering; and my servant Job shall pray for you, for I will accept his prayer not to deal with you according to your folly; for you have not spoken of me what is right, as my servant Job has.”
text.Rotherham: Now, therefore, take unto you seven bullocks and seven rams, and go unto my servant Job, and ye shall offer up an ascending-sacrifice in your own behalf, and, Job my servant, shall pray over you,—for, him, will I accept, that I may not deal out to you disgrace, because ye have not spoken concerning me the thing that is right, like my servant Job.
text.KJV: Therefore take unto you now seven bullocks and seven rams, and go to my servant Job, and offer up for yourselves a burnt offering; and my servant Job shall pray for you: for him will I accept: lest I deal with you after your folly, in that ye have not spoken of me the thing which is right, like my servant Job.
text.NABRE: So now take seven bulls and seven rams, and go to my servant Job, and sacrifice a burnt offering for yourselves, and let my servant Job pray for you. To him I will show favor, and not punish your folly, for you have not spoken rightly concerning me, as has my servant Job.”
text.DRB: Take therefore unto you seven oxen, and seven rams, & go to my servant Job, and offer holocaust for yourselves: and my servant Job shall pray for you: his face I will receive, that the folly be not imputed to you: for you have not spoken right things before me, as my servant Job.

text.NRSV: So Eliphaz the Temanite and Bildad the Shuhite and Zophar the Naamathite went and did what the Lord had told them; and the Lord accepted Job’s prayer.
text.Rotherham: So Eliphaz the Temanite and Bildad the Shuhite [and] Zophar the Naamathite went, and did according to that which Yahweh had spoken unto them,—and Yahweh accepted Job.
text.KJV: So Eliphaz the Temanite and Bildad the Shuhite and Zophar the Naamathite went, and did according as the Lord commanded them: the Lord also accepted Job.
text.NABRE: Then Eliphaz the Temanite, and Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite, went and did as the Lord had commanded them. The Lord showed favor to Job.
text.DRB: Eliphaz therefore the Themanite, and Baldad the Suhite, and Sophar the Naamathite went, and did as our Lord had spoken to them, and our Lord received the face of Job.

text.NRSV: And the Lord restored the fortunes of Job when he had prayed for his friends; and the Lord gave Job twice as much as he had before.
text.Rotherham: And, Yahweh himself, turned the captivity of Job, when he prayed in behalf of his friends,—and Yahweh increased all that Job had possessed, unto twice as much.
text.KJV: And the Lord turned the captivity of Job, when he prayed for his friends: also the Lord gave Job twice as much as he had before.
text.NABRE: The Lord also restored the prosperity of Job, after he had prayed for his friends; the Lord even gave to Job twice as much as he had before.
text.DRB: Our Lord also was turned at the penance of Job, when he prayed for his friends. And our Lord added all things whatsoever had been Job's, double.

text.NRSV: Then there came to him all his brothers and sisters and all who had known him before, and they ate bread with him in his house; they showed him sympathy and comforted him for all the evil that the Lord had brought upon him; and each of them gave him a piece of money and a gold ring.
text.Rotherham: Then came unto him all his brethren and all his sisters, and all his former acquaintances, and they did eat bread with him in his house,—and shewed sympathy with him and comforted him, over all the calamity which Yahweh had brought upon him,—and they gave him, every one a weight of money, and every one, a ring of gold.
text.KJV: Then came there unto him all his brethren, and all his sisters, and all they that had been of his acquaintance before, and did eat bread with him in his house: and they bemoaned him, and comforted him over all the evil that the Lord had brought upon him: every man also gave him a piece of money, and every one an earring of gold.
text.NABRE: Then all his brothers and sisters came to him, and all his former acquaintances, and they dined with him in his house. They consoled and comforted him for all the evil the Lord had brought upon him, and each one gave him a piece of money and a gold ring.
text.DRB: And all his brethren came to him, and all his sisters, and all that knew him before, and they did eat bread with him in his house: and wagged the head upon him, & comforted him upon all the evil that God had brought in upon him. And every man gave him one ewe, and one earlet of gold.

text.NRSV: The Lord blessed the latter days of Job more than his beginning; and he had fourteen thousand sheep, six thousand camels, one thousand yoke of oxen, and one thousand donkeys.
text.Rotherham: And, Yahweh, blessed the latter end of Job, more than his beginning,—and so he came to have fourteen thousand sheep, and six thousand camels, and a thousand yoke of oxen, and a thousand she-asses.
text.KJV: So the Lord blessed the latter end of Job more than his beginning: for he had fourteen thousand sheep, and six thousand camels, and a thousand yoke of oxen, and a thousand she asses.
text.NABRE: Thus the Lord blessed the later days of Job more than his earlier ones. Now he had fourteen thousand sheep, six thousand camels, a thousand yoke of oxen, and a thousand she-donkeys.
text.DRB: And our Lord blessed the last days of Job more than his beginning. And he had fourteen thousand sheep, and six thousand camels, and a thousand yoke of oxen, and a thousand she asses.

text.NRSV: He also had seven sons and three daughters.
text.Rotherham: And he came to have seven sons, and three daughters;
text.KJV: He had also seven sons and three daughters.
text.NABRE: He also had seven sons and three daughters:
text.DRB: And he had seven sons, and three daughters.

text.NRSV: He named the first Jemimah, the second Keziah, and the third Keren-happuch.
text.Rotherham: and he called the name of the first Jemima, and the name of the second Kezia,—and the name of the third, Keren-happuch.
text.KJV: And he called the name of the first, Jemima; and the name of the second, Kezia; and the name of the third, Keren–happuch.
text.NABRE: the first daughter he called Jemimah, the second Keziah, and the third Keren-happuch.
text.DRB: And he called the name of one Dies, and the name of the second Cassia, and the name of the third Cornustibii.

text.NRSV: In all the land there were no women so beautiful as Job’s daughters; and their father gave them an inheritance along with their brothers.
text.Rotherham: And there were found no women so fair as the daughters of Job, in all the land,—and their father gave them an inheritance, in the midst of their brethren.
text.KJV: And in all the land were no women found so fair as the daughters of Job: and their father gave them inheritance among their brethren.
text.NABRE: In all the land no other women were as beautiful as the daughters of Job; and their father gave them an inheritance among their brothers.
text.DRB: And there were not found in all the earth women so beautiful as the daughters of Job: and their father gave them inheritance among their brethren.

text.NRSV: After this Job lived one hundred forty years, and saw his children, and his children’s children, four generations.
text.Rotherham: And Job lived, after this, a hundred and forty years,—and saw his sons and his sons’ sons, four generations.
text.KJV: After this lived Job an hundred and forty years, and saw his sons, and his sons’ sons, even four generations.
text.NABRE: After this, Job lived a hundred and forty years; and he saw his children, his grandchildren, and even his great-grandchildren.
text.DRB: And Job lived after these things, an hundred forty years, and he saw his children, and his children's children, unto the fourth generation, and he died an old man, and full of days.

text.NRSV: And Job died, old and full of days.
text.Rotherham: So Job died, old and satisfied with days.
text.KJV: So Job died, being old and full of days.
text.NABRE: Then Job died, old and full of years.

41.27: He regards iron as chaff, and bronze as rotten wood.
41.29: Clubs he regards as straw; he laughs at the crash of the spear.
41.30: Under him are sharp pottery fragments, spreading a threshing sledge upon the mire.
41.31: He makes the depths boil like a pot; he makes the sea like a perfume bottle.
41.32: Behind him he leaves a shining path; you would think the deep had white hair.
41.34: He looks over all who are haughty, he is king over all proud beasts.
